name: Semaglutide
short_description: Semaglutide is a long-acting, once-weekly GLP-1 receptor agonist with 94% sequence homology to human GLP-1. It is FDA-approved in multiple formulations (Ozempic, Wegovy, Rybelsus, and the higher-dose Wegovy HD) for type 2 diabetes management and, at the 2.4 mg subcutaneous dose, for chronic weight management in adults and adolescents 12 and older. The SELECT trial (2023, NEJM; n=17,604) demonstrated a 20% relative reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events in people with overweight/obesity but without diabetes, marking a milestone for obesity pharmacotherapy beyond glycemic control.
mechanism: Semaglutide is a synthetic analog of human glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) modified with a C18 fatty diacid chain that facilitates albumin binding, extending the plasma half-life to approximately 7 days and enabling once-weekly dosing. It acts as a full agonist at the GLP-1 receptor (a class B G-protein-coupled receptor), stimulating adenylate cyclase via Gs-coupled cAMP/PKA signaling. Downstream effects include: (1) glucose-dependent enhancement of insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells; (2) suppression of inappropriate glucagon release; (3) slowing of gastric emptying, which blunts postprandial glucose excursions and reduces caloric absorption; (4) central nervous system actions at hypothalamic and brainstem GLP-1 receptors that reduce appetite, increase satiety, and reduce food intake. Structural modifications relative to native GLP-1 confer resistance to enzymatic degradation by dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4). The combined effect is improved glycemic control, substantial body weight reduction, and demonstrated cardiorenal benefits beyond glucose lowering.
monitoring_notes: Blood glucose and HbA1c — monitor at baseline and periodically, especially with other glucose-lowering agents; adjust insulin/sulfonylurea to prevent hypoglycemia
Renal function (eGFR, serum creatinine) — baseline plus recheck with persistent GI side effects or dehydration
Thyroid symptoms — educate patients to report neck mass, hoarseness, dysphagia, or dyspnea; refer to endocrinology if a nodule or calcitonin elevation is found
Gallbladder/pancreatic symptoms — monitor for RUQ pain, jaundice, or severe epigastric pain radiating to the back; order ultrasound or lipase as indicated
Ophthalmologic evaluation — baseline eye exam in diabetes; monitor for worsening retinopathy in the first year of treatment
Weight and body composition — track body weight and, where feasible, lean mass (DEXA/BIA) given potential lean-mass loss
Nutritional status — monitor protein intake and adequacy given appetite suppression, especially in those at risk for sarcopenia
Vital signs and heart rate — semaglutide is associated with modest heart-rate increases; monitor in patients with baseline arrhythmia
Pregnancy status — confirm negative test before initiation in those of reproductive potential; advise contraception; discontinue >=2 months before planned conception
Hypersensitivity — advise seeking emergency care for angioedema, breathing difficulty, or anaphylaxis
absolute_contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) — FDA boxed warning
Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2) — FDA boxed warning
Known hypersensitivity to semaglutide or any formulation excipient (risk of anaphylaxis or angioedema)
Concomitant use of another GLP-1 receptor agonist (e.g., liraglutide, dulaglutide, exenatide) or a dual GLP-1/GIP agonist (tirzepatide)
relative_contraindications: Type 1 diabetes mellitus — not an approved indication; insulin deficiency still requires insulin therapy
History of acute or chronic pancreatitis — GLP-1 RAs have been associated with pancreatitis; evaluate risk/benefit and use with caution
Severe gastrointestinal disease (e.g., gastroparesis) — gastric-emptying delay may worsen symptoms
Pregnancy — animal data show fetal harm; FDA labeling recommends discontinuing at least 2 months before a planned pregnancy given the ~7-day half-life
Breastfeeding — insufficient human safety data
Prior bariatric surgery — increased risk of GI complications; monitor closely
Significant history of diabetic retinopathy — SUSTAIN-6 showed increased retinopathy complications with rapid glucose normalization; ophthalmologic monitoring recommended
Severe renal impairment (eGFR <15 or dialysis-dependent) — limited evidence; monitor for volume-depletion-related AKI
History of active or recent suicidal ideation or behavior — large meta-analyses found no causal link, but individual clinical judgment is warranted
Concurrent use of oral medications with a narrow therapeutic index — gastric-emptying delay may alter absorption timing
common_side_effects: Nausea (most common — up to ~44% at the 2.4 mg Wegovy dose; typically most prominent during escalation and improves over time)
Vomiting
Diarrhea
Constipation
Abdominal pain / discomfort
Decreased appetite / reduced food intake (intended pharmacologic effect but can impair nutritional adequacy)
Dysgeusia (altered taste)
Headache
Fatigue
Dysesthesia (notably higher at 7.2 mg Wegovy HD vs 2.4 mg)
Injection-site reactions (erythema, bruising, pruritus)
serious_adverse_events: Thyroid C-cell tumors (including medullary thyroid carcinoma) — observed in rodent studies; human relevance unknown but carries an FDA BOXED WARNING
Acute pancreatitis — monitor for persistent severe abdominal pain radiating to the back
Gallbladder disease — cholelithiasis and cholecystitis reported, likely related in part to rapid weight loss
Acute kidney injury — secondary to dehydration from nausea/vomiting/diarrhea
Diabetic retinopathy complications — increased risk during rapid glucose normalization (SUSTAIN-6)
Hypoglycemia — significant risk when combined with insulin or sulfonylureas; consider reducing the concomitant agent
Severe hypersensitivity reactions — anaphylaxis and angioedema reported; discontinue immediately and treat
Ileus / severe GI events — reported in postmarketing settings; rare but serious

dose_references.context: Ozempic (subcutaneous injection) — Type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular risk reduction. FDA-approved dose escalation: 0.25 mg SC once weekly for 4 weeks (initiation/tolerability), then 0.5 mg once weekly; may increase to 1 mg once weekly after at least 4 weeks; may further increase to 2 mg once weekly after at least 4 weeks if additional glycemic control needed. Maximum approved dose 2 mg/week.

dose_references.notes: The 0.25 mg starting dose is for tolerability only and not therapeutically effective. Low/high values reflect the maintenance therapeutic range per FDA label. Approved January 2017 for T2D; CV indication expanded January 2020; CKD indication January 2025. FDA-approved labeled dosing — not a recommendation by this app.

dose_references.context: Wegovy (subcutaneous injection) — Chronic weight management in adults (BMI >=30, or >=27 with at least one weight-related comorbidity) and adolescents >=12. Escalation: 0.25 mg/wk (wks 1-4), 0.5 mg/wk (wks 5-8), 1.0 mg/wk (wks 9-12), 1.7 mg/wk (wks 13-16), then 2.4 mg/wk maintenance. Also approved for cardiovascular risk reduction in adults with established CVD (March 2024).

dose_references.notes: STEP 1 (n=1961) demonstrated mean body weight reduction of ~14.9% vs ~2.4% placebo over 68 weeks at the 2.4 mg dose. FDA approved June 2021 for adults; December 2022 extended to adolescents >=12. FDA-approved labeled dosing — not a recommendation by this app.

dose_references.context: Wegovy HD (subcutaneous injection, 7.2 mg) — Higher-dose formulation FDA-approved March 19, 2026 under accelerated/priority review. Indicated for additional weight reduction in adults with obesity in whom further weight reduction is clinically indicated. In the 72-week STEP UP trial (n~1,400), produced mean weight loss of ~20.7% vs placebo, with about one in three participants achieving >=25% weight loss.

FDA approves high-dose Wegovy 7.2 mg for adults with obesity

dose_references.notes: Corrected: STEP UP headline weight loss is ~20.7% (not 18.7% as originally stated). Dysesthesia was higher at 7.2 mg vs 2.4 mg and placebo. Tolerability otherwise consistent with the established semaglutide profile. FDA-approved labeled dosing — not a recommendation by this app.

dose_references.context: Rybelsus (oral tablet) — Type 2 diabetes. FDA-approved dose escalation: 3 mg orally once daily for 30 days (tolerability only, not therapeutic), then 7 mg once daily; may increase to 14 mg once daily after at least 30 days if additional glycemic control needed. Must be taken on empty stomach >=30 minutes before first food/drink of the day, with no more than 120 mL water.

citation.title: Semaglutide — StatPearls (NCBI Bookshelf)
Efficacy of Oral Semaglutide: Overview of the PIONEER Clinical Trial Program — AJMC

dose_references.notes: Oral bioavailability is low (~1%) but adequate at these doses due to the SNAC absorption enhancer. PIONEER phase 3a program enrolled >9,500 patients. FDA approved September 2019. FDA-approved labeled dosing — not a recommendation by this app.

synergies.rationale: Complementary mechanisms: metformin reduces hepatic glucose production and improves insulin sensitivity via AMPK activation, while semaglutide enhances glucose-dependent insulin secretion and reduces appetite. The SUSTAIN 2 trial tested semaglutide added to metformin (with or without thiazolidinediones) and showed additive HbA1c reduction. Metformin was common background therapy across the SUSTAIN and PIONEER programs. Educational context only.
synergies.caution_notes: Combination is not associated with increased hypoglycemia risk (metformin alone has low hypoglycemia risk), but both agents may contribute to GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea). Clinician oversight required for any dose adjustment. Not a prescribed combination — educational framing only. Combination observed as background therapy in trials rather than the direct randomized intervention; graded HUMAN_OBS pending a direct citation.

synergies.rationale: Mechanistically complementary: SGLT2 inhibitors lower glucose via urinary glucose excretion and provide cardiorenal protection through pathways distinct from GLP-1 signaling. Prespecified analyses of the SOUL (oral semaglutide) and FLOW (subcutaneous semaglutide) trials examined outcomes stratified by baseline SGLT2i use, with cardiovascular and renal benefits of semaglutide appearing additive to and tolerable with concomitant SGLT2i use. Educational context only.
synergies.caution_notes: Combination may increase risk of dehydration, volume depletion, and urinary tract infections. Euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis risk from SGLT2i remains a class consideration. Not a prescribed combination — educational framing only. Combination observed as background therapy in trials rather than the direct randomized intervention; graded HUMAN_OBS pending a direct citation.

synergies.rationale: GLP-1 RA-induced weight loss includes a component of lean (fat-free) mass loss, particularly with rapid weight reduction. Resistance training stimulates muscle protein synthesis through pathways mechanistically independent of GLP-1 receptor activity, providing a plausible counterbalance. The SEMALEAN observational cohort (n=115) found that despite an initial lean-mass decline, handgrip strength improved and sarcopenic-obesity prevalence fell over 12 months on semaglutide 2.4 mg; this was a single-arm cohort and did not test a dedicated exercise-intervention arm, so it is hypothesis-generating regarding the added benefit of structured resistance training. Educational context only.
synergies.caution_notes: Exercise programming should be individualized to functional capacity, comorbidities, and musculoskeletal health. Patients with deconditioning, orthopedic limitations, or autonomic neuropathy require supervised progression. Not a prescribed protocol — educational framing only.

synergies.rationale: Higher protein intake provides substrate for muscle protein synthesis and may attenuate the obligatory lean-mass loss accompanying a caloric deficit. Pairing semaglutide-induced energy restriction with adequate protein is mechanistically reasonable, and obesity-medicine and nutrition guidance generally support emphasizing protein during pharmacologic weight loss. Direct RCT confirmation of a protein-by-semaglutide interaction on lean mass is limited, so this remains mechanistic. Educational context only.
synergies.caution_notes: Protein targets require caution in pre-existing chronic kidney disease (CKD stages 3b-5) and should be individualized by a registered dietitian or clinician. Not a prescribed protocol — educational framing only.

synergies.with_name: Tirzepatide (contraindicated combination — flagged, not a synergy)
synergies.rationale: Included strictly as an educational contraindication contrast, NOT as a beneficial combination. Tirzepatide is a dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist; co-administration of two GLP-1 receptor agonists is contraindicated per FDA prescribing information for both agents because of overlapping mechanism and additive adverse-effect risk. The compounds should NOT be combined.
synergies.caution_notes: DO NOT COMBINE. Co-administration of semaglutide and tirzepatide is contraindicated per FDA labeling for both agents. This row exists only to surface the contraindication; a clinician must select a single agent.

name: Tirzepatide
short_description: Tirzepatide is a synthetic 39-amino acid peptide that acts as a dual agonist at both the GIP (glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide) and GLP-1 (glucagon-like peptide-1) receptors. FDA-approved as Mounjaro for type 2 diabetes (May 2022) and as Zepbound for chronic weight management (November 2023) and moderate-to-severe obstructive sleep apnea in adults with obesity (December 2024). It produces superior weight loss compared to selective GLP-1 agonists in head-to-head trials, with a mean body weight reduction of up to 22.5% over 72 weeks in the SURMOUNT-1 trial.
mechanism: Tirzepatide is a 39-amino acid peptide incorporating a C20 unsaturated di-acid acyl chain that enables albumin binding, yielding an approximately 5-day (120-hour) half-life and supporting once-weekly dosing. It shows equi-affinity for the GIP receptor compared to native GIP but approximately 5-fold weaker affinity for the GLP-1 receptor than native GLP-1 — an intentional imbalance. At the GLP-1 receptor, it exhibits biased agonism: robust cAMP signaling with less than 10% maximal efficacy for beta-arrestin recruitment, reducing GLP-1R internalization and potentially sustaining receptor responsiveness. Combined GIP and GLP-1 receptor activation produces: (1) glucose-dependent insulin secretion from pancreatic beta-cells; (2) glucagon suppression in the fasted state; (3) slowed gastric emptying and reduced appetite via central satiety pathways; (4) direct adipose tissue effects improving lipid metabolism; and (5) reductions in hepatic fat and visceral adiposity. The dual receptor engagement produces greater weight loss and glycemic improvement than selective GLP-1 agonists in clinical trials. Sources: JCI Insight 2020 (PMC7526454), Frontiers Endocrinology 2024.
monitoring_notes: Baseline and periodic HbA1c in patients with T2D to assess glycemic response
Renal function (serum creatinine, eGFR) at baseline and when significant GI adverse effects (vomiting, diarrhea) cause dehydration risk, particularly in patients with pre-existing renal impairment
Blood pressure monitoring — tirzepatide reduces systolic BP by a mean of ~7–10 mmHg in T2D trials; dose adjustment of antihypertensive agents may be needed
Lipid panel — tirzepatide improves LDL-C, triglycerides, and HDL-C in T2D populations; medication adjustments may follow

Thyroid symptom surveillance: monitor for neck mass, dysphagia, dyspnea, or hoarseness (signs of medullary thyroid carcinoma). Routine serum calcitonin or thyroid ultrasound not recommended by FDA label as benefit is uncertain; if serum calcitonin is measured and exceeds 50 ng/L, refer for further evaluation
Ophthalmology referral for patients with T2D and pre-existing diabetic retinopathy before initiating tirzepatide due to risk of early worsening with rapid glycemic improvement
Liver function tests (LFTs) may be considered in patients with pre-existing hepatic disease or MASH given off-label interest

For patients using oral contraceptives: per FDA label, delayed gastric emptying may reduce efficacy; backup barrier contraception or a non-oral method for 4 weeks after initiation and after each dose increase may be advised by the prescriber
For patients on levothyroxine: monitor thyroid function after significant weight loss (>10% body weight) as levothyroxine dose requirements may decrease
Body composition assessment (DXA or clinical proxy) is clinically useful to distinguish fat vs. lean mass changes during therapy, especially in patients with sarcopenia risk
Gallbladder evaluation if symptoms of biliary colic develop (right upper quadrant pain, nausea with fatty foods); RCT meta-analysis shows RR 1.52–1.67 for gallbladder/biliary disease vs. placebo
Hypoglycemia monitoring with glucometer in all T2D patients, especially those on concomitant insulin or sulfonylureas; the prescriber may reduce the dose of these agents at tirzepatide initiation
absolute_contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) — boxed warning on FDA label
Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2) — boxed warning on FDA label
Known serious hypersensitivity reaction to tirzepatide or any component of the formulation (e.g., anaphylaxis, angioedema)
relative_contraindications: History of acute pancreatitis or chronic pancreatitis — GLP-1 class warning; evaluate on a case-by-case basis with clinician
Gastroparesis or other severe gastrointestinal motility disorders — tirzepatide further slows gastric emptying
Proliferative diabetic retinopathy or diabetic macular edema requiring acute therapy — rapid glycemic improvement may transiently worsen retinal disease
Pregnancy or planned pregnancy within 1 month — discontinue at least 1 month before planned conception due to long half-life; animal studies show fetal malformations; limited human safety data
Breastfeeding — unknown if tirzepatide is excreted in human milk; risk-benefit must be assessed
History of gallbladder disease or active cholelithiasis — RCT meta-analysis shows 1.52–1.67-fold increased risk of gallbladder/biliary events
Significant renal impairment — increased risk of AKI from dehydration secondary to GI adverse effects; monitor renal function
Concurrent use of insulin or sulfonylureas — substantially increases hypoglycemia risk; dose reduction of those agents may be required under clinician supervision
common_side_effects: Nausea (most common, dose-dependent, typically peaks during titration)
Diarrhea
Vomiting
Constipation
Decreased appetite
Abdominal pain / dyspepsia
Injection-site reactions (pain, erythema, hemorrhage, bruising)
Sinus tachycardia / increased resting heart rate
Fatigue
Belching / flatulence
Alopecia (hair thinning reported in weight-loss setting, likely secondary to rapid weight loss)
serious_adverse_events: Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC): dose- and duration-dependent thyroid C-cell tumors in rodents; human risk unknown but boxed warning issued. Discontinue if MTC suspected
Acute pancreatitis (including hemorrhagic and necrotizing, some fatal): GLP-1 class warning. Discontinue immediately if pancreatitis confirmed; do not restart
Hypoglycemia: severe episodes reported, particularly with concomitant insulin or sulfonylureas
Acute kidney injury: secondary to dehydration from severe GI adverse effects, especially in patients with baseline renal impairment
Serious hypersensitivity reactions: anaphylaxis, angioedema — discontinue immediately
Diabetic retinopathy complications: rapid glycemic improvement can precipitate early worsening in patients with pre-existing diabetic retinopathy
Gallbladder disease: cholecystitis and cholelithiasis reported; RR ~1.52–1.67 vs. placebo in meta-analysis of 12 RCTs (n=12,351); may require cholecystectomy

Oral drug absorption changes: delayed gastric emptying may alter Cmax and AUC of orally administered drugs with narrow therapeutic indices (e.g., levothyroxine, oral contraceptives, warfarin) — monitor accordingly

citation.title: Tirzepatide Once Weekly for the Treatment of Obesity (SURMOUNT-1), NEJM 2022

dose_references.notes: n=2,539 randomized 1:1:1:1 to tirzepatide 5/10/15 mg or placebo. Mean weight loss: 16.0% (5 mg), 21.4% (10 mg), 22.5% (15 mg) vs 2.4% placebo at 72 weeks. 89–96% of tirzepatide participants achieved ≥5% weight loss vs 28% on placebo. Starting dose for titration was 2.5 mg, escalated per protocol. Reported for education, not as a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.notes: Five global phase 3 SURPASS trials produced HbA1c reductions of −1.9 to −2.6% and body weight reductions of −6.6 to −13.9 kg across 5/10/15 mg doses over 40–52 weeks in T2D populations. Educational reference only.

citation.title: Cardiovascular Outcomes with Tirzepatide versus Dulaglutide in Type 2 Diabetes (SURPASS-CVOT), NEJM 2025

dose_references.notes: n=13,165 (6,586 tirzepatide, 6,579 dulaglutide). Primary MACE endpoint occurred in 12.2% (tirzepatide) vs 13.1% (dulaglutide); noninferiority met, superiority NOT met on MACE (HR 0.92, 95.3% CI 0.83–1.01). Approximately 8% relative reduction in 3-point MACE and 16% reduction in all-cause mortality vs dulaglutide. Larger reductions in BP, lipids, and weight vs comparator. Educational reference only.

dose_references.context: SYNERGY-NASH phase 2 trial: metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) with liver fibrosis
dose_references.frequency: Once weekly for 52 weeks; dose-finding design

citation.title: Tirzepatide for Metabolic Dysfunction–Associated Steatohepatitis with Liver Fibrosis, NEJM 2024

dose_references.notes: Phase 2, multicenter, dose-finding, double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT (130 sites, 10 countries). Tirzepatide was more effective than placebo for MASH resolution without worsening of fibrosis. Larger and longer phase 3 studies needed to confirm efficacy and safety for this indication. NOT an approved indication as of 2026 — off-label. Educational reference only.

synergies.rationale: Tirzepatide produces caloric-deficit-driven weight loss in which a meaningful fraction of lost weight may come from lean mass (SURMOUNT-1 DXA substudy and a 2025 systematic review of skeletal muscle effects, PMC12394919). Resistance exercise and adequate protein intake stimulate muscle protein synthesis via mTOR pathways, mechanistically complementing tirzepatide's fat-preferential weight loss to help preserve lean mass during therapy. A prospective study (n=200) reported relatively modest muscle loss when resistance training and protein education were incorporated. This is a behavioral/nutritional combination with sound mechanistic rationale, not a drug-drug combination, and is described for education only.
synergies.caution_notes: Educational context only — not a prescribed protocol. Structured exercise programming should account for any musculoskeletal limitations or cardiovascular risk factors. Protein targets should be individualized with clinician or registered dietitian guidance.

synergies.rationale: A 12-week non-randomized, open-label prospective pilot study (n=60; 30 per arm) reported that tirzepatide combined with low-energy ketogenic therapy (LEKT) was associated with greater preservation of fat-free mass (−0.50% vs −4.29% with low-calorie diet) and muscle strength (−0.3% vs −4.1%) and attenuated resting metabolic rate decline. Ketosis-induced metabolic adaptations — including preferential fat oxidation and protein-sparing effects — may complement tirzepatide's appetite suppression. The authors themselves describe these as preliminary, hypothesis-generating data.
synergies.caution_notes: Preliminary, non-randomized, open-label pilot evidence only (small n, 12-week duration) — not an RCT and not a recommendation. Ketogenic diets require medical supervision, particularly in patients with T2D on insulin or sulfonylureas due to compounded hypoglycemia risk. Electrolyte monitoring is advised.

synergies.rationale: Metformin is a first-line oral antidiabetic agent that improves insulin sensitivity via AMPK activation and reduces hepatic glucose output, a mechanism complementary to tirzepatide's incretin-mediated glucose-dependent insulin secretion. Tirzepatide was studied in T2D populations frequently already on background metformin (SURPASS program), so combined use has been observed in trial settings and is consistent with standard-of-care T2D management per ADA guidance. Hypoglycemia risk is minimal when used without sulfonylureas or insulin.
synergies.caution_notes: Background-therapy observation, not a head-to-head combination RCT. Metformin dose adjustment is generally not required when adding tirzepatide, but ongoing monitoring of renal function (eGFR) is warranted. Educational context only — any medication change requires clinician oversight.

synergies.rationale: Tirzepatide already acts as a GLP-1R agonist; combining with a selective GLP-1 receptor agonist would engage the same receptor and is not supported by any clinical evidence for co-administration. Listed explicitly as a safety anti-pattern: the combination is NOT pharmacologically rational and would be expected to add GI toxicity without established additive benefit.
synergies.caution_notes: This is a safety flag, NOT a synergy endorsement. Combination with another GLP-1 receptor agonist is not recommended. No clinical trial supports dual GLP-1R agonism with tirzepatide plus a GLP-1RA. Risk of additive GI toxicity, pancreatitis, and thyroid C-cell stimulation.

name: Liraglutide
short_description: Liraglutide is a long-acting GLP-1 receptor agonist (97% homology to endogenous GLP-1) FDA-approved for type 2 diabetes glycemic control (Victoza, 2010) and chronic weight management (Saxenda, 2014). It stimulates glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppresses glucagon, delays gastric emptying, and reduces appetite via central hypothalamic pathways. It carries a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors and is contraindicated in personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2.
mechanism: Liraglutide binds and activates GLP-1 receptors in pancreatic beta cells, raising intracellular cAMP and triggering glucose-dependent insulin release while suppressing glucagon. Its palmitic acid side chain confers resistance to DPP-4 and neutral endopeptidase degradation, extending plasma half-life to ~13 hours and enabling once-daily dosing. Beyond the pancreas, GLP-1 receptors in the hypothalamus (arcuate nucleus), nucleus tractus solitarius, ventral tegmental area, stomach, and heart mediate appetite suppression, delayed gastric emptying, and modest chronotropic effects. The net result is improved postprandial glucose homeostasis, reduced caloric intake, and weight loss.
monitoring_notes: HbA1c and fasting plasma glucose: every ~3 months when uncontrolled; every ~6 months when stable (per ADA guidelines).
Heart rate: monitor at regular intervals — liraglutide increases resting heart rate by a mean of ~2–9 bpm; consider discontinuation if persistent, unexplained tachycardia develops.
Renal function (serum creatinine, eGFR): assess before initiating and monitor when GI adverse reactions (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) cause volume depletion.
Thyroid: counsel patients to report neck masses, dysphagia, dyspnea, or persistent hoarseness. Routine serum calcitonin monitoring is of uncertain diagnostic value per FDA label.
Lipase/amylase: consider monitoring if abdominal symptoms arise; acute pancreatitis has been reported — discontinue if suspected.
Gallbladder: evaluate with ultrasound if symptoms of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis develop (2.2% vs. 0.8% placebo in trials).
Weight-loss response: reassess at 16 weeks in adults (target ≥4% body weight loss) and 12 weeks in pediatric patients (target ≥1% BMI reduction) per Saxenda label; discontinue if threshold not met.
Mental health / suicidal ideation: monitor for new or worsening depression, mood changes, or suicidal thoughts (FDA Warnings and Precautions, 2017).

Oral medication absorption: liraglutide delays gastric emptying and may reduce peak concentrations of time-sensitive oral drugs (e.g., oral contraceptives, digoxin, warfarin, lisinopril, atorvastatin). Review concomitant oral medications at initiation.
Injection site: rotate sites (abdomen, thigh, upper arm) to reduce local reactions.
absolute_contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) — FDA boxed warning; rodent studies show C-cell tumors at clinically relevant exposures; human risk unknown but class-level precaution applies.
Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN2) — genetic predisposition to MTC makes GLP-1 receptor agonist use contraindicated.
Serious hypersensitivity (anaphylaxis, angioedema) to liraglutide or any excipient in the formulation.
Do not use Victoza and Saxenda concurrently — same active ingredient at different doses; co-use constitutes overdose.
relative_contraindications: History of acute or chronic pancreatitis — GLP-1 receptor agonists have been associated with acute pancreatitis; weigh risk vs. benefit and counsel patients on warning signs.
History of gallbladder disease or cholelithiasis — incidence of cholelithiasis (2.2%) and cholecystitis (0.8%) is higher than placebo in trials.
Concurrent use of insulin secretagogues (sulfonylureas) or insulin — markedly increases hypoglycemia risk; dose reduction of the secretagogue or insulin is typically required.
Severe gastrointestinal disease (e.g., gastroparesis) — liraglutide further delays gastric emptying and may worsen symptoms; generally not recommended.
Severe renal impairment (eGFR <15 mL/min) or end-stage renal disease — limited clinical data; volume depletion from GI side effects can precipitate acute kidney injury.
Pregnancy — not recommended; animal data show fetal harm at clinically relevant doses. Discontinue at least 2 months before planned pregnancy (Saxenda label).
Concomitant use with other GLP-1 receptor agonists — additive adverse effects without additional benefit.
Active or recent suicidal ideation — monitor closely given the association with suicidal behavior reported in trials (0.3% adults, 0.8% pediatric).
Patients with resting tachycardia or known cardiac arrhythmia — liraglutide increases resting heart rate; clinical significance with baseline tachycardia is uncertain.
common_side_effects: Nausea (most common, particularly during dose escalation; typically attenuates over weeks)
Diarrhea
Vomiting
Constipation
Decreased appetite
Dyspepsia / abdominal pain
Injection site reactions (redness, bruising, pain)
Headache
Fatigue / asthenia
Dizziness
Increased resting heart rate (mean +2–9 bpm)
serious_adverse_events: Thyroid C-cell tumors / medullary thyroid carcinoma — demonstrated in rodents at clinically relevant exposures; human risk not established but contraindicated in MTC/MEN2 (FDA boxed warning).
Acute pancreatitis — including fatal and non-fatal hemorrhagic or necrotizing forms; discontinue immediately if suspected.
Acute gallbladder disease — cholelithiasis (2.2%) and cholecystitis (0.8%) at higher rates than placebo; may require cholecystectomy.
Severe hypoglycemia — especially when combined with sulfonylureas or insulin; can be life-threatening.
Acute kidney injury — secondary to volume depletion from severe GI adverse reactions; reported with the GLP-1 receptor agonist class.
Anaphylaxis and angioedema — serious hypersensitivity reactions reported; discontinue immediately and treat appropriately.
Pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia — delayed gastric emptying may result in retained gastric contents; inform surgical/anesthesia team.
Suicidal ideation and behavior — added to label in 2017; 0.3% of adults and 0.8% of pediatric patients in trials.
Heart failure — liraglutide's heart rate elevation is a monitoring concern; LEADER trial showed no harm in CV-high-risk patients but individualize assessment.

dose_references.context: SCALE Diabetes RCT — weight loss in overweight/obese adults with T2D

citation.title: Efficacy of Liraglutide for Weight Loss Among Patients With Type 2 Diabetes: The SCALE Diabetes Randomized Clinical Trial

dose_references.notes: Verified: 56-week double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT (2:1:1 randomization), n=846. Liraglutide 3.0 mg produced 6.0% weight loss (6.4 kg) vs. 2.0% (2.2 kg) for placebo; 54.3% of 3.0 mg group achieved ≥5% weight loss vs. 21.4% placebo. The 1.8 mg arm produced 4.7% loss. More GI adverse events in liraglutide arms.

dose_references.context: LEADER cardiovascular outcomes trial — T2D patients at high CV risk

citation.title: Liraglutide and Cardiovascular Outcomes in Type 2 Diabetes (LEADER Trial)

dose_references.notes: RCT, n=9340, median follow-up 3.8 years. Liraglutide reduced all-cause mortality (HR 0.85, 95% CI 0.74–0.97, P=0.02) and the composite MACE endpoint vs. placebo. Renal composite endpoint also reduced (LEADER renal outcomes analysis, NEJM 2017, doi 10.1056/NEJMoa1616011).

synergies.rationale: Educational rationale: metformin reduces hepatic gluconeogenesis via AMPK activation while liraglutide acts on incretin pathways, beta-cell insulin secretion, and central appetite suppression — mechanistically complementary. ADA/EASD guidelines position metformin as a foundational agent to which GLP-1 receptor agonists are commonly added in T2D management.
synergies.caution_notes: No unique pharmacokinetic interaction. Metformin-associated GI side effects may compound liraglutide-induced nausea early in titration. Monitor renal function if dehydration from GI side effects occurs. Educational framework only — not a prescribed combination; defer to treating clinician. Combination observed as background therapy in trials rather than the direct randomized intervention; graded HUMAN_OBS pending a direct citation.

synergies.rationale: Educational rationale: GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT2 inhibitors address distinct mechanisms — liraglutide reduces appetite and improves insulin secretion; SGLT2 inhibitors promote urinary glucose excretion and reduce cardiac preload/afterload. A post-hoc analysis of the LIRA-ADD2SGLT2i trial found that adding liraglutide to SGLT2 inhibitor background therapy produced a significant additional HbA1c reduction (treatment difference −0.74%); the additional body-weight reduction was modest and did NOT reach statistical significance (−0.86 kg, p=0.06). Both drug classes carry independent cardiovascular and renal outcome benefits in their own trials.
synergies.caution_notes: Evidence for the combination comes from a post-hoc subgroup analysis, not a primary RCT endpoint, so it is graded as observational. Combined use can increase risk of volume depletion and hypotension. SGLT2i carry an independent diabetic ketoacidosis risk that should be monitored. Hypoglycemia risk is low in the absence of a sulfonylurea or insulin. Educational framework only.

synergies.rationale: Educational rationale: liraglutide addresses postprandial glucose excursions and appetite while basal insulin covers fasting hyperglycemia — pharmacologically complementary. Adding a GLP-1 RA to existing insulin therapy can reduce daily glucose variability and may allow insulin dose reduction while limiting weight gain.
synergies.caution_notes: Significant hypoglycemia risk if basal insulin dose is not reduced when liraglutide is initiated; the FDA label recommends considering insulin dose reduction. This is a high-risk combination that must be managed by the prescribing clinician — the app does not recommend or adjust insulin doses. Avoid co-administration with bolus insulin unless under specialist oversight. Combination observed as background therapy in trials rather than the direct randomized intervention; graded HUMAN_OBS pending a direct citation.

synergies.rationale: Educational/mechanistic rationale: GLP-1 receptor agonists produce weight loss that includes some absolute lean-mass reduction, and resistance exercise preserves and builds lean mass. A 2024 Diabetes Care review proposed tailored resistance training as a mechanistically rational adjunct to incretin therapy to optimize body composition (maximize fat loss, minimize lean-mass loss). A pre-specified secondary analysis of an RCT in non-diabetic adults with overweight/obesity (PMC11154779) found liraglutide reduced thigh muscle fat (treatment difference −0.24%, P=0.009), which is consistent with a complementary role for resistance training.
synergies.caution_notes: No dedicated liraglutide + supervised resistance-training RCT with body composition as a primary endpoint has been published as of 2025; this is a mechanistic and review-level rationale, not direct combination evidence, so it is graded MECHANISTIC. Exercise capacity may be limited early in therapy by GI side effects. Educational framework, not a protocol.

name: Cagrilintide
short_description: Cagrilintide is a long-acting, lipidated dual amylin and calcitonin receptor agonist (DACRA) developed by Novo Nordisk. It is derived from human amylin (IAPP) and engineered for once-weekly subcutaneous dosing. As of May 2026, it is NOT FDA-approved as a standalone agent; a New Drug Application for CagriSema (cagrilintide + semaglutide combination) was submitted to the FDA in December 2025 and is under review. Phase 3 RCT data (REDEFINE 1, NEJM 2025) demonstrates clinically meaningful weight loss in non-diabetic adults with obesity. This is an educational summary of published research, not a dosing recommendation; any use should be supervised by a qualified clinician.
mechanism: Cagrilintide is a 37-amino-acid synthetic peptide derived from human amylin (islet amyloid polypeptide, IAPP) with key substitutions and N-terminal acylation with a C20 fatty diacid via a gamma-glutamic acid linker, enabling albumin binding and a prolonged half-life compatible with once-weekly dosing. It acts as a dual amylin and calcitonin receptor agonist (DACRA), binding to heteromeric receptor complexes formed by the calcitonin receptor (CTR) and receptor activity-modifying proteins (RAMP1 and RAMP3), thereby activating amylin receptor subtypes AMY1R and AMY3R. Animal mechanistic data (eBioMedicine 2025, mouse RAMP1/3 knockout model) demonstrates that weight loss is dependent on AMY1R and AMY3R and is absent in knockout animals. Activated receptors are located in the area postrema, nucleus of the solitary tract, lateral parabrachial nucleus, and mediobasal hypothalamus — hindbrain and hypothalamic satiety centers distinct from GLP-1 receptor pathways. Central effects include enhanced satiety signaling, slowed gastric emptying, and suppression of glucagon, without the insulin-stimulating action of GLP-1 agonists. Because amylin and GLP-1 receptors are anatomically and functionally non-redundant, co-administration with semaglutide has been observed to produce additive-to-greater weight loss than either monotherapy in clinical trials.
monitoring_notes: Serum calcitonin at baseline and periodically — per analogy to GLP-1 receptor agonist protocols; investigate persistent elevations
Amylase and lipase at baseline; re-check if abdominal pain develops to evaluate for pancreatitis
Body weight and BMI at each visit to assess response
HbA1c and fasting glucose — cagrilintide suppresses glucagon and improves glycemic markers; monitor for hypoglycemia, especially if combined with insulin or sulfonylureas
Renal function (eGFR) — especially if GI losses occur (vomiting, diarrhea) due to dehydration risk
Electrolytes in patients with significant GI adverse events
Thyroid imaging/workup (thyroid ultrasound, fine-needle aspiration) if calcitonin becomes persistently elevated or a thyroid nodule is detected
Injection site inspection — rotate sites, monitor for persistent reactions
absolute_contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) — theoretical basis: calcitonin receptor co-agonism; applied by analogy to GLP-1 class protocol language and mechanistic rationale
Multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN2) — same mechanistic basis as above
Hypersensitivity to cagrilintide or any excipient
Active or prior acute pancreatitis — exclusion criterion in published clinical trials
Pregnancy — not studied; amylin analogues and GLP-1 agents are not recommended in pregnancy per trial exclusion criteria
relative_contraindications: Severe gastroparesis or chronic GI dysmotility — cagrilintide slows gastric emptying, likely exacerbating these conditions
Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73 m2) — insufficient safety data in this population; use with caution
History of pancreatitis — excluded from pivotal trials; risk-benefit discussion required
Lactation — no human safety data
Pediatric populations — not studied
Inflammatory bowel disease — not studied; gastric motility effects are a theoretical concern
common_side_effects: Nausea (most common GI adverse event, dose-dependent, typically mild-to-moderate and transient)
Constipation
Diarrhea
Vomiting
Injection site reactions — erythema, induration, pruritus, pain (notably higher rate vs. placebo in phase 2 data; site rotation advised)
Decreased appetite
Abdominal discomfort
serious_adverse_events: Acute pancreatitis — class concern for amylin/GLP-1 class agents; subjects with prior history excluded from trials; monitor for characteristic symptoms

Thyroid C-cell proliferation — observed in rodent carcinogenicity studies with calcitonin receptor agonists; human clinical relevance unknown but precautionary language applies (see contraindications)
Severe gastrointestinal events requiring hospitalization (dehydration, electrolyte imbalance) — risk amplified by combination with GLP-1 agonists
Hypersensitivity reactions to injectable peptide components

citation.title: Once-weekly cagrilintide for weight management in people with overweight and obesity: a multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled and active-controlled, dose-finding phase 2 trial

dose_references.notes: Doses tested: 0.3, 0.6, 1.2, 2.4, and 4.5 mg once weekly SC, n=706 cagrilintide, n=99 liraglutide, n=101 placebo across 57 sites in 10 countries. Mean weight loss ranged from 6.0% (0.3 mg) to 10.8% (4.5 mg) vs. 3.0% placebo at 26 weeks. The 4.5 mg dose exceeded liraglutide 3.0 mg daily (10.8% vs. 9.0%). GI adverse events occurred in 41-63% of cagrilintide-treated participants vs. 32% placebo. Reported for educational/research context; not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.notes: n=3417 adults without T2D. Cagrilintide 2.4 mg monotherapy arm (n=302): 11.8% mean body weight reduction at 68 weeks (trial product estimand) vs. 2.3% placebo; 31.6% achieved >=15% weight loss vs. 4.7% placebo. CagriSema combination arm (cagrilintide 2.4 mg + semaglutide 2.4 mg, n=2108): 22.7% mean weight reduction. Semaglutide 2.4 mg alone: 16.1%. GI adverse events in 80% of CagriSema vs. 40% placebo. 2.4 mg once weekly is the phase 3 dose. Educational context only.

Mean body weight reduction: 13.7% CagriSema vs. 3.4% placebo at 68 weeks. 74% on CagriSema achieved HbA1c <=6.5% vs. 15.9% placebo. Low incidence of hypoglycemia. Cagrilintide used at 2.4 mg once weekly in this combination context.

citation.title: Efficacy and safety of co-administered once-weekly cagrilintide 2.4 mg with once-weekly semaglutide 2.4 mg in type 2 diabetes: a multicentre, randomised, double-blind, active-controlled, phase 2 trial

dose_references.notes: Combination CagriSema produced 17.1% mean body weight reduction at 32 weeks vs. 9.8% semaglutide alone. Confirms 2.4 mg as the selected dose carried into phase 3. Educational context only.

dose_references.context: Animal mechanistic study (eBioMedicine 2025, Carvas et al.) — SC cagrilintide in high-fat diet-fed male mice to map receptor dependency

citation.title: Cagrilintide lowers bodyweight through brain amylin receptors 1 and 3

dose_references.notes: Dose was 3 nmol/kg daily SC in mice. Wild-type animals showed significant fat mass reduction; RAMP1/3 knockout animals showed no response, confirming AMY1R/AMY3R receptor dependency. Animal dose is NOT translatable to human use; ranges intentionally null. Cited for mechanistic context only.

synergies.rationale: Semaglutide activates GLP-1 receptors in the hypothalamus and vagal afferents, reducing appetite via pathways distinct from the amylin/calcitonin receptor system activated by cagrilintide. In REDEFINE 1 (NEJM 2025, n=3417), co-administration was associated with 22.7% mean weight loss vs. 11.8% with cagrilintide alone and 16.1% with semaglutide alone — consistent with engagement of non-redundant receptor pathways. This is the mechanistic basis for the CagriSema combination under FDA review.
synergies.caution_notes: Educational context only; not a dosing recommendation. Additive GI side effects: GI adverse events occurred in 80% of CagriSema-treated participants vs. 40% placebo in REDEFINE 1. The combination amplifies gastric emptying delay — caution in gastroparesis or chronic GI dysmotility. Calcitonin receptor co-activation may compound thyroid C-cell concerns shared with the GLP-1 class in rodent models; human relevance is uncertain, but both agents carry precautionary MTC/MEN2 contraindication language. Not FDA-approved in combination as of May 2026; NDA under review. Any combination use must be clinician-supervised. Combination observed as background therapy in trials rather than the direct randomized intervention; graded HUMAN_OBS pending a direct citation.

synergies.rationale: The phase 2 dose-finding trial (Lancet 2021) included liraglutide 3.0 mg daily only as an active comparator, not as a co-administered combination. Cagrilintide 4.5 mg once weekly produced greater weight loss than liraglutide in that trial (10.8% vs. 9.0%), supporting the idea that the amylin pathway contributes to weight regulation independent of GLP-1 receptor agonism. The rationale for any cagrilintide + liraglutide combination is purely mechanistic by analogy to the semaglutide data; no combination has been tested.
synergies.caution_notes: Educational context only; not a recommendation. Cagrilintide + liraglutide co-administration has NOT been studied in any dedicated combination trial — the liraglutide arm was a monotherapy comparator. GI adverse-event risk would likely be additive, and the same precautionary MTC/MEN2 thyroid C-cell concerns apply. Do not infer a usable protocol from comparator data.

name: Survodutide
short_description: Survodutide (BI 456906) is an investigational once-weekly subcutaneous peptide dual agonist targeting both the glucagon receptor (GCGR) and the GLP-1 receptor (GLP-1R), developed by Boehringer Ingelheim and Zealand Pharma. Peer-reviewed Phase 2 RCTs demonstrated up to 14.9% body weight reduction at 46 weeks (planned-treatment analysis; up to 18.7% in the actual-treatment/completer analysis) in obesity, and histologic MASH improvement in up to 62% of participants. Phase 3 SYNCHRONIZE-1 (76 weeks) reported 16.6% mean body weight loss in topline (press-release) results as of April 2026; the full peer-reviewed publication is pending. FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation was granted in 2024 for non-cirrhotic MASH with moderate-to-advanced fibrosis. Survodutide is NOT FDA-approved as of May 2026; regulatory filing status is unsettled, with the developers' own communications indicating an FDA application is anticipated later (around 2027) rather than already submitted. Educational/research entry only — this is not a prescribing label.
mechanism: Survodutide is a 29-amino-acid glucagon-derived peptide modified to confer balanced GLP-1 receptor co-agonism, with a fatty diacid side chain enabling once-weekly dosing. GLP-1R activation reduces appetite, lowers postprandial glucose, slows gastric emptying, and enhances glucose-dependent insulin secretion. Concurrent GCGR activation increases hepatic and adipose energy expenditure through stimulation of thermogenesis and lipolysis — adding an energy-expenditure dimension absent from pure GLP-1 agonists, while also raising counterregulatory glucose production (gluconeogenesis, glycogenolysis), which is relevant to hypoglycemia/hyperglycemia balance. Preclinical studies in diet-induced obese mice showed greater weight reduction than maximally effective semaglutide doses. Hepatic transcriptional profiling in preclinical work has been proposed to suggest direct hepatoprotective mechanisms relevant to MASH, though this remains mechanistic/preclinical. Sources: Zimmermann et al., Mol Metab 2022 (PMC9679702); Thomas et al., Diabetes Obes Metab 2024 (PMID 38560764). Note: specific in vitro EC50 values and exact residue-modification positions cited in the original draft were not independently re-verified in this audit and have been softened to avoid asserting unverified precision.
monitoring_notes: Calcitonin at baseline and periodically — GLP-1R class precaution for thyroid C-cell changes
Serum lipase and pancreatic amylase at baseline; clinical vigilance for pancreatitis (persistent severe upper abdominal pain radiating to the back)
Heart rate at each visit — glucagon receptor agonism associated with modest tachycardia; relevant in pre-existing arrhythmia
Fasting glucose and HbA1c — monitor for hypoglycemia if co-administered with insulin or sulfonylurea
Liver function tests (ALT, AST) — especially in MASH context; ensure no paradoxical worsening
Body weight and waist circumference — track response
Gallbladder imaging if biliary symptoms develop — class-level cholelithiasis risk from rapid weight loss
Injection-site inspection with site rotation
Blood pressure — possible hemodynamic effects of glucagon receptor activation
Renal function (eGFR) — especially if co-prescribed SGLT2 inhibitors or metformin
absolute_contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) — GLP-1R agonist class precaution; applies by analogy given the GLP-1R component
Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN2) — same GLP-1R class precaution
Known hypersensitivity to survodutide or any excipient
NOTE: Survodutide is NOT FDA-approved; these reflect clinical-trial exclusion criteria and GLP-1 class precautions, not an approved prescribing label
relative_contraindications: History of pancreatitis — trial exclusion criterion; individualized risk-benefit assessment required
Serum lipase or amylase >2x ULN at baseline — trial exclusion criterion
Severe gastrointestinal disease (gastroparesis, inflammatory bowel disease) — gastric-emptying effects may worsen symptoms
Concurrent use of insulin secretagogues (sulfonylureas) or insulin — increased hypoglycemia risk
Pregnancy and breastfeeding — reproductive safety not established in humans
Decompensated cirrhosis — limited safety data; larger safety database lacking
Pediatric patients — no safety or efficacy data
History of suicidal ideation or eating disorders — class precaution noted in GLP-1 agent safety monitoring
common_side_effects: Nausea (markedly more common than placebo in Phase 2 obesity trial; dose-dependent, most pronounced during dose escalation)
Vomiting (substantially elevated vs placebo; dose-limiting in some participants)
Diarrhea
Dyspepsia
Constipation
Decreased appetite
Fatigue
Injection-site reactions
Modest heart rate increase (attributed to glucagon receptor agonism)
serious_adverse_events: Acute pancreatitis (class concern for GLP-1 receptor agonists; baseline lipase/amylase exclusion and monitoring used in trials)
Acute gallbladder disease / cholelithiasis (class risk with rapid weight loss and GLP-1 agonism; event of interest in trial protocols)
Thyroid C-cell tumors (rodent carcinogenicity signal across GLP-1R agonists; human relevance unknown; calcitonin monitoring used in trials)
Hypoglycemia when combined with insulin or insulin secretagogues (risk heightened in T2D patients on sulfonylureas or insulin)
Worsening of diabetic retinopathy (event of interest in T2D protocols; class effect linked to rapid glycemic improvement)
Cardiovascular effects related to glucagon receptor activation (under evaluation in the dedicated SYNCHRONIZE cardiovascular outcomes program; not yet fully characterized)

dose_references.notes: Trial dose arms (NOT a recommendation): 2.4, 4.8, 6.0 mg SC once weekly in 293 participants. MASH improvement with no fibrosis worsening: 47% (2.4 mg), 62% (4.8 mg), 43% (6.0 mg) vs 14% placebo. 24-week rapid escalation then 24-week maintenance. Investigational.

citation.source: Boehringer Ingelheim / Zealand Pharma press release, April 28 2026 (design: Obesity 2025, Wharton et al., doi 10.1002/oby.24184)

dose_references.notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS for this audit: efficacy figures derive from a corporate topline press release, not a peer-reviewed publication (full data expected at ADA Scientific Sessions, June 2026). Dose-flexible design titrated toward 3.6 mg or 6.0 mg once weekly (trial design, NOT a recommendation). Reported: mean 16.6% body weight loss vs 3.2% placebo at 76 weeks; 85.1% achieved >=5% weight loss vs 38.8% placebo. Investigational; figures may change on peer review.

dose_references.context: Preclinical — diet-induced obese mice, chronic dosing (animal data, not human-translatable)

citation.title: BI 456906: Discovery and preclinical pharmacology of a novel GCGR/GLP-1R dual agonist with robust anti-obesity efficacy

dose_references.notes: Source expresses dose as 3–30 nmol/kg/day; the mcg/kg conversion here is approximate and should not be treated as a human-equivalent dose. 30 nmol/kg achieved ~32% body weight reduction in mice, exceeding maximally effective semaglutide. Animal data only; NOT translatable to human dosing.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic note: metformin reduces hepatic glucose output (partly via AMPK) through pathways independent of incretin receptors, and is not an insulin secretagogue, so co-use in T2D is unlikely to add intrinsic hypoglycemia risk. In the Phase 2 T2D survodutide trial, metformin was permitted as background therapy. This is a pharmacologic complement inferred from background-therapy use, NOT a studied co-primary intervention and NOT a recommended protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT: no dedicated survodutide+metformin combination RCT exists; this is background-therapy inference only. Metformin is contraindicated in severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) and needs dose review for eGFR 30–45. Any combination decision is a clinician's call.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic note: SGLT2 inhibitors lower glucose via renal glucose excretion and confer cardiorenal benefit, while GLP-1R agonism lowers glucose via appetite suppression and insulin sensitization — complementary pathways with additive glycemic/cardiometabolic effects demonstrated for the GLP-1+SGLT2 class generally. Any benefit specific to survodutide is class-level extrapolation, NOT a studied combination and NOT a recommended protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT: no dedicated survodutide+SGLT2i RCT exists; rationale is class-level extrapolation. SGLT2 inhibitors can cause euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis, a heightened concern under the caloric restriction and appetite suppression associated with survodutide; genital mycotic and urinary infection risk also rise. Clinician oversight required.

synergies.rationale: Educational lifestyle note (not a drug-drug synergy): potent incretin-class weight loss includes a lean-mass component, and resistance training is the primary evidence-based intervention to preserve or build lean mass during a caloric deficit. Pairing pharmacologic fat loss with structured resistance exercise is mechanistically rational for body-composition outcomes. This is general lifestyle guidance, not a survodutide-specific finding.
synergies.caution_notes: No RCT has evaluated survodutide + resistance training as an intervention. SYNCHRONIZE-1 body-composition data are topline only and pending peer review. Framing is educational and mechanistic.

synergies.rationale: Educational supportive-care note (not a pharmacologic synergy): substantial weight loss with any potent appetite-suppressing agent can reduce dietary intake and micronutrient status. Vitamin D deficiency is common in obesity. Periodic micronutrient awareness is a standard supportive consideration for patients on potent weight-loss agents, extrapolated from GLP-1 and bariatric literature.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_OBS: no survodutide-specific micronutrient data exist; this is extrapolation from bariatric/GLP-1 observational literature, so it is not survodutide observational evidence. Monitoring is precautionary; avoid supraphysiologic vitamin D without confirmed deficiency. Supportive co-intervention only, not a recommendation.

name: Mazdutide
short_description: Mazdutide (IBI362/LY3305677) is a first-in-class, once-weekly subcutaneous dual agonist of the GLP-1 receptor and glucagon receptor (GCGR), developed by Innovent Biologics and Eli Lilly. It is an analog of oxyntomodulin, a naturally occurring gut peptide. Approved in China (NMPA) in 2025 for chronic weight management and type 2 diabetes; NOT FDA- or EMA-approved. Peer-reviewed Phase 2 and Phase 3 (GLORY-1) RCTs demonstrate clinically meaningful body weight reduction; higher-dose (9 mg) and head-to-head data are so far announced via press release/conference and await peer-reviewed publication. Educational/tracking content only — not a prescription or endorsement of use outside China or a clinical trial.
mechanism: Mazdutide is a synthetic analog of oxyntomodulin that simultaneously activates two complementary receptor pathways. GLP-1 receptor (GLP-1R) agonism increases glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppresses inappropriate glucagon release, slows gastric emptying, and reduces appetite via hypothalamic satiety centers. Glucagon receptor (GCGR) agonism stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis/gluconeogenesis, increases energy expenditure through thermogenesis (including brown adipose tissue activation), enhances hepatic fatty acid oxidation via cAMP-PKA signaling, and reduces hepatic lipid accumulation. The dual mechanism is proposed to drive weight loss through both reduced energy intake (GLP-1R arm) and increased energy expenditure (GCGR arm), mechanistically distinct from pure GLP-1R agonists such as semaglutide that primarily reduce intake.
monitoring_notes: Heart rate: GCGR agonism produces dose-dependent resting heart rate increases (up to +17.4 bpm at 9 mg in Phase 1b; +2.6 bpm at week 48 in GLORY-1). Consider baseline and periodic pulse monitoring, with extra caution in pre-existing tachyarrhythmia.
Pancreatic enzymes (lipase, amylase): one Phase 1b participant reached lipase 739 U/L. Monitor at baseline and periodically; evaluate for pancreatitis if symptomatic abdominal pain occurs.
Thyroid calcitonin: GLP-1R agonists carry a rodent C-cell hyperplasia/MTC signal. Calcitonin stayed within normal range in published mazdutide trials, but class-level surveillance is standard.
HbA1c and fasting glucose: glucose lowering occurs even in non-diabetic participants. Monitor in T2D patients on concurrent glucose-lowering agents to avoid hypoglycemia.
Body weight / BMI trajectory: assess every 4-12 weeks; evaluate lean-mass preservation (DEXA if available) given the lack of published mazdutide-specific lean-mass data.
Liver enzymes (ALT, AST): reductions observed in trials (ALT -23 to -29% in Phase 2), but baseline and periodic LFTs are prudent given the hepatic/glucagon mechanism.

Immunogenicity / anti-drug antibodies: ADAs detected in 22-33% of Phase 2 participants; one neutralizing antibody in Phase 1b. Long-term significance unknown — monitor for unexplained loss of efficacy.
Gastrointestinal tolerance: nausea/vomiting/diarrhea are common (up to ~41% nausea at 6 mg in Phase 2). Use dose-escalation titration; assess nutrition and hydration with persistent symptoms.
Renal function (eGFR, creatinine): incretin-class agents affect renal hemodynamics; baseline and periodic assessment is advisable, especially with pre-existing CKD or significant GI fluid losses.
Blood pressure: systolic reductions of -1.3 to -6.9 mmHg observed in Phase 2; monitor patients on antihypertensives to avoid hypotension.
absolute_contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC): GLP-1R agonists carry a boxed-warning-level class concern from rodent C-cell tumor data; mazdutide's GLP-1R component shares it.
Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2 (MEN2): contraindicated due to elevated MTC risk, consistent with GLP-1R agonist class labeling.
Known hypersensitivity or serious allergic reaction to mazdutide or any formulation component.
Pregnancy: weight-loss pharmacotherapy is contraindicated in pregnancy; GLP-1R agonist animal studies show developmental toxicity.
Breastfeeding: not studied; weight-loss incretin therapy is generally avoided during lactation.
relative_contraindications: History of pancreatitis (acute or chronic): incretin-class agents have a monitored association with pancreatitis; lipase elevation seen in Phase 1b — use only with close monitoring.
Severe gastroparesis: GLP-1R-mediated gastric-emptying delay may worsen symptoms.
Pre-existing resting tachycardia or significant arrhythmia: GCGR agonism elevates heart rate dose-dependently; caution with baseline HR >100 bpm or known arrhythmia.
Severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C): pharmacokinetics not established; glucagon-driven hepatic effects may be altered.
Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73 m2): risk of dehydration-related AKI from GI losses; use cautiously with adequate hydration.
Type 1 diabetes mellitus: not studied; hypoglycemia risk without functioning beta-cell reserve.
History of suicidal ideation or active serious psychiatric disorder: GLP-1R class is under pharmacovigilance for neuropsychiatric effects; caution warranted despite no signal in trial PHQ-9 data.
Diabetic retinopathy: rapid HbA1c reduction with GLP-1R agonists has been linked to transient retinopathy worsening; ophthalmologic monitoring advised.
Concurrent insulin or insulin secretagogues (sulfonylureas, meglitinides): increased hypoglycemia risk; concomitant agents may need clinician-directed dose adjustment.
common_side_effects: Nausea (21-41% in Phase 2, dose-dependent; higher in Phase 1b high-dose cohorts)
Diarrhea (19-31% in Phase 2 vs ~15% placebo)
Vomiting (13-28% in Phase 2, dose-dependent)
Decreased appetite
Abdominal distension / bloating
Upper respiratory tract infection (reported in Phase 1b)
Resting heart rate increase (dose-dependent; up to +17.4 bpm at 9 mg in Phase 1b; +2.6 bpm at week 48 in GLORY-1)
Injection-site reactions (incidence comparable to placebo in Phase 2)
Urinary tract infection (reported in Phase 1b)
Development of anti-drug antibodies (22-33% in Phase 2; clinical significance of most not established)
serious_adverse_events: Lipase elevation >3x ULN: one Phase 1b participant reached 739 U/L (ULN ~300), normalized at follow-up; pancreatitis risk requires monitoring.
Neutralizing anti-drug antibodies: one Phase 1b participant (10 mg) developed neutralizing antibodies attenuating GCGR cAMP signaling; long-term immunogenic consequences uncharacterized.
Medullary thyroid carcinoma (class-level theoretical risk): GLP-1R class carries a rodent C-cell tumor signal; calcitonin remained normal in published trials.
Cardiovascular risk from sustained GCGR-mediated heart rate elevation: theoretical long-term concern; no dedicated cardiovascular outcome trial for mazdutide yet.
Acute pancreatitis: class-level concern; no cases definitively attributed to mazdutide in trials, but rapid weight loss is itself a pancreatitis risk factor.
Acute kidney injury secondary to GI fluid losses: class-level risk; requires hydration monitoring in patients with GI adverse events.

Reported mean weight reduction 18.55% (up to 20.1% in responders; 44.0% achieved >=20% vs 2.6% placebo) at week 60; liver fat reduced in those with high baseline liver fat.

A supplementary 9 mg NDA was accepted by NMPA for moderate-to-severe obesity. dose_references.context: Phase 3 DREAMS-3 head-to-head vs semaglutide in Chinese adults with type 2 diabetes. At week 32, 48% of mazdutide-treated participants achieved both HbA1c <7.0% and >=10% weight loss vs 21% with semaglutide (p<0.0001). Reports describe 6 mg and 9 mg mazdutide arms.

dose_references.notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS: as cited, DREAMS-3 is reported via conference presentation (ADA 2025) and company press release, not a peer-reviewed full publication; the dose arms are described as 'per available reports' and are not fully confirmed in the cited source. (Related DREAMS-1/DREAMS-2 are published in Nature, but those are separate trials.) Doses are uncertain and reported only — not a prescribing recommendation.

dose_references.context: No FDA- or EMA-established therapeutic dose. Not approved by FDA or EMA as of the May 2026 knowledge cutoff. Any use outside China or an enrolled clinical trial is investigational. No peer-reviewed community/compounding dosing data.
dose_references.notes: Mazdutide is NOT FDA-approved. No established dose for use outside China or clinical-trial enrollment. Any community or gray-market use is unsanctioned and carries unknown safety risks. Do not extrapolate China NMPA-approved doses to other populations without clinician oversight. Educational/tracking entry only — not a prescription.

synergies.rationale: Educational pharmacology context: mazdutide's GCGR agonism can raise hepatic glucose output, while SGLT2 inhibitors lower glucose via glycosuria and carry established renal/cardiovascular outcome data (EMPA-REG, CREDENCE). The two act on different metabolic nodes (appetite/energy expenditure vs urinary glucose elimination). No dedicated RCT of mazdutide + SGLT2i exists; this is mechanistic/observational context only, not a combination protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: GCGR agonism may theoretically attenuate some ketoacidosis protection; watch for euglycemic DKA risk if these are co-prescribed by a clinician in T2D. Educational context only — not a prescription or recommendation to combine.

synergies.rationale: Educational context: the DREAMS-2 Phase 3 program studied mazdutide as add-on to background oral antidiabetics including metformin in Chinese T2D adults. Metformin lowers hepatic gluconeogenesis via AMPK, which mechanistically opposes the transient gluconeogenic effect of mazdutide's glucagon arm while GLP-1R benefits are preserved. This describes observed add-on use in a trial population, not a head-to-head combination-efficacy RCT.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT: metformin was background therapy in trials, not a randomized combination comparison. Hypoglycemia risk is low given the glucose-dependent GLP-1R mechanism, but a clinician should monitor glucose. Educational summary of trial context only — not a prescription.

synergies.rationale: Educational lifestyle context: substantial weight loss from incretin-class agents raises concern for lean-mass loss during caloric restriction. Mechanistic and observational evidence across the GLP-1 class suggests concurrent resistance exercise helps attenuate lean-mass loss. No mazdutide-specific exercise co-intervention RCT exists; this is a class-level/lifestyle rationale.
synergies.caution_notes: Supportive lifestyle practice framing only; no mazdutide-specific resistance-training RCT data. Not a treatment protocol.

synergies.rationale: Listed only to contextualize the receptor-pharmacology landscape, NOT as a combination. Retatrutide adds GIP agonism on top of the GLP-1R/GCGR axis mazdutide already covers, so the two share overlapping targets (GLP-1R, GCGR). Co-administration would be redundant/additive receptor stimulation with compounded adverse-event risk and no established incremental benefit. No trial has evaluated this combination.
synergies.caution_notes: COMBINATION NOT SUPPORTED BY EVIDENCE AND POTENTIALLY HARMFUL. Overlapping receptor agonism means additive GI toxicity, heart-rate elevation, and hypoglycemia risk without proven added benefit. Listed for pharmacological context only — do not combine.

name: HGH Fragment 176-191
short_description: A synthetic 16-amino acid peptide corresponding to residues 176-191 of human growth hormone (hGH), hypothesized to retain the lipolytic portion of the hGH molecule without GH-receptor binding, IGF-1 elevation, or diabetogenic effects. The unmodified fragment has NO published human clinical trials. Most available human data derive from AOD-9604, a structurally distinct Tyr-substituted analog that underwent Phase I/II trials but failed its primary weight-loss endpoint in a 24-week RCT (n=536) and was discontinued as a drug candidate in 2007. Not approved by any regulatory authority; prohibited by WADA under the S.0 non-approved substances category.
mechanism: Proposed mechanism (derived from animal studies and in-vitro work): the C-terminal 176-191 region of hGH is hypothesized to mimic GH-stimulated lipolysis in adipose tissue without activating the canonical GH receptor pathway responsible for IGF-1 production or insulin antagonism. Animal studies (Heffernan et al., Endocrinology 2001) using beta-3 adrenergic receptor (beta-3-AR) knockout mice demonstrated that chronic lipolytic effects of the structurally related AOD-9604 were abolished in knockout animals, implicating beta-3-AR expression and signaling as a primary mechanism. The fragment also appears to inhibit de novo lipogenesis (fat synthesis) in adipocyte cell-culture models. Because it lacks the domain responsible for GH receptor dimerization, it did not stimulate cell proliferation or raise IGF-1 in the animal studies reviewed. The unmodified HGH Fragment 176-191 has not been pharmacologically characterized in human subjects.
monitoring_notes: No established human monitoring protocol exists for HGH Fragment 176-191, as no human clinical trials have been conducted for the unmodified peptide.

If a clinician is supervising use, baseline and periodic fasting glucose and HbA1c are reasonable given general peptide metabolic effects, even though AOD-9604 trials showed no significant glucose changes.

IGF-1 monitoring is less critical for this fragment than for full-length hGH or GHRH analogs, as both animal studies and AOD-9604 human trials showed no IGF-1 elevation. Monitor if stacked with GHRH analogs or GH secretagogues.
Monitor injection sites for redness, swelling, or induration (subcutaneous injection general precaution).
Lipid panel (total cholesterol, HDL, LDL, triglycerides) at baseline if used for body composition purposes; AOD-9604 showed no adverse lipid changes in trials.
Any constitutional symptoms (palpitations, chest tightness, headache, flushing, hypoglycemia-like symptoms) should prompt discontinuation and clinician evaluation.
WADA-prohibited: athletes in tested sports must be aware this compound is banned under S.0 (non-approved substances) regardless of intended use.
FDA December 2024 PCAC voted against including AOD-9604 on the 503A compounding bulks list; clinicians should be aware no approved compounding supply chain exists.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy where growth factor or adipokine signaling may be relevant (general precaution for any unapproved GH-axis peptide; insufficient human data to characterize oncologic risk).
Pregnancy and breastfeeding (no safety data in humans; general precaution for all unapproved research peptides).
Known hypersensitivity or allergy to any component of the peptide preparation.
Pediatric use (open growth plates; no pediatric data; growth-axis active compounds contraindicated without specialist oversight).
relative_contraindications: Diabetes mellitus requiring insulin therapy (lipolysis increases free fatty acids; monitor glucose closely; AOD-9604 trials showed no adverse glucose effect but this was in non-insulin-dependent subjects).
Severe hepatic or renal impairment (no PK data for peptide clearance in organ impairment).
History of pituitary tumors or acromegaly (any GH-axis compound requires specialist caution).
Concurrent use of insulin or oral hypoglycemics (free fatty acid release may alter insulin sensitivity unpredictably).
Concurrent use with GH secretagogues or GHRH analogs that raise IGF-1 (reintroduces mitogenic risks the fragment alone is hypothesized to avoid).
Competitive athletes in WADA-tested sports (prohibited under S.0 non-approved substances).
common_side_effects: Injection site reactions: redness, irritation, mild pain, swelling (class effect for SC peptide injections; reported anecdotally).
Transient headache (reported anecdotally with community use).
Transient flushing or warmth (anecdotal).
Mild hypoglycemia-like symptoms when injected in a fasted state (theoretical; lipolysis increases free fatty acid mobilization).
Palpitations (anecdotally reported; no mechanistic explanation established).
In AOD-9604 human trials (closest proxy), adverse event rates were reported as indistinguishable from placebo across approximately 900 participants (Stier et al. 2013).
serious_adverse_events: No serious adverse events attributable to AOD-9604 were identified in Phase I/II clinical trials covering approximately 900 human subjects (Stier et al. 2013, JOFEM).
No human safety data exist for the unmodified HGH Fragment 176-191; serious adverse event profile in humans is unknown.
Theoretical concern: immunogenicity (antibody formation against synthetic peptide). AOD-9604 trials showed no anti-AOD9604 antibodies, but this may not generalize to the unmodified fragment.

Theoretical concern: compounding/impurity risk. FDA PCAC (December 2024) cited peptide impurities and potential immunogenicity as reasons for rejecting AOD-9604 from the 503A compounding list; unregulated sources carry unknown contamination risk.
Theoretical long-term carcinogenesis risk cannot be ruled out given absence of long-term human data, despite the fragment lacking IGF-1-raising activity in animal and AOD-9604 studies.
