(Int J Obes 2001, PMID 11673763) administered human GH and AOD9604 to obese ob/ob mice via mini-osmotic pumps for 14 days; both reduced body weight gain and increased fat oxidation. Exact mg/kg doses not extractable from the abstract. No human dose data exist for the unmodified 176-191 fragment.

dose_references.context: Community/bodybuilding anecdotal subcutaneous dosing of unmodified HGH Fragment 176-191 — NO clinical trial basis
dose_references.notes: Online communities circulate SC injection protocols (commonly cited figures around 250-500 mcg once or twice daily) for the unmodified fragment. There are ZERO published human clinical trials for HGH Fragment 176-191 by any route. These figures are NOT sourced from any peer-reviewed study or regulatory document, carry no citation, and must NOT be treated as established or recommended doses. Numbers are intentionally left null. Flag prominently in any UI display.

synergies.rationale: CJC-1295 is a GHRH analog that stimulates pituitary GH secretion, raising endogenous GH and downstream IGF-1. HGH Fragment 176-191 is hypothesized to act peripherally on adipocyte beta-3 adrenergic pathways independent of the GH receptor. The mechanistic rationale sometimes cited for the pairing is that GHRH analogs work upstream at the pituitary while the fragment is hypothesized to act on adipose lipolysis directly. This is a pharmacodynamic hypothesis only — no human combination data exist.
synergies.caution_notes: CJC-1295 raises IGF-1; the fragment alone does not. Combining them may reintroduce IGF-1-related risks (glucose dysregulation, potential cell-proliferative effects) that the fragment is hypothesized to avoid. No human safety data for this combination. Educational context only; not a protocol recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Ipamorelin is a selective GH secretagogue (ghrelin/GHSR-1a agonist) that can trigger pulsatile GH release with minimal cortisol or prolactin elevation. It acts at a different receptor than the C-terminal fragment, which is hypothesized to act peripherally. The educational rationale cited for co-use is non-overlapping receptor targets. No human data for this combination exist.
synergies.caution_notes: Ipamorelin may mildly raise IGF-1 depending on dose and frequency. Stacking two GH-axis-active compounds compounds the lack of human safety data. Educational context only; not a protocol recommendation.

synergies.rationale: MOTS-c is a mitochondrially-encoded peptide reported to activate AMPK signaling in preclinical models. The educational mechanistic rationale sometimes cited for co-use is that HGH Fragment 176-191 is hypothesized to mobilize adipose lipolysis while MOTS-c is hypothesized to enhance mitochondrial substrate oxidation — potentially complementary metabolic pathways. No published human or animal combination data exist.
synergies.caution_notes: Both compounds lack robust human clinical trial data. The purported synergy is based entirely on mechanistic extrapolation. Educational framing only; not a protocol recommendation.

synergies.with_name: Semaglutide (or other GLP-1 / GLP-1-GIP receptor agonists)
synergies.rationale: GLP-1 receptor agonists (e.g., semaglutide, tirzepatide) produce weight loss predominantly through CNS-mediated appetite suppression and gut-hormone signaling. HGH Fragment 176-191 is hypothesized to act peripherally on adipose lipolysis via a distinct mechanism. The educational rationale sometimes cited is non-overlapping receptor targets. No human data for this combination exist, and AOD-9604 itself failed to show meaningful weight loss in its largest human trial.
synergies.caution_notes: GLP-1 agonists are FDA-approved with established efficacy; adding the fragment has no evidence of additive benefit in humans, and the closest analog's own Phase IIb trial failed. Combining an unproven research peptide with an FDA-approved medication requires clinician oversight. Educational context only; not a protocol recommendation.

name: Tesofensine
short_description: Tesofensine (NS2330) is an investigational oral triple monoamine reuptake inhibitor (serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine) originally developed for neurodegenerative disease and repurposed for obesity after weight-loss side effects were observed in early trials. It is NOT FDA-approved. Phase 2b RCT data (TIPO-1, Lancet 2008) reported placebo-subtracted weight loss of up to 10.6% over 24 weeks, but that trial carries an unresolved Lancet Expression of Concern (2013) regarding adverse-event under-reporting, so its safety data should be treated with caution. A combination product (Tesomet: tesofensine + metoprolol) is in development by Saniona/Medix to mitigate cardiovascular side effects. In December 2025 the FDA issued a warning letter to a compounder (Darmerica) for distributing tesofensine as an API ineligible for compounding under section 503A. This entry is educational and does not constitute a dosing recommendation; any use should be directed by a qualified clinician.
mechanism: Tesofensine blocks the presynaptic reuptake transporters for all three monoamines: dopamine (DAT), norepinephrine (NET), and serotonin (SERT), belonging to the phenyltropane chemical class. Elevated synaptic monoamine concentrations are thought to suppress appetite via multiple CNS pathways including the lateral hypothalamus, where preclinical work reports silencing of GABAergic neurons that drive feeding behavior (transgenic mouse and rat models, PLOS ONE 2024). Elevated norepinephrine may also activate sympathetic thermogenesis, contributing a modest increase in nocturnal energy expenditure (~4.6% in one short human study) and fat oxidation. The dopaminergic component may reduce reward-driven eating. Because the norepinephrine component causes dose-dependent increases in heart rate and blood pressure, co-development with a beta-1 blocker (metoprolol, the Tesomet formulation) was pursued to attenuate cardiovascular effects.
monitoring_notes: Baseline and periodic cardiovascular assessment (resting heart rate, blood pressure, ECG) — tesofensine produces dose-dependent increases in heart rate (up to ~9.8 bpm at 1.0 mg in TIPO-1) and modest blood-pressure increases.

Psychiatric screening: baseline assessment for anxiety/panic disorder, PTSD, eating disorders, depression, and suicidal ideation — sympathomimetic and serotonergic activity may exacerbate these. One Tesomet pilot patient discontinued due to exacerbated anxiety.
Liver and renal function: patients with significant hepatic or renal impairment were excluded from trials; clearance implications are not well characterized.
Thyroid function: untreated thyroid disease was a trial exclusion criterion.
Monitor for serotonin syndrome (agitation, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, clonus, tachycardia) if any serotonergic agent is co-administered.
Sleep quality: insomnia/sleep disturbance was a frequent, dose-dependent adverse event (up to ~50% in the Tesomet pilot); assess at follow-up.
Body weight and body composition at regular intervals.

Heightened vigilance for neuropsychiatric symptoms is warranted because the TIPO-1 Lancet trial carries an unresolved 2013 Expression of Concern regarding under-reporting of headache, depression, and migraine adverse events.
absolute_contraindications: Current or recent (within 14 days) use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) — risk of hypertensive crisis and serotonin syndrome.
Concurrent use of other serotonergic drugs (SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, triptans, linezolid, methylene blue) — heightened serotonin syndrome risk.
Uncontrolled hypertension — norepinephrine reuptake inhibition can worsen blood pressure.
Significant cardiac arrhythmias — heart-rate increases documented across therapeutic doses.
Recent myocardial infarction or unstable cardiac disease.
Active eating disorders (anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa).
Active psychiatric conditions with suicidal ideation.
Pregnancy and breastfeeding — no human safety data; reproductive toxicity not characterized.
Procurement outside a sanctioned clinical trial in the US: tesofensine is NOT FDA-approved and is NOT eligible for compounding under section 503A (FDA warning letter to Darmerica, December 2025).
relative_contraindications: Anxiety disorders (generalized anxiety, panic, PTSD) — sympathomimetic/stimulant properties may worsen symptoms.
Significant hepatic impairment — excluded from trials; clearance unknown.
Significant renal impairment — excluded from trials; clearance unknown.
History of substance use disorders — dopaminergic activation may carry abuse-potential considerations (formal abuse-potential studies not reported in available literature).
Untreated thyroid disease — trial exclusion criterion; hyperthyroidism could amplify sympathomimetic effects.
Pre-existing sleep disorders — insomnia is a dose-dependent adverse event.
Age extremes — no data in children (outside investigational Prader-Willi adolescent trials) or the very elderly.
Concomitant stimulant medications (amphetamines, methylphenidate, modafinil) — additive sympathomimetic/cardiovascular effects.
common_side_effects: Dry mouth (dose-dependent; up to ~46% in the Tesomet pilot at 0.5 mg)
Insomnia / sleep disturbances (dose-dependent; up to ~50% in the Tesomet pilot)
Headache (up to ~38%; under-reporting flagged in TIPO-1)
Nausea
Constipation / hard stools
Diarrhea
Dizziness (up to ~46% in the Tesomet hypothalamic-obesity pilot)
Heart-rate increase (mean ~7.3-9.8 bpm at 0.5-1.0 mg in TIPO-1; reportedly mitigated by metoprolol in Tesomet)
Mild blood-pressure elevation (~1-5 mmHg systolic at 0.5-1.0 mg)
serious_adverse_events: Hypertension requiring discontinuation — reported at therapeutic doses; dose-dependent sympathomimetic effect.
Tachycardia / significant heart-rate increase — 56.2% of tesofensine subjects vs 18.8% placebo had a maximum heart-rate increase >=10 bpm in TIPO-1.

Psychiatric decompensation (anxiety, depression) — exacerbated anxiety caused one discontinuation in the Tesomet pilot; under-reporting of depression/headache flagged by a Danish health authority audit (Lancet Expression of Concern 2013).
Serotonin syndrome — theoretical but serious risk if combined with other serotonergic agents or MAOIs; no cases reported in isolated trials.
Overall withdrawal due to adverse events: ~13% (tesofensine) vs ~6% (placebo) in TIPO-1.
DATA-INTEGRITY CAVEAT: the Lancet Expression of Concern (2013) indicates the TIPO-1 adverse-event profile may be incompletely reported — treat available safety data with heightened caution.

dose_references.context: TIPO-1 Phase 2b RCT (Astrup et al., Lancet 2008) — 203 obese adults (BMI 30-40), 24 weeks; three oral dose arms in a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design. The Lancet issued an unresolved Expression of Concern in 2013 regarding adverse-event under-reporting in this trial, so its safety data are considered unreliable.

citation.title: Effect of tesofensine on bodyweight loss, body composition, and quality of life in obese patients: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (Astrup et al., Lancet 2008)
Expression of Concern: Tesofensine TIPO-1 Trial (The Lancet 2013)

dose_references.notes: Doses 0.25 mg, 0.5 mg, and 1.0 mg once daily tested. Reported placebo-subtracted weight loss: 4.5% (0.25 mg), 9.2% (0.5 mg), 10.6% (1.0 mg) at 24 weeks. Heart rate increased ~7.3 bpm (0.5 mg) and ~9.8 bpm (1.0 mg). The 0.5 mg arm is described by the developer as the intended therapeutic dose. IMPORTANT: the unresolved Lancet Expression of Concern (2013) indicates adverse events (headache, depression) may have been under-reported per a Danish health authority audit; these figures are documented from the published literature for education only and are NOT a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: Tesomet Phase 2a RCT (Arlien-Soborg et al., European Journal of Endocrinology 2022) — small pilot RCT in 21 adult hypopituitary patients with acquired hypothalamic obesity, 24 weeks; fixed oral dose tesofensine 0.5 mg combined with metoprolol 50 mg daily. Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS due to very small sample size (n=21, pilot).
dose_references.frequency: once daily (as Tesomet: 0.5 mg tesofensine + 50 mg metoprolol)

citation.title: Randomized controlled trial of Tesomet for weight loss in hypothalamic obesity (Arlien-Soborg et al., European Journal of Endocrinology 2022)

dose_references.notes: n=21 (small pilot RCT); reported mean weight loss 6.6% vs 0.3% placebo (p=0.02). No significant heart rate or blood pressure difference vs placebo — cited as supporting the cardiovascular rationale for the metoprolol combination. Most common adverse events: sleep disturbances (50%), dry mouth (43%), headache (36%). One patient discontinued due to exacerbated anxiety. FDA Orphan Drug Designation granted for hypothalamic obesity (2021). Educational reference only, not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: Energy metabolism and appetite study (Sjodin et al., Int J Obesity 2010) — short-term 14-day dose-escalation study in 32 healthy overweight/moderately obese men; exploratory metabolic endpoints, not a weight-loss efficacy RCT. Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS (small, short-term exploratory).
dose_references.frequency: 2.0 mg/day for 7 days then 1.0 mg/day for 7 days

citation.title: The effect of the triple monoamine reuptake inhibitor tesofensine on energy metabolism and appetite in overweight and moderately obese men (Sjodin et al., Int J Obesity 2010)

dose_references.notes: These doses (1.0-2.0 mg) EXCEED the intended therapeutic range from TIPO-1 and were used only in this short 14-day exploratory study. No significant effect on total 24-hour energy expenditure vs placebo; nighttime energy expenditure reportedly increased 4.6%; fat oxidation increased ~18 g/24h. Included solely to document doses that appeared in a published trial — explicitly NOT a long-term dosing recommendation and not a safe target dose.

dose_references.context: Animal model doses (rats and mice) — mechanistic preclinical studies only, NOT translatable to human dosing.

citation.title: Tesofensine, a novel antiobesity drug, silences GABAergic hypothalamic neurons (PLOS ONE 2024)

Tesofensine, a Novel Triple Monoamine Reuptake Inhibitor, Induces Appetite Suppression by Indirect Stimulation of alpha1 Adrenoceptor and Dopamine D1 Receptor Pathways in the Diet-Induced Obese Rat (PMC 2011)

dose_references.notes: Animal-only data: numeric mg/kg ranges nulled per policy because they are not translatable to humans and must not be used to infer human dosing. Preclinical rat/mouse studies (roughly 1-6 mg/kg SC in the literature) reported appetite suppression and, at higher doses, stereotypy (head-weaving). Mechanistic context only.

synergies.rationale: The Tesomet investigational fixed-dose combination pairs tesofensine with the beta-1-selective adrenoceptor blocker metoprolol. The pharmacologic rationale is that beta-1 blockade blunts the sympathomimetic cardiovascular effects (tachycardia, blood-pressure elevation) driven by tesofensine's norepinephrine reuptake inhibition while preserving central appetite suppression. A small Phase 2a pilot RCT in hypothalamic obesity (PMC 2022, n=21) reported no significant heart-rate or blood-pressure difference vs placebo with the combination. Educational mechanistic and small-pilot rationale only — not a recommended protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: Metoprolol itself carries contraindications including cardiogenic shock, decompensated heart failure, severe bradycardia, sick sinus syndrome, and (relative) bronchospastic disease. Combining a beta-blocker with a triple monoamine reuptake inhibitor is not a casual practice and requires cardiovascular evaluation and clinician oversight. Tesomet is an unapproved investigational formulation. Do not self-combine or substitute other beta-blockers without pharmacologic/medical guidance.

synergies.with_name: GLP-1 receptor agonists (e.g., semaglutide, liraglutide, tirzepatide class)
synergies.rationale: Mechanistically these classes act on largely non-overlapping pathways: GLP-1 receptor agonists increase satiety, slow gastric emptying, and improve insulin sensitivity via GLP-1R signaling, while tesofensine acts centrally through dopaminergic/noradrenergic/serotonergic reuptake inhibition with effects on reward-driven eating. This creates a theoretical, additive appetite-suppression hypothesis only. No human combination trials exist. Pharmacologic hypothesis, not a recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: No human combination data exist. The cardiovascular risk of tesofensine (tachycardia, blood-pressure elevation) is NOT mitigated by a GLP-1 agent. Additive nausea, appetite suppression, and GI effects are plausible, and the combined autonomic/monoaminergic interaction profile is uncharacterized. Treat strictly as an unvalidated educational hypothesis; do not combine without clinician supervision.

name: Ipamorelin
short_description: Ipamorelin is a synthetic pentapeptide and selective growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHS-R1a / ghrelin receptor) agonist. It stimulates pulsatile GH release from the anterior pituitary with high selectivity, notably without significantly elevating ACTH, cortisol, or prolactin at GH-effective doses in preclinical models. It is NOT FDA-approved. Clinical development was discontinued after a Phase II proof-of-concept trial for postoperative ileus failed to show efficacy, and the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) voted against adding it to the 503A Bulks list in October 2024. All use for body composition, recovery, or longevity is research-only and not supported by human trials.
mechanism: Ipamorelin (Aib-His-D-2-Nal-D-Phe-Lys-NH2) is a selective agonist of the GHS-R1a (ghrelin receptor) on anterior pituitary somatotrophs. Receptor activation couples to Gq/11 protein, activating phospholipase C and generating IP3 and DAG, which mobilizes intracellular calcium and triggers GH vesicle exocytosis. GHS-R1a activation also amplifies GHRH-receptor-mediated cAMP accumulation via PKC-dependent cross-talk, providing a mechanistic basis for the commonly discussed pairing with GHRH analogs. The resulting GH pulse remains subject to somatostatin-mediated negative feedback, preserving physiologic regulatory braking — a key distinction from exogenous GH administration. Elevated GH secondarily increases hepatic IGF-1 production. In animal models, ipamorelin does not significantly affect cortisol, ACTH, FSH, LH, PRL, or TSH at GH-effective doses, unlike older GHRPs such as GHRP-6. A 2005 rat pancreatic tissue study found ipamorelin can evoke insulin release through calcium channel and adrenergic pathways, with possible glucose-homeostasis implications.
monitoring_notes: Baseline IGF-1 before initiation; recheck at 4–8 weeks and periodically. Elevated baseline IGF-1 is a relative contraindication.
Fasting glucose and HbA1c at baseline and periodically — GH elevation promotes insulin resistance and can worsen glycemic control, especially in T2D or prediabetes.
Blood pressure monitoring — GH-axis stimulation can cause fluid retention and mild edema, relevant in hypertensive individuals.

If used after cancer treatment: oncologist consultation required; IGF-1 elevation raises theoretical tumor-promotion concerns based on epidemiologic links between elevated IGF-1 and breast, prostate, and colorectal cancer risk.
No FDA-approved indication exists; any monitoring protocol is at the discretion of a licensed clinician.
Thyroid function (TSH) at baseline — GH-axis changes can interact with thyroid hormone metabolism.
Watch for carpal tunnel symptoms or joint pain (hallmarks of GH excess), particularly with higher or prolonged use.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy (any cancer) — GH/IGF-1 elevation has theoretical tumor-promoting effects; no human safety data in active cancer patients
Known hypersensitivity to ipamorelin or any formulation component
Acromegaly or gigantism (active or history) — would further stimulate an already-pathological GH axis
relative_contraindications: Uncontrolled type 2 diabetes or significant insulin resistance — GH opposes insulin action and can worsen hyperglycemia (FDA PCAC briefing noted hyperglycemia as an adverse event)
Pre-existing elevated IGF-1 — further stimulation provides no benefit and increases risk
Personal or first-degree family history of hormonally sensitive cancers (breast, prostate, colorectal) — theoretical IGF-1-mediated risk, no definitive causal data
Pregnancy or breastfeeding — no safety data
Pediatric use — long-term effects on the developing GH axis are unknown
Uncontrolled stage 2+ hypertension — GH-associated fluid retention may worsen control
Active inflammatory or autoimmune conditions — GH can modulate immune signaling; clinical impact unknown
Concurrent exogenous GH administration — additive GH-axis stimulation, no safety data
common_side_effects: Injection site reactions: mild erythema, transient swelling, or discomfort
Mild headache
Transient facial flushing shortly after injection
Mild nausea
Mild water retention or peripheral edema (less pronounced than with exogenous GH)
Altered sleep patterns or vivid dreams (GH peaks during slow-wave sleep)
serious_adverse_events: Hyperglycemia / worsening insulin resistance — GH is a counter-regulatory hormone; flagged in FDA PCAC 2024 briefing materials
Hypokalemia — reported in clinical studies reviewed by FDA PCAC 2024
Fluid retention sufficient to cause clinically significant edema — GH class effect; reported in PCAC briefing
Theoretical: IGF-1-driven promotion of pre-existing or occult malignancy — long-term human safety data absent
Theoretical: pituitary axis dysregulation with chronic use — no long-term human trials conducted

This was a single-dose pharmacology characterization study, NOT a therapeutic dose-finding study. No therapeutic dose for any human indication has been established. Educational reference only — do not treat these IV research doses as a usable protocol.

Development was discontinued. CORRECTION: original entry listed this as SC and cited a trial-registry stub (NCT01280344); the published trial is the IV NCT00672074 proof-of-concept by Beck et al. This is the ONLY published human therapeutic dose; no dose for body composition, longevity, or performance has been tested in humans. Educational reference only, not a recommended protocol.

dose_references.notes: Numeric range nulled per research-only / ANIMAL grading rule (no human-applicable dose). Reported rat doses were 0, 18, 90, and 450 mcg/day SC (per-animal total daily dose), producing dose-dependent increases in longitudinal bone growth rate (42 to 52 µm/day) and body weight gain. Animal data only — do NOT extrapolate to humans.

dose_references.context: Animal study — glucocorticoid-induced bone loss counteraction in adult female rats (subcutaneous, 3 months). Rat doses only; no human-applicable range.

citation.title: The growth hormone secretagogue ipamorelin counteracts glucocorticoid-induced decrease in bone formation of adult rats
citation.source: PubMed PMID 11735244, Growth Hormone & IGF Research, 2001

dose_references.notes: Numeric value nulled per research-only / ANIMAL grading rule. Reported rat dose was 100 mcg/kg SC three times daily, which restored periosteal bone formation rate (~4-fold vs glucocorticoid-only) and muscle strength. Animal data only; no human-equivalent dose established.

dose_references.context: Community / compounding-clinic off-label subcutaneous use for body composition, GH optimization, and recovery; no human trial data for these indications
dose_references.notes: ANECDOTAL / RESEARCH-ONLY. No peer-reviewed human RCT has tested ipamorelin for body composition, fat loss, muscle gain, recovery, or longevity. Any circulating dose numbers for these indications are not from published clinical trials and must not be presented as established or safe. The FDA PCAC voted against approving ipamorelin for compounding in October 2024. Licensed clinician oversight required.

synergies.rationale: Ipamorelin activates GHS-R1a (Gq/11 → calcium → GH exocytosis), while GHRH analogs such as CJC-1295 and sermorelin activate the GHRH receptor (Gs → cAMP → GH synthesis and release). These are pharmacologically distinct, non-competing receptor pathways on the same pituitary somatotroph. Mechanistic data indicate GHS-R1a activation can amplify GHRH-receptor-mediated cAMP accumulation through PKC cross-talk, which is the basis frequently cited for an additive GH-secretion effect. This is a mechanistic rationale only — controlled human RCT data on the specific combination are absent.
synergies.caution_notes: Educational framing only; this is not a recommended protocol. Combining two GH-axis stimulants amplifies IGF-1 elevation and associated risks (glucose intolerance, fluid retention, theoretical tumor-promoting effects). A clinician would typically monitor IGF-1, fasting glucose, and HbA1c. Generally avoided with active malignancy, acromegaly history, uncontrolled diabetes, or elevated baseline IGF-1.

synergies.rationale: Same mechanistic class as the GHRH-analog rationale above. Tesamorelin is a GHRH-receptor agonist (FDA-approved for HIV-associated lipodystrophy). Pairing a GHS-R1a agonist with a GHRH analog converges on the same pituitary GH-release signaling. Tesamorelin already produces robust GH/IGF-1 responses at its approved dose, so adding a second secretagogue would further elevate GH-axis activity. Mechanistic rationale only — no human efficacy or safety data exist for the combination.
synergies.caution_notes: Educational framing only; not a recommended protocol. Tesamorelin's FDA label warns of glucose intolerance and fluid retention; these risks are additive with any further GH secretagogue. No human trial data for the combination. Particular caution with T2D, metabolic syndrome, or cancer history.

name: CJC-1295 with DAC
aliases: drug affinity complex growth hormone-releasing factor
short_description: CJC-1295 with DAC is a synthetic, long-acting analog of growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) developed by ConjuChem Biotechnologies. The Drug Affinity Complex (DAC) technology covalently links the peptide to serum albumin via a maleimide-lysine bond, extending its plasma half-life from minutes (native GHRH) to approximately 6-8 days. It reached Phase II clinical trials for HIV-associated lipodystrophy and growth hormone deficiency but was never approved and formal development was discontinued in 2006 following a participant death during a trial. It is not FDA-approved, not available by prescription, and is prohibited by WADA. It remains a research compound only.
mechanism: CJC-1295 with DAC acts as a GHRH receptor (GHRHR) agonist on anterior pituitary somatotroph cells. Binding to GHRHR activates adenylyl cyclase, elevates intracellular cAMP, and stimulates both synthesis and pulsatile release of growth hormone (GH). The DAC modification consists of a maleimidopropionamide-lysine group at the C-terminus that reacts with the free thiol of Cys34 on circulating serum albumin, forming a covalent adduct. This albumin-bound depot resists dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) enzymatic degradation and renal clearance, extending the half-life to 6-8 days in humans (compared to ~7 minutes for native GHRH 1-44). Elevated GH then stimulates hepatic and peripheral production of insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1), which mediates downstream anabolic, lipolytic, and tissue-remodeling effects. Human trials confirmed that GH pulsatility is preserved during continuous CJC-1295 stimulation, though the sustained non-pulsatile baseline elevation distinguishes this agent from shorter-acting GHRH analogs.
monitoring_notes: IGF-1 serum levels: Obtain baseline and monitor during any use. CJC-1295 elevates IGF-1 by 1.5- to 3-fold for 9-11 days after a single dose (Teichman 2006); sustained supraphysiologic IGF-1 is associated with theoretical cancer promotion risk and is a monitoring priority.

Fasting glucose and HbA1c: GH stimulates hepatic glucose output and reduces insulin sensitivity. Monitor fasting glucose at baseline and periodically, with increased frequency in individuals with pre-diabetes, T2D, or metabolic syndrome. The FDA-approved GHRH analog tesamorelin (same class) recommends glucose monitoring; this caution extrapolates to CJC-1295 DAC.

Fluid retention / edema: Assess for peripheral edema, weight gain, joint swelling, and morning stiffness, particularly in the hands and wrists. GH increases renal sodium reabsorption; sustained elevation from the long half-life of the DAC formulation may prolong these effects.
Carpal tunnel symptoms: Screen for paresthesias, nocturnal hand/wrist pain, and median nerve compression signs — GH-induced fluid retention can exacerbate carpal tunnel syndrome.

Injection site assessment: Inspect for pain, swelling, induration, and local urticaria. Injection site reactions were the most common adverse event in Teichman 2006. Nonclinical studies presented to the 2024 FDA PCAC also flagged injection site inflammation and necrosis.
Lipid panel: Nonclinical studies submitted to the 2024 FDA PCAC noted increased cholesterol with CJC-1295 exposure. A baseline lipid panel and periodic monitoring is warranted.
Hemoglobin / CBC: Nonclinical studies flagged reduced hemoglobin. Baseline CBC is prudent.

Pituitary health: Any history of pituitary pathology (adenoma, prior radiation, hypopituitarism) should be evaluated before use. Nonclinical studies reported DNA damage in pituitary cells (FDA PCAC 2024 briefing).

WADA status: CJC-1295 is prohibited at all times under WADA S2 (Peptide Hormones, Growth Factors, Related Substances, and Mimetics). Athletes subject to anti-doping testing must be aware that detection windows for DAC formulations extend to 28-35 days due to slow release kinetics.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy of any type: GH and IGF-1 signaling can promote tumor cell proliferation, inhibit apoptosis, and facilitate metastasis. Active cancer is a hard contraindication for all GH-axis stimulating agents.
History of GH-dependent tumors (e.g., GH-secreting pituitary adenoma / acromegaly): Additional exogenous GHRH stimulation is contraindicated.
Active proliferative or severe nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy: GH is established to promote progression of diabetic retinopathy (PMID 8082520); GH-axis stimulation is contraindicated.
Hypersensitivity to CJC-1295 or any component of the formulation.
Pregnancy: GH-axis manipulation during pregnancy carries unknown fetal risks; no safety data exist.
Prader-Willi syndrome with obesity or respiratory impairment (extrapolated from somatropin class contraindications).
relative_contraindications: Type 2 diabetes or pre-diabetes: GH-induced insulin resistance may worsen glucose control. Use with heightened caution, frequent monitoring, and clinician supervision only.
Uncontrolled hypothyroidism: Hypothyroidism can blunt GH axis response and may interact with GH-mediated metabolic effects; thyroid status should be optimized before any GH secretagogue use.
History of malignancy in remission: Theoretical concern that elevated GH/IGF-1 could stimulate residual cancer cells. Risk-benefit assessment required with oncology input.
Elevated baseline IGF-1 (above age-sex reference range): Pre-existing IGF-1 excess argues against further GH axis stimulation.
Significant fluid retention states (heart failure, hepatic cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome): GH-induced sodium retention may exacerbate these conditions.
Obstructive sleep apnea: GH elevation may worsen upper airway soft tissue hypertrophy and OSA severity.
Carpal tunnel syndrome (existing): GH-mediated fluid retention may worsen symptoms.
Athletes subject to WADA or other anti-doping rules: Prohibited substance at all times under WADA S2.

Coronary artery disease or high cardiovascular risk: A participant death (attributed by the attending physician to plaque rupture/MI, likely unrelated to treatment) led ConjuChem to halt Phase II trials in 2006 as a precaution. The causal relationship was not established, but uncontrolled CAD warrants caution.
common_side_effects: Injection site reactions: transient pain, swelling, induration, and local urticaria (most frequently reported adverse event in Teichman 2006)
Headache (reported in clinical trial subjects)
Flushing / facial flushing (reported in trial subjects)
Nausea (reported in some trial subjects)
Mild peripheral edema / fluid retention (GH class effect; transient in most cases)
Dizziness (reported in trial subjects)
Fatigue or somnolence shortly after injection (GH surge-related)
serious_adverse_events: Trial participant death (Phase II HIV lipodystrophy study, ConjuChem, July 2006): A male participant died following his 11th CJC-1295 injection. The attending physician's assessment attributed the most likely cause to asymptomatic coronary artery disease with plaque rupture and occlusion, believed unrelated to the study drug; however, the causal relationship was never definitively established and ConjuChem halted the program.

Pituitary cell DNA damage (nonclinical finding): FDA PCAC December 2024 briefing documents flagged DNA damage in pituitary cells from nonclinical studies as a safety concern contributing to the committee's vote against 503A compounding approval.
Injection site necrosis (nonclinical finding): FDA PCAC 2024 briefing noted injection site inflammation and necrosis in nonclinical studies.

Potential for neoplasia promotion via sustained IGF-1 elevation: High circulating IGF-1 is associated with increased cancer risk in epidemiologic data (class-level concern, not a trial-derived finding specific to CJC-1295).
Carpal tunnel syndrome: Rare cases reported in association with GH excess / sustained IGF-1 elevation (GH class effect).
Glucose intolerance / new-onset diabetes: GH-induced insulin resistance is a class risk; closely monitored in GHRH analog trials.

dose_references.context: Teichman et al. 2006 (JCEM) — Phase II, randomized, placebo-controlled, double-blind, ascending-dose study in healthy adults aged 21-61. Study 1 (28 days): single subcutaneous injection of one of four ascending doses. Study 2 (49 days): two or three weekly or biweekly subcutaneous doses. Authors reported doses of 30 and 60 mcg/kg as the best-tolerated range.

citation.title: Prolonged Stimulation of Growth Hormone (GH) and Insulin-Like Growth Factor I Secretion by CJC-1295, a Long-Acting Analog of GH-Releasing Hormone, in Healthy Adults
citation.source: Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 2006, Vol 91(3):799-805 — Teichman SL et al.

dose_references.notes: Small ascending-dose Phase II study with short follow-up (28-49 days). Citation verified (JCEM 91(3):799-805, Teichman et al.). The cited mcg/kg values are the doses reported as best-tolerated in the publication. These are NOT established therapeutic doses — clinical development was discontinued before dose optimization was complete. No established therapeutic dose exists for any indication. Educational reference only; not a dose recommendation.

dose_references.context: Elias et al. 2006 (JCEM) — Healthy men, single subcutaneous injection study examining GH pulsatility during CJC-1295 exposure. Two dose groups studied.

citation.title: Pulsatile secretion of growth hormone (GH) persists during continuous stimulation by CJC-1295, a long-acting GH-releasing hormone analog
citation.source: Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 2006 (Dec) — PMID 17018654

dose_references.notes: Single-injection study design; not a chronic dosing trial. Source attribution corrected: PMID 17018654 is published in JCEM (Dec 2006), not American Journal of Physiology, and the year is 2006 (not 2007). URL verified. Doses are reported as studied, not as recommendations. GH pulsatility was preserved. Long-term safety at these doses is unknown.

dose_references.context: Animal study (Kim et al. 2006) — GHRH knockout mice, 1 week old, treated for 5 weeks with CJC-1295 at 2 mcg per animal every 24, 48, or 72 hours subcutaneously. Daily dosing normalized body weight and length.

citation.title: Once-daily administration of CJC-1295, a long-acting growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) analog, normalizes growth in the GHRH knockout mouse

dose_references.notes: Mouse study only. Absolute mcg doses in mice do not translate to human dosing. Numeric range left null per research-only / animal-grade rule. Included for mechanistic reference only.

dose_references.context: Community/off-label use — No peer-reviewed human trial has established a safe and effective dose for body recomposition or longevity use. Any fixed mg or mcg doses circulating in wellness/bodybuilding communities are not derived from controlled trials and should be treated as anecdotal.
dose_references.notes: NO established human dose exists for off-label use (body recomposition, anti-aging, performance). Any specific doses encountered in non-peer-reviewed sources are anecdotal. This compound is not approved for any indication. A clinician must be involved before any use is considered.

synergies.rationale: Ipamorelin is a selective growth hormone-releasing peptide (GHRP) that activates the ghrelin receptor (GHS-R1a) on pituitary somatotrophs via a calcium-dependent signaling pathway, while CJC-1295 activates the GHRH receptor via a cAMP-dependent pathway. These are independent, complementary intracellular cascades converging on GH release. Co-administration of a GHRH analog and a GHRP has been shown in mechanistic and animal studies to produce greater GH release than either compound alone. Ipamorelin is also noted for high GH selectivity with relatively low cortisol and prolactin stimulation compared to older GHRPs (Bowers et al., PMID 9349622). This is educational mechanistic context, not a protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: Additive GH and IGF-1 elevation amplifies all class risks: fluid retention, glucose dysregulation, potential carpal tunnel syndrome, and theoretical concerns around malignancy promotion. No controlled human trial has evaluated the CJC-1295 DAC + ipamorelin combination specifically. Educational and mechanistic rationale only — not a prescription, dose, or protocol recommendation. Both compounds are non-FDA-approved research substances; clinician involvement required before any use.

synergies.rationale: MK-677 is an orally active, non-peptide GHS-R1a agonist (same receptor class as ipamorelin) with a half-life of approximately 24 hours, providing sustained rather than pulsatile GH stimulation. Mechanistically, its GHS-R1a activation engages a different pathway than CJC-1295's GHRH receptor activation, following the same dual-pathway rationale as ipamorelin. This is offered as educational mechanistic context only.
synergies.caution_notes: MK-677's sustained, non-pulsatile GH elevation may compound the glucose dysregulation and insulin-resistance risk from CJC-1295 DAC's own long duration of action, producing prolonged tonic GH and IGF-1 elevation far removed from physiologic pulsatility. Neither MK-677 nor CJC-1295 DAC is FDA-approved. No controlled human trial has evaluated this combination. Educational rationale only — not a dose or protocol recommendation.

name: CJC-1295 without DAC (Mod GRF 1-29)
short_description: A synthetic, tetrasubstituted analog of the first 29 amino acids of human growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH 1-29) engineered for resistance to dipeptidyl peptidase-IV (DPP-IV) degradation. It stimulates pulsatile endogenous growth hormone release from anterior pituitary somatotrophs via the GHRH receptor. NOT FDA-approved; research compound only with no completed human trial for this specific (no-DAC) form. The DAC-conjugated form (CJC-1295 with DAC) reached Phase II trials, which were halted in 2006.
mechanism: Mod GRF 1-29 is a GHRH receptor agonist. It binds to the GHRH receptor (GHRHR) on anterior pituitary somatotrophs, activating the Gs protein/adenylate cyclase/cAMP pathway and stimulating endogenous GH synthesis and secretion. Four strategic amino acid substitutions (positions 2, 8, 15, and 27) confer resistance to DPP-IV cleavage, extending the effective biological half-life from approximately 5 minutes (native GHRH 1-29) to approximately 30 minutes. Because Mod GRF 1-29 lacks the Drug Affinity Complex (DAC) albumin-binding moiety present in CJC-1295 (with DAC), it does not bind albumin; its action is correspondingly shorter-lived and produces a more physiologically pulsatile GH release pattern. There is mechanistic evidence (PMC2603429) that GHRH also acts as an allosteric modulator of the ghrelin receptor (GHS-R1a), potentiating ghrelin binding and downstream inositol phosphate signaling, which partially explains the observed mechanistic synergy between GHRH analogs and GHS-R1a agonists such as ipamorelin.
monitoring_notes: Baseline and periodic serum IGF-1: standard surrogate marker for GH-axis activation, used to assess whether GH secretion is being driven into a supraphysiologic range. Elevated IGF-1 has been associated with insulin resistance and carries theoretical proliferative/cancer risk with chronic elevation.

Fasting glucose and HbA1c: GH exerts counter-regulatory (anti-insulin) effects and can worsen insulin resistance and glycemic control, particularly in pre-diabetes or T2D. Class-level warning derived from tesamorelin (EGRIFTA) FDA prescribing information.
Insulin sensitivity / HOMA-IR: monitor in metabolic syndrome, obesity, or T2D.
Signs of fluid retention: peripheral edema, weight gain, carpal tunnel symptoms (hand numbness/tingling), joint pain — known GH-related effects when GH/IGF-1 are chronically elevated.
Blood pressure: GH elevation can contribute to fluid retention and modest blood pressure increases.
Thyroid function (fasting TSH, free T4): GH-axis activation can influence thyroid hormone conversion; relevant in those on thyroid medication.
Pituitary/hypothalamic axis history: exclude HPA disruption before initiating any GHRH analog (class guidance from tesamorelin FDA label).

Cancer screening status: given theoretical IGF-1-related proliferative risk, ensure age-appropriate cancer screening is current. Active malignancy is a class contraindication per the tesamorelin label.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy of any type (class contraindication per tesamorelin/EGRIFTA FDA label — GHRH agonists elevate IGF-1, a growth factor for many tumor types)

Disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis (hypophysectomy, hypopituitarism, pituitary tumor, pituitary surgery, head irradiation, or significant head trauma) — class contraindication per tesamorelin FDA label
Pregnancy (GH/IGF-1 excess associated with gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, and fetal macrosomia; class guidance from sermorelin and tesamorelin references)
Known hypersensitivity to GHRH analogs or any component of the formulation
relative_contraindications: Uncontrolled type 2 diabetes or type 1 diabetes with poor glycemic control: GH exerts counter-regulatory effects on insulin; monitor glucose closely if any use is considered under clinician supervision
Pre-diabetes / impaired glucose tolerance: GH-driven insulin resistance may worsen metabolic status
Personal history of malignancy, even if in remission: risk-benefit requires individualized clinician evaluation (relative consideration per tesamorelin label analogy)
Active proliferative or severe non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy: consistent with GH/IGF-1 class caution
Breastfeeding: insufficient safety data; GH-axis stimulation in nursing context not established
Pediatric populations: GH-axis effects in children outside diagnosed GHD require specialist oversight
Severe obesity (BMI >40): secretagogue efficacy may be attenuated by somatostatin hypersecretion, and baseline metabolic risk is higher
common_side_effects: Injection site reactions: redness, pain, swelling (most common, typically mild and transient)
Transient flushing or warmth shortly after injection (reported with GHRH class)
Mild headache (reported within 30-60 minutes of injection, correlating with the GH pulse)
Mild water retention / peripheral edema (GH-driven sodium and water retention)
Joint stiffness or arthralgia
Fatigue or somnolence (temporary, associated with the acute GH pulse)
serious_adverse_events: Insulin resistance and glucose intolerance: GH is counter-regulatory; elevation can worsen glycemic control, particularly in metabolic syndrome or T2D. Class warning from tesamorelin (EGRIFTA) FDA label.
Carpal tunnel syndrome: fluid retention from GH excess can compress the median nerve; documented class effect in GH therapy literature.
IGF-1-mediated proliferative risk with chronic use: supraphysiologic IGF-1 is biologically associated with cellular proliferation; long-term safety in humans is not established for Mod GRF 1-29.
Hypothalamic-pituitary axis desensitization/downregulation with chronic pulsatile stimulation: theoretical concern based on receptor biology; not well-characterized for this peptide.

Phase II trial of CJC-1295 (DAC form) was halted in 2006 following a participant death (myocardial infarction); causality was assessed as likely unrelated by attending physicians, but the sponsor halted development as a precaution. This applies to the DAC form in an HIV/lipodystrophy population and should not be extrapolated directly to Mod GRF 1-29, but it underscores the incomplete safety dossier for this compound class in unapproved settings.
Unknown long-term safety profile: Mod GRF 1-29 has never completed a Phase II or Phase III clinical trial; long-term human safety data are absent.

dose_references.context: CJC-1295 (DAC form, NOT Mod GRF 1-29) — human RCT in healthy adults aged 21-61: single ascending SC doses produced dose-dependent 2-10-fold increases in plasma GH for >=6 days and 1.5-3-fold increases in IGF-1 for 9-11 days; 30 and 60 mcg/kg were noted as well tolerated. This evidence is presented for context only and does NOT establish a dose for Mod GRF 1-29 (no DAC). The pharmacokinetics differ fundamentally (DAC half-life ~6-8 days vs no-DAC ~30 min), so extrapolation is not scientifically valid. Numeric values are intentionally nulled because they do not apply to this compound.

citation.title: Prolonged Stimulation of Growth Hormone (GH) and Insulin-Like Growth Factor I Secretion by CJC-1295, a Long-Acting Analog of GH-Releasing Hormone, in Healthy Adults

dose_references.notes: Numbers nulled: the cited RCT studied CJC-1295 WITH DAC, a different compound. No human dose-finding RCT exists for Mod GRF 1-29 (no DAC). HUMAN_RCT grade retained to describe the source study quality, but is_human_data is false for THIS compound and no numeric range is asserted. Cross-reference context from the structurally related CJC-1295-DAC only; no human data for Mod GRF 1-29 itself.

dose_references.context: CJC-1295 (DAC form, NOT Mod GRF 1-29) — human study in healthy men: single SC injection examining GH pulsatility preservation; 60 and 90 mcg/kg groups, basal GH increased ~7.5-fold, mean GH +46%, IGF-1 +45%. DAC form only. Provided for mechanistic context; numbers nulled because they do not apply to the no-DAC compound.

citation.title: Pulsatile secretion of growth hormone (GH) persists during continuous stimulation by CJC-1295, a long-acting GH-releasing hormone analog
citation.source: American Journal of Physiology-Endocrinology and Metabolism, 2006

dose_references.notes: Numbers nulled: cited study is the DAC form. No published human PK/PD study exists for Mod GRF 1-29 (no DAC) as a standalone compound; no established human dose can be stated.

dose_references.context: Mod GRF 1-29 (no DAC form) — no established human therapeutic dose exists. No dedicated Phase I or Phase II RCT has been conducted for this specific compound in humans. Doses circulating in the longevity/biohacker community are anecdotal and unverified by peer-reviewed literature.
dose_references.notes: No human clinical trial dose data exists for Mod GRF 1-29 (no DAC). Community-reported doses must not be cited as clinically established. The class-reference compound sermorelin (GHRH 1-29) was FDA-approved at pediatric doses but discontinued in 2008. Tesamorelin, a different GHRH analog, is FDA-approved for HIV lipodystrophy. Neither approval applies to Mod GRF 1-29.

synergies.rationale: Educational pharmacology: ipamorelin is a selective GHS-R1a agonist (ghrelin mimetic) that stimulates GH release through a calcium/IP3 pathway distinct from Mod GRF 1-29's cAMP/Gs pathway. Mechanistic research (Mousseaux et al., PMC2603429) demonstrates that GHRH acts as an allosteric modulator of GHS-R1a, potentiating ghrelin binding and IP3 signaling. The two complementary mechanisms (GHRHR activation plus GHS-R1a activation) provide a mechanistic basis for amplified GH secretory amplitude. Ipamorelin's selectivity (minimal cortisol/prolactin elevation) makes it the most-studied GHS-R1a agonist in this context. This is an explanation of pharmacology, not a protocol or recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: No human RCT has evaluated Mod GRF 1-29 + ipamorelin. Combined GH-axis stimulation may amplify risks of insulin resistance, fluid retention, carpal tunnel symptoms, and elevated IGF-1. Additive IGF-1 elevation is a particular concern in diabetes, pre-diabetes, or cancer history. Any use should be discussed with a clinician.

synergies.rationale: Educational pharmacology: GHRP-2 is a potent synthetic GHS-R1a agonist. Combined GHRH-receptor plus GHS-R1a stimulation is described in human studies of GHRH/GHRP coadministration as producing markedly greater pulsatile GH secretion than either alone, via complementary receptor mechanisms. GHRP-2 is less selective than ipamorelin and is associated with cortisol and prolactin elevation.
synergies.caution_notes: Human synergy data come from studies using native GHRH or sermorelin, NOT specifically Mod GRF 1-29, so the magnitude does not transfer directly; is_human_data is false for this compound pairing. No verifiable primary citation for a specific fold-change is attached, so quantitative claims have been removed. GHRP-2 adds cortisol and prolactin elevation risk relative to ipamorelin. Neither GHRP-2 nor Mod GRF 1-29 is FDA-approved; combined use may produce supraphysiologic GH/IGF-1 elevations.

synergies.rationale: Educational pharmacology: GHRP-6 is a GHS-R1a agonist that complements GHRH-analog activity through the same dual-axis mechanistic rationale as GHRP-2. It is pharmacologically similar but more strongly stimulates appetite via central ghrelin-receptor activation, which may be undesirable in body-recomposition contexts.
synergies.caution_notes: GHRP-6 causes significant appetite stimulation, which may counteract fat-loss goals. Cortisol and prolactin elevations are reported. Neither compound is FDA-approved. Stacking multiple GHS-R1a agonists compounds these risks.

synergies.rationale: Educational note on pharmacologic redundancy (not a synergy): sermorelin is the unmodified GHRH(1-29) peptide acting through the identical GHRH receptor as Mod GRF 1-29. The two target the same receptor and offer no complementary mechanism, so co-administration adds peptide load without mechanistic benefit.
synergies.caution_notes: Sermorelin and Mod GRF 1-29 share the same receptor target (GHRHR). Co-administration offers no mechanistic advantage and is not synergistic.

name: Sermorelin
short_description: Sermorelin is a synthetic 29-amino-acid analog of endogenous growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH). It was formerly FDA-approved as Geref / Geref Pediatric for idiopathic growth hormone deficiency in children (NDA 19-863 approved 1990; NDA 20-443 approved 1997); that product was voluntarily withdrawn from the US market in 2008-2009 for commercial (not safety or efficacy) reasons, per the 2013 FDA Federal Register determination. No FDA-approved sermorelin product is currently marketed; it is available only through compounding pharmacies under physician prescription. Small, short-term human studies of GHRH-(1-29) show restoration of pulsatile GH and IGF-1 levels in older adults and modest lean-mass effects, but there is no FDA-approved adult indication and no RCT establishing a standard adult dose. Educational and tracking use only — this is not a prescription or dose recommendation.
mechanism: Sermorelin comprises the first 29 amino acids of native human GHRH, the minimal sequence required for full binding to and activation of the GHRH receptor (GHRHR) on somatotrophs of the anterior pituitary. Receptor binding stimulates adenylyl cyclase, raising intracellular cAMP, which drives synthesis and pulsatile secretion of growth hormone (GH). Unlike exogenous recombinant GH, sermorelin preserves the hypothalamic-pituitary negative-feedback loop: rising GH and IGF-1 levels increase somatostatin tone and dampen further secretion, which limits supraphysiologic GH elevations. The half-life after subcutaneous or intravenous administration is approximately 11-12 minutes. Downstream, GH stimulates hepatic IGF-1 production, which mediates most anabolic and lipolytic effects.
monitoring_notes: Measure serum IGF-1 at baseline, again at 4-8 weeks, and every 3-6 months during ongoing use; reduce or discontinue if IGF-1 rises above the age-adjusted upper limit of normal (per original Geref prescribing information and standard GH-axis monitoring practice).
Measure serum inorganic phosphorus and alkaline phosphatase periodically, as GH-axis activation can affect phosphate handling.

Evaluate thyroid function (TSH, free T4) before initiation and periodically; hypothyroidism occurred in 6.5% of patients in Geref clinical trials and can blunt the GH response. Correct hypothyroidism before or during use.

Monitor fasting glucose and HbA1c in patients with prediabetes, T2D, or insulin resistance; GH can induce insulin resistance. Corpas 1992 found no fasting glucose change short-term, but longer or higher-dose use may affect glycemic control.
Assess injection sites for erythema, swelling, or lipohypertrophy; rotate sites.

In patients with a history of or risk for pituitary adenoma or intracranial lesion, obtain pituitary MRI before initiation, since sermorelin requires intact somatotroph function and is inappropriate with structural pathology.
A periodic lipid panel is reasonable; the Khorram 1997 analog trial noted transient hyperlipidemia that resolved by study end.
Monitor blood pressure; GH-axis activation can cause fluid retention, though BP was not significantly changed in available short-term trials.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy or known/suspected cancer not in complete remission — GH and IGF-1 promote cell proliferation and may accelerate tumor growth.
Known hypersensitivity to sermorelin acetate or any formulation component.

GH deficiency secondary to an intracranial lesion (e.g., pituitary adenoma, craniopharyngioma) — not studied; sermorelin requires functional somatotrophs and structural pituitary pathology precludes use (per Geref prescribing information).
Pregnancy — no adequate human safety data; GH-axis stimulation carries unknown fetal risk.

Critical illness (acute respiratory failure, post-surgical/trauma critical care) — GH-axis stimulation is associated with increased mortality in acutely critically ill patients (GH-class safety signal).
relative_contraindications: Untreated or undertreated hypothyroidism — blunts the GH response; normalize thyroid function before or during initiation.
Uncontrolled or poorly controlled diabetes mellitus or significant insulin resistance — GH can worsen insulin resistance; careful monitoring and possible antidiabetic adjustment required.
Chronic glucocorticoid therapy — glucocorticoids inhibit the GH response to sermorelin and may substantially reduce efficacy (per Geref drug-interaction data).
Significant hepatic or renal impairment — altered clearance and abnormal IGF-1 production may yield unpredictable hormone levels.
History of hormone-sensitive malignancy in remission — theoretical IGF-1-mediated recurrence risk; use only with oncology clearance.
Pediatric patients with closed epiphyses — ineffective for stature once growth plates have fused.

High abdominal visceral fat / obesity — human data (Veldhuis et al. 2009, PMC2681313) indicate high visceral fat attenuates the GH secretory response to GHRH-axis stimulation, potentially reducing efficacy.
common_side_effects: Injection-site reactions (pain, redness, swelling) — most common adverse event; ~17% in clinical trials (per Geref prescribing information / RxList)
Transient facial flushing
Headache
Dizziness
Nausea
Hyperactivity or restlessness (<1% in clinical trials)
Transient hyperlipidemia (observed with the [Nle27]GHRH analog in Khorram 1997; resolved by study end)
Mild fluid retention or peripheral edema from GH-mediated sodium retention
Sleepiness or sedation (rare, <1%)
serious_adverse_events: Anaphylaxis / systemic allergic reaction — possible with any peptide; local allergic reactions and urticaria have been reported, rare systemic reactions possible.
Hypothyroidism — occurred in 6.5% of pediatric patients in Geref trials; may require thyroid hormone replacement.
Sustained IGF-1 elevation above age-adjusted normal — supraphysiologic IGF-1 is associated with increased cancer risk and acromegalic features; requires dose adjustment or discontinuation.
Worsening of occult/undiagnosed malignancy — theoretical risk; underscores cancer screening before initiation.
New-onset or worsening insulin resistance / hyperglycemia — GH is counter-regulatory to insulin; clinically significant in underlying metabolic disease.
Carpal tunnel syndrome or joint/muscle pain — class effects of GH-axis activation at supraphysiologic IGF-1 levels.

dose_references.context: Formerly FDA-approved pediatric dosing (Geref Pediatric) for idiopathic GH deficiency in children — once-daily subcutaneous injection at bedtime. Historical/withdrawn label; NOT applicable to adults.

citation.title: Determination That GEREF (Sermorelin Acetate) Injection Was Not Withdrawn From Sale for Reasons of Safety or Effectiveness — Federal Register 2013-04827
RxList Sermorelin Acetate Drug Reference (derived from original Geref prescribing information)

dose_references.notes: FORMERLY FDA-APPROVED (Geref Pediatric, withdrawn 2008-2009); pediatric-only and not applicable to adults. Graded HUMAN_OBS. This was the approved pediatric dosing for Geref Pediatric (NDA 19-863, approved 1990). The branded products were withdrawn from the US market in 2008-2009 for commercial reasons, not safety or efficacy, per the 2013 FDA Federal Register determination (verified). This pediatric indication and weight-based dose are NOT applicable to adults and must not be used as an adult dosing reference.

dose_references.context: Small human crossover study in healthy older men (mean age 68) — short-term subcutaneous GHRH-(1-29) administration to assess GH/IGF-1 restoration. Pilot-scale, not a treatment-efficacy trial.

citation.title: Growth hormone (GH)-releasing hormone-(1-29) twice daily reverses the decreased GH and insulin-like growth factor-I levels in old men — Corpas E et al., JCEM 1992
citation.source: PubMed PMID 1379256 / Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism

dose_references.notes: Corpas et al. 1992 (verified). Randomized crossover but very small (n=10 older men) and short (14 days per arm); graded HUMAN_OBS rather than HUMAN_RCT given the pilot scale and lack of a clinical endpoint. The higher dose (1 mg twice daily) restored 24-hour GH and IGF-1 to values comparable to young men; fasting glucose, blood pressure, and standard chemistry/hematology were unaffected. No dose was established for long-term adult use. This is a physiologic/mechanistic finding, not a therapeutic dose recommendation.

dose_references.context: Analog study: single-blind randomized placebo-controlled trial in men and women aged 55-71 — nightly subcutaneous [Nle27]GHRH-(1-29)-NH2 (a related analog, NOT sermorelin acetate) for 16 weeks, body composition endpoints. Dose nulled because it does not establish a sermorelin dose.

citation.title: Endocrine and Metabolic Effects of Long-Term Administration of [Nle27]Growth Hormone-Releasing Hormone-(1-29)-NH2 in Age-Advanced Men and Women — Khorram O et al., JCEM 1997

dose_references.notes: Khorram et al. 1997, n=19, single-blind. The original 10 mcg/kg figure has been NULLED and is_human_data set to false because this study used [Nle27]GHRH-(1-29)-NH2, a structurally distinct analog, NOT sermorelin acetate; it cannot establish a sermorelin dose. Reported direction of effect (increased nocturnal GH/IGF-1; ~1.26 kg lean mass gain in men; transient hyperlipidemia that resolved) is retained as mechanistically analogous context only. Downgraded to HUMAN_OBS given single-blind design and use of a different compound. No currently approved adult sermorelin dosing exists.

dose_references.context: Off-label compounded adult use — telehealth/compounding-clinic community reports; no published human RCT establishes a standard adult regimen.
dose_references.notes: Various telehealth and compounding-pharmacy sources cite roughly 200-500 mcg once nightly SC in adults for off-label GH optimization; however, NO peer-reviewed RCT or FDA label supports a specific adult dose or regimen. This range is community-derived, intentionally left null, and must NOT be treated as an established therapeutic dose. Any use requires individualized physician supervision with baseline and follow-up IGF-1 monitoring. Educational/tracking context only — not a recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Ipamorelin is a selective GH secretagogue receptor (GHS-R1a / ghrelin receptor) agonist acting on a pathway distinct from the GHRHR. In principle, combining a GHRHR agonist (sermorelin) with a GHS-R1a agonist converges on the somatotroph and can produce greater GH pulse amplitude than either class alone; human data with GHRH + a GHRP (Veldhuis et al. 2009, PMC2681313) show the GHRH-plus-secretagogue interaction in healthy men. Ipamorelin is of interest within this class because it does not appreciably raise cortisol or prolactin at typical doses, unlike GHRP-2/GHRP-6. No direct sermorelin + ipamorelin human trial exists; this rationale is class-level/mechanistic only.
synergies.caution_notes: No direct human data for the sermorelin + ipamorelin pairing. Combining two GH-axis stimulants can raise IGF-1 more than either alone, so IGF-1 monitoring would be essential. Not FDA-approved; it is unknown whether any enhanced GH pulse translates to greater body-composition benefit or to greater harm. Avoid in active malignancy, pituitary pathology, or uncontrolled diabetes. Educational framing only — not a protocol or dose recommendation.

synergies.rationale: GHRP-2 and GHRH analogs act on complementary intracellular pathways in pituitary somatotrophs (GHRH via cAMP/PKA; GHRP-2 via Gq/IP3/Ca2+). Veldhuis et al. 2009 (PMC2681313), a randomized double-blind study in 47 healthy men, showed combined GHRH + GHRP-2 stimulation produces greater GH secretion than either alone. This was a mechanism/determinants study in healthy volunteers, not a treatment RCT of a sermorelin + GHRP-2 regimen. No established therapeutic protocol exists for this combination. Educational reference only.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS: the cited study characterizes the GHRH+GHRP interaction and its determinants, not the efficacy/safety of a therapeutic sermorelin + GHRP-2 stack. GHRP-2 can elevate cortisol and prolactin, especially at higher or more frequent doses. The combination amplifies IGF-1 more than sermorelin alone, requiring careful monitoring in metabolic syndrome or insulin resistance. GHRP-2 is not FDA-approved and no long-term combination safety data exist. Educational framing only — not a prescriptive protocol.

synergies.rationale: Tesamorelin is a stabilized GHRH analog (trans-3-hexenoic acid moiety on GHRH 1-44, DPP-IV-resistant) and is the only FDA-approved GHRH-class analog for adults (Egrifta, approved 2010 for HIV-associated lipodystrophy; verified). It shares the GHRHR mechanism with sermorelin but has higher potency and longer effective action (higher peak GH/IGF-1). Included here strictly as an educational comparator: because both act on the same receptor, they would be pharmacologically redundant, not additive. A clinician choosing a GHRH-class agent with stronger adult evidence for visceral-fat reduction should be aware tesamorelin has Phase III RCT data and an FDA label, whereas sermorelin does not.
synergies.caution_notes: Tesamorelin and sermorelin should NOT be used concurrently — same-receptor redundancy, not synergy. This is an educational comparator entry, not a stacking suggestion or recommendation. If visceral-fat reduction is the goal, tesamorelin carries superior clinical evidence and an FDA-approved indication; discuss with a clinician.

name: Tesamorelin
short_description: FDA-approved synthetic GHRH analog (Egrifta/Egrifta SV/Egrifta WR) indicated to reduce excess visceral abdominal fat in HIV-infected adults with lipodystrophy. Stimulates endogenous pulsatile GH release via pituitary GHRH receptors, raising IGF-1 and promoting visceral lipolysis without direct GH administration. Off-label interest in NAFLD and cognitive aging remains investigational.
mechanism: Tesamorelin is a 44-amino-acid synthetic analog of endogenous growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH). The N-terminal trans-3-hexenoyl modification protects the molecule from dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) cleavage, extending its plasma half-life relative to native GHRH. It binds and activates GHRH receptors on anterior pituitary somatotroph cells via the Gs/cAMP/PKA pathway, stimulating pulsatile synthesis and secretion of endogenous growth hormone. GH in turn triggers hepatic production of IGF-1, which mediates downstream anabolic and lipolytic effects — including visceral adipose tissue mobilization, lean mass support, and hepatic fat reduction. Because it stimulates endogenous pulsatile GH rather than supplying exogenous GH, physiologic feedback is preserved to a greater degree than with recombinant human GH.
monitoring_notes: Serum IGF-1: measure at baseline, then periodically during treatment; FDA label recommends considering discontinuation if persistent elevations above 3 standard deviation scores (SDS) occur, especially with poor clinical response
Fasting glucose and HbA1c: assess before initiating and periodically during treatment, given risk of glucose intolerance and new-onset diabetes
Signs and symptoms of fluid retention: monitor for peripheral edema, joint pain, carpal tunnel syndrome — particularly in the first months when GH-mediated sodium retention peaks
Malignancy surveillance: evaluate for recurrent malignancy in patients with prior cancer history; discontinue if malignancy recurs
Glucocorticoid dosing: patients on cortisol replacement should be assessed for adequacy of dosing, as GH inhibits peripheral cortisone-to-cortisol conversion via 11β-HSD1 suppression
CYP3A4-metabolized drugs: GH induction of hepatic CYP3A4 may modestly reduce plasma levels of co-administered CYP3A4 substrates; monitor as clinically indicated
Lipid panel: baseline and periodic monitoring is reasonable given the dyslipidemia common in HIV-associated lipodystrophy
Long-term cardiovascular safety: per FDA label, long-term cardiovascular safety of tesamorelin has NOT been established; this must be communicated to patients
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy (any) — tesamorelin raises IGF-1, which may promote tumor growth; per FDA label, preexisting malignancy must be inactive and treatment complete before use
Pregnancy — animal data showed hydrocephaly in rat offspring at approximately 2–4x clinical exposure; tesamorelin could cause fetal harm and is contraindicated in pregnancy per FDA label
Known hypersensitivity to tesamorelin or mannitol (excipient) — hypersensitivity reactions including urticaria and facial flushing have occurred

Disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis (e.g., hypophysectomy, hypopituitarism, pituitary tumor, pituitary surgery or radiation) — the drug acts upstream at the pituitary; a non-functional axis eliminates the mechanism of action and may indicate GH deficiency requiring direct replacement instead
relative_contraindications: Pre-diabetes or poorly controlled type 2 diabetes — tesamorelin increases fasting glucose and HbA1c; in clinical trials, ~5% of patients developed HbA1c ≥6.5% vs. ~1% with placebo
History of (inactive/remitted) malignancy — IGF-1 elevation may theoretically promote occult recurrence; oncology clearance and careful IGF-1 monitoring warranted

Critical illness — FDA label warns of possible increased mortality risk in critically ill patients receiving GH or GH-stimulating agents (class effect); consider discontinuing during acute critical illness
Fluid-sensitive conditions (heart failure, severe hypertension, renal impairment) — GH stimulation causes sodium and water retention, edema, and can worsen these states
Glucocorticoid replacement therapy — GH inhibits 11β-HSD1, reducing conversion of cortisone to cortisol in peripheral tissues; patients on glucocorticoid replacement may need dose adjustment
Carpal tunnel syndrome history — fluid retention and GH-related median nerve compression can exacerbate pre-existing carpal tunnel syndrome
common_side_effects: Injection site reactions: erythema, pruritus, pain, bruising (reported in >5% of clinical trial patients per FDA label)
Arthralgia (joint pain) — among the most frequently reported systemic adverse effects (>5%)
Myalgia (muscle pain) — >5% incidence
Peripheral edema — GH-mediated sodium and water retention
Extremity pain
Paresthesias — reported in ~21% of tesamorelin-treated patients vs. ~5% placebo in one RCT (Stanley et al. 2014, JAMA)
Transient glucose elevation — fasting glucose may rise early in treatment; generally returns toward baseline over months but HbA1c can increase modestly
serious_adverse_events: Neoplasm growth promotion — IGF-1 is mitogenic; FDA label requires active malignancy contraindication and monitoring for recurrence in patients with prior cancer history
New-onset diabetes mellitus — ~5% of patients developed elevated HbA1c/diabetes in trials vs. ~1% placebo (FDA label)
Persistent IGF-1 supraphysiologic elevation (>3 SDS) — FDA label states consider discontinuation; long-term effects of prolonged supraphysiologic IGF-1 are unknown
Hypersensitivity reactions — occurred in ~4% of clinical trial patients; included urticaria and facial flushing requiring discontinuation
Carpal tunnel syndrome — GH-class fluid retention can cause compression neuropathy
Fluid retention leading to clinically significant edema — may precipitate or worsen heart failure in susceptible patients

Increased mortality in critical illness — class warning for GH-axis-stimulating agents in critically ill patients (based on exogenous GH data; flagged by FDA, not directly observed in tesamorelin trials)

dose_references.notes: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. 1 mg/day SC for 20 weeks in 152 adults aged 55–87 (66 with MCI), 137 completers. This is an investigational research dose in a NON-APPROVED population and is NOT the FDA-approved clinical dose. Improved executive function (P=0.005); IGF-1 rose ~117%. Off-label; results require replication before any conclusions are drawn. Dose and design verified against PMC source.
