citation.title: Effects of tesamorelin on non-alcoholic fatty liver disease in HIV: a randomised, double-blind, multicentre trial — Lancet HIV 2019
Tesamorelin reduces liver fat and fibrosis progression in people with HIV and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease — aidsmap summary

dose_references.notes: Randomised, double-blind, multicentre trial. 2 mg/day SC for 12 months (n=61 HIV patients with hepatic steatosis; 31 tesamorelin, 30 placebo). 35% of tesamorelin patients reached normal hepatic fat fraction vs. 4% placebo; reduced odds of fibrosis progression. Off-label for NAFLD; not an approved indication. NOTE: the original entry attributed this to 'Bhanu et al.' — the trial is from the Stanley/Grinspoon group; author attribution corrected.

synergies.rationale: Ipamorelin is a selective growth hormone secretagogue receptor 1a (GHS-R1a) agonist (ghrelin mimetic) acting via the Gq/calcium pathway, whereas tesamorelin acts on GHRH receptors via Gs/cAMP. Because these are distinct receptor systems on pituitary somatotrophs, classic pharmacology studies of a GHRP (ghrelin-mimetic) combined with GHRH have shown greater acute GH release than either agent alone in normal men (Bowers et al. 1990). This provides a mechanistic rationale for why dual-pathway GH stimulation is sometimes discussed, but the Bowers data are acute GH-pulse pharmacology in healthy volunteers — NOT a controlled trial of tesamorelin plus ipamorelin specifically, and not a study of any clinical body-composition or safety outcome. Presented here for mechanistic education only.
synergies.caution_notes: Combining two GH-stimulating agents would be expected to amplify IGF-1 elevation and all associated GH-excess risks: glucose intolerance, fluid retention, joint pain, carpal tunnel syndrome, and theoretical promotion of occult neoplasms. No controlled safety study of this specific combination exists. Each agent independently requires full contraindication screening (active malignancy, hypothalamic-pituitary axis disruption, pregnancy, IGF-1 monitoring). Educational reference only — not a prescription, recommended protocol, or endorsement of co-administration.

synergies.rationale: Sermorelin is a truncated GHRH(1-29)-NH2 analog acting on the same GHRH receptor as tesamorelin. There is no pharmacologic rationale for combining two GHRH-receptor agonists, since they compete for the same receptor and would not be expected to produce synergy. Noted only to clarify that tesamorelin vs. sermorelin is a choice between GHRH-analog options, not a synergistic pairing.
synergies.caution_notes: Combining two GHRH analogs is not supported by mechanism and would not be expected to add benefit over either agent alone. Both carry the same class risks (IGF-1 elevation, glucose perturbation, theoretical neoplasm promotion). Listed here only as a pharmacologic distinction, not as a recommended co-administration.

name: GHRP-2 (Growth Hormone Releasing Peptide-2)
short_description: GHRP-2 (pralmorelin) is a synthetic hexapeptide GH secretagogue that potently binds the ghrelin receptor (GHS-R1a), triggering robust GH release from the anterior pituitary. It is approved in Japan as a diagnostic agent for GH deficiency but is NOT FDA-approved in the United States for any indication. Chronic therapeutic use in humans is research-only with limited long-term safety data.
mechanism: GHRP-2 (D-Ala-D-BNal-Ala-Trp-D-Phe-Lys-NH2) is a second-generation synthetic hexapeptide that acts as a potent full agonist at the GHS-R1a (ghrelin receptor) on pituitary somatotrophs and hypothalamic neurons. Receptor binding activates the Gq/11-PLC-IP3-calcium signaling cascade, triggering GH vesicle exocytosis. Simultaneously, GHRP-2 stimulates GHRH-releasing arcuate nucleus neurons and suppresses somatostatin release from periventricular neurons, amplifying endogenous GH pulsatility. Downstream GH release drives hepatic IGF-1 synthesis. GHRP-2 also binds the scavenger receptor CD36, activating PI-3K/AKT1 and PPARgamma prosurvival pathways, which underlies GH-independent cytoprotective and anti-inflammatory effects documented in preclinical models. Unlike some GHRP family members, GHRP-2 does not exhibit meaningful opioid receptor activity.
monitoring_notes: Baseline and periodic (every 3-6 months) serum IGF-1 measurement; elevated IGF-1 (above age-adjusted upper reference range) signals excessive GH-axis stimulation and warrants clinician-led dose review or discontinuation.
Fasting glucose and HbA1c at baseline and every 3 months; GH excess impairs insulin sensitivity and can unmask or worsen glucose intolerance or T2D.
Serum prolactin at baseline and periodically; GHRP-2 can elevate prolactin, which may affect libido, menstrual regularity, and reproductive hormones.
Morning cortisol or ACTH if signs/symptoms of hypercortisolism develop (weight gain, easy bruising, hypertension, hyperglycemia); GHRP-2 stimulates ACTH/cortisol release at studied doses.
Periodic blood pressure assessment; fluid retention from elevated GH/IGF-1 can contribute to edema and blood pressure changes.
Screen for carpal tunnel syndrome, arthralgias, or peripheral edema - known class effects of elevated GH/IGF-1.
In persons with diabetes: more frequent glucose monitoring (fasting and post-prandial) and quarterly HbA1c due to GH-mediated insulin resistance.
Avoid use in individuals with elevated baseline IGF-1 or conditions of GH excess (acromegaly, active pituitary adenoma).

No long-term human safety data beyond several months; cancer-promotion risk from sustained IGF-1 elevation is theoretical but biologically plausible based on acromegaly epidemiology. Discuss with a clinician before any use.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy of any type: elevated GH and IGF-1 may promote tumor growth; acromegaly literature shows increased colorectal and thyroid cancer incidence with chronic GH/IGF-1 excess.
Pregnancy and breastfeeding: no human safety data; animal data insufficient.
Acromegaly or other states of active GH excess.
Active pituitary tumor or pituitary disease where additional GH stimulation is contraindicated.
Known hypersensitivity to GHRP-2 or any component of the formulation.
relative_contraindications: Type 2 diabetes or significant insulin resistance: GH secretagogues can worsen glycemic control; use only with close monitoring and clinician supervision.
Prediabetes or metabolic syndrome: elevated GH acutely impairs insulin sensitivity.
Proliferative diabetic retinopathy: IGF-1 elevation may exacerbate retinal disease.
Obesity (BMI greater than 30): GH response to GHRP-2 is attenuated by adiposity while cortisol and prolactin responses remain; risk-benefit profile less favorable.
Hypothyroidism: untreated hypothyroidism blunts GH response and may interact with IGF-1 metabolism.
History of malignancy in remission: risk of IGF-1-promoted recurrence cannot be excluded; requires oncologist input.
Hyperprolactinemia from any cause: GHRP-2 may further elevate prolactin.
Children and adolescents outside a supervised clinical trial or diagnostic context: growth-plate and hormonal effects not adequately characterized for off-label therapeutic use.
Concurrent corticosteroids or other HPA-axis-active agents: additive cortisol effects.
common_side_effects: Increased appetite and hunger (GHS-R1a activation on hypothalamic feeding circuits)
Transient flushing or warmth at time of injection
Mild somnolence or fatigue shortly after injection
Water retention and mild peripheral edema (GH effect)
Mild cortisol and prolactin elevation
Tingling or paresthesias in extremities (carpal tunnel-type, GH/IGF-1 class effect)
Joint discomfort or arthralgias (GH/IGF-1 class effect)
Injection site reactions (erythema, bruising with subcutaneous administration)
Progressive attenuation of GH response with repeated daily dosing (tachyphylaxis): GH peak declined from 83 to 51 mcg/L over 5 daily injections in one open-label study
serious_adverse_events: GH-induced insulin resistance leading to hyperglycemia or unmasking of latent T2D with chronic use.

Theoretically elevated cancer-promotion risk with prolonged supraphysiologic IGF-1, based on acromegaly epidemiology showing elevated colorectal, thyroid, and other cancer incidence. No direct human evidence from GHRP-2 trials, but biologically plausible.
Significant hypercortisolism with very high doses or prolonged use (ACTH/cortisol stimulation documented at studied doses).
Severe hyperprolactinemia (theoretical with chronic use; hypogonadism, galactorrhea, menstrual disruption).
Hypertension or fluid overload in susceptible individuals (GH-driven sodium and water retention).
Carpal tunnel syndrome requiring intervention (class effect of sustained GH/IGF-1 elevation).

No serious adverse events were reported in published short-term human trials at diagnostic and research doses, but controlled long-term safety trials in humans do not exist for chronic therapeutic use.

dose_references.context: Japan-approved diagnostic dose for adult and pediatric GH deficiency testing (single IV bolus, regulatory standard)

citation.title: A simple diagnostic test using GH-releasing peptide-2 in adult GH deficiency

dose_references.notes: 100 mcg IV bolus validated in a controlled clinical / diagnostic-validation study (77 healthy vs 58 GH-deficient subjects); not a randomized controlled therapeutic trial, so graded HUMAN_OBS. Peak GH response within 60 minutes; diagnostic cutoff 15 mcg/L (or 9 mcg/L with WHO 98/574 rGH standard). Approved by Japan PMDA (Kaken Pharmaceutical, 2004) under the trade name GHRP-2. NOT FDA-approved in the United States. This is a single-bolus diagnostic dose, not a therapeutic regimen.

dose_references.context: Dose-response characterization in older adults with reduced GH secretion - acute and chronic IV/SC dosing
dose_references.frequency: Acute single doses; chronic: 1 mcg/kg/hour SC infusion for 30 days in one arm

citation.title: Growth hormone/insulin-like growth factor-1 response to acute and chronic GHRP-2, GHRH and in combination in older men and women with decreased GH secretion
citation.source: PubMed / Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism

dose_references.notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS: small (n=10) within-subject dose-response / exploratory study, not randomized or placebo-controlled. Doses of 0.1, 1.0, and 10.0 mcg/kg IV/SC tested. GH response to 10 mcg/kg was approximately twice that of 1 mcg/kg. Chronic 1 mcg/kg/hour SC infusion over 30 days raised serum IGF-1 for the duration. Combination of 0.1 mcg/kg GHRP-2 + 1 mcg/kg GHRH produced greater GH release than either alone. Research-context doses only; not an approved therapeutic regimen.

dose_references.context: Dose-response for GH, prolactin, ACTH, and cortisol in young and elderly humans
dose_references.frequency: Single acute IV bolus in research setting

citation.title: Effects of GHRP-2 and Hexarelin on GH, Prolactin, ACTH and Cortisol Levels in Man

dose_references.notes: 1 and 2 mcg/kg IV tested in 6 young adults (ages 22-27) and 6 elderly (ages 66-73). The 2 mcg/kg dose produced a significantly higher GH response (p<0.05) than 1 mcg/kg. Both doses produced measurable ACTH and cortisol elevations comparable in magnitude to hCRH administration. Prolactin elevated but lower than TRH response. Age-related attenuation of GH response observed. Small acute pharmacodynamic study.

dose_references.context: Five-day SC subcutaneous daily dosing in healthy young men - response attenuation and IGF-1 assessment

citation.title: A five day treatment with daily subcutaneous injections of GHRP-2 causes response attenuation and does not stimulate IGF-I secretion in healthy young men

dose_references.notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS: source is explicitly open-label, uncontrolled, with no placebo group (n=9 healthy men). Peak GH declined from 83 mcg/L (day 1) to 51 mcg/L (day 5) with once-daily 100 mcg SC, demonstrating tachyphylaxis (p<0.01). Serum IGF-1 did NOT increase over the 5-day period despite repeated GH pulses. Osteocalcin rose significantly. Indicates once-daily SC at this dose produces progressive GH attenuation and may be insufficient to durably elevate IGF-1.

dose_references.context: Intranasal GHRP-2 in children with GH deficiency - 48-week RCT (negative growth outcome despite GH stimulation)
dose_references.frequency: Twice daily intranasal, weight-adjusted (under 20 kg: 50-100 mcg; 20 kg or more: 100-200 mcg)

citation.title: Increased Secretion of Endogenous GH after Treatment with an Intranasal GH-releasing Peptide-2 Spray Does Not Promote Growth in Short Children with GH Deficiency
citation.source: PubMed / Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism

dose_references.notes: Genuine double-blind RCT, n=126 prepubertal GH-deficient children, 48 weeks (placebo 44, low-dose 41, high-dose 41). Intranasal GHRP-2 stimulated endogenous GH secretion but did NOT improve height SD scores versus placebo (placebo +0.07 SD, low-dose +0.03 SD, high-dose +0.02 SD; no significant difference). HUMAN_RCT grade confirmed. Clinically important NEGATIVE finding for intranasal route efficacy - the transient GH elevation was insufficient for meaningful growth promotion.

dose_references.context: Retrospective observational series - GH secretagogues (GHRP-2, GHRP-6, Sermorelin) raising IGF-1 in hypogonadal men on testosterone therapy

citation.title: Growth Hormone Secretagogue Treatment in Hypogonadal Men Raises Serum Insulin-Like Growth Factor-1 Levels
citation.source: PubMed / American Journal of Men's Health

dose_references.notes: Retrospective review, n=14 men (mean age 33.2 years) on testosterone therapy. GHRP-2, GHRP-6, and/or sermorelin administered at 100 mcg three times daily SC for mean 134 days. Mean IGF-1 rose from 159.5 to 239.0 ng/mL (p<0.0001). Not an RCT; small sample; mixed compounds; all patients on concurrent testosterone, limiting isolation of GHRP-2 effects. Doses reported verbatim from source and are NOT established therapeutic standards.

dose_references.context: Community/off-label subcutaneous use - no prospective controlled trial data for these specific protocols
dose_references.notes: Body-recomposition and anti-aging communities report subcutaneous self-administration of GHRP-2, typically 2-3 times daily, but these practices are NOT supported by controlled human trials establishing safety or efficacy for those purposes. Numeric figures circulating online are nulled here because no cited human study supports them for body composition or longevity. No established human therapeutic dose exists for off-label use. All such figures are anecdotal and must not be treated as clinical guidance.

synergies.rationale: GHRP-2 and GHRH analogs (CJC-1295, sermorelin) act on distinct receptors - GHRP-2 binds GHS-R1a while GHRH analogs bind the GHRH receptor (GHRHR) - activating separate intracellular signaling cascades (Gq/11-calcium vs. Gs-cAMP/PKA). In a small non-randomized human study, combining a low dose of GHRP-2 (0.1 mcg/kg) with GHRH (1 mcg/kg) produced a greater GH response than either agent alone, consistent with the dual-pathway mechanism. This is educational mechanistic framing describing why the two classes are studied together, not a protocol recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS - the supporting combination data come from a small (n=10) non-randomized study, not a controlled trial. The combination can amplify cortisol, prolactin, and ACTH elevation. Greater GH/IGF-1 elevation raises theoretical risk in active malignancy, uncontrolled diabetes, and proliferative diabetic retinopathy. IGF-1 should be monitored to avoid supraphysiologic elevation. Educational framing only - not a protocol recommendation, and no dose is endorsed here.

synergies.rationale: Ipamorelin is a more receptor-selective GHS-R1a agonist that, unlike GHRP-2, produces minimal ACTH/cortisol elevation at GH-stimulating doses. GHRP-2 and ipamorelin share the same receptor target. The rationale for discussing them together is purely mechanistic - derived from each compound's individually characterized receptor pharmacology. There is no published human trial studying this specific combination.
synergies.caution_notes: Combining two GHS-R1a agonists risks redundant receptor occupancy and accelerated tachyphylaxis without proven additive benefit. Ipamorelin's lower-cortisol advantage may be partially offset when co-administered with GHRP-2. Educational framing only - not a protocol recommendation.

synergies.rationale: As with CJC-1295/sermorelin, the GHS-R1a + GHRHR dual-pathway mechanism applies to the entire GHRH-analog class, which is why GHRP-2 plus a GHRH analog is discussed mechanistically. Tesamorelin is FDA-approved specifically for HIV-associated lipodystrophy. The amplified-GH mechanism is biologically consistent across GHRH analogs combined with a GHS-R1a agonist, but the human combination data are limited and non-randomized. Educational mechanistic framing only.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS - extrapolated from the same small non-randomized GHRP-2 + GHRH human data, generalized across a drug class. Tesamorelin carries its own contraindications (active malignancy, pregnancy). Combining GHRP-2 with any GHRH analog in persons with metabolic syndrome, T2D, or impaired glucose tolerance warrants careful fasting glucose and HbA1c monitoring given amplified GH-mediated insulin resistance. Educational framing only - no protocol or dose is endorsed.

name: GHRP-6 (Growth Hormone-Releasing Peptide-6)
short_description: GHRP-6 is a synthetic hexapeptide GH secretagogue and ghrelin mimetic that stimulates pulsatile growth hormone release via GHS-R1a and CD36 receptor activation. It is NOT FDA-approved; it is a research compound with Phase I human pharmacokinetic data, limited small human mechanistic studies, and extensive preclinical (animal) evidence. No controlled human subcutaneous dose-finding or body-composition trial exists. Clinician supervision required for any use; not for self-administration.
mechanism: GHRP-6 binds two distinct receptor types: (1) GHS-R1a (the ghrelin receptor), a Gq/11-coupled GPCR on pituitary somatotrophs and hypothalamic arcuate nucleus neurons, and (2) the scavenger receptor CD36. At GHS-R1a, binding activates phospholipase C, generating IP3 and DAG, triggering intracellular calcium release and GH exocytosis from pituitary secretory granules. Endogenous hypothalamic GHRH is required for maximal GH response — a human study (n=9) showed that GHRH antagonism reduced the GH area-under-curve response to GHRP-6 by approximately 78% (from 1701 to 376 mcg·min/L), indicating a substantial hypothalamic component to GHRP-6 action. Via GHS-R1a activation in the arcuate nucleus, GHRP-6 activates NPY/AgRP orexigenic neurons, producing pronounced appetite stimulation — a characteristic that distinguishes it from more selective GH secretagogues (e.g., ipamorelin). Via CD36, GHRP-6 has been reported in animal models to activate the PI3K/AKT1 pro-survival pathway, reduce reactive oxygen species, suppress pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6), and exert GH-independent cytoprotective effects across cardiac, hepatic, renal, and pulmonary tissues. These cytoprotective effects are preclinical and have not been confirmed in humans.
monitoring_notes: IGF-1 (serum): GH secretagogue use raises downstream IGF-1; chronically elevated IGF-1 has been associated with proliferative risk in some preclinical and epidemiological contexts. Baseline and periodic monitoring appropriate if any sustained use is contemplated under clinical supervision.

Fasting glucose and HbA1c: GH counter-regulates insulin action. GH elevation plus GHRP-6's pronounced appetite stimulation (orexigenic NPY/AgRP activation) may worsen glycemic control, particularly in pre-diabetes, T2D, or metabolic syndrome.

Cortisol (serum): GHRP-6 activates the HPA axis, producing transient ACTH and cortisol elevation — more pronounced than ipamorelin at equivalent GH-releasing doses (animal comparative data). Relevant in patients on corticosteroids or with adrenal pathology.
Prolactin (serum): Transient prolactin elevation documented with GHRP-6. Relevant in prolactinoma or patients on prolactin-elevating medications.
Blood pressure and heart rate: GHRP-6 has reported inotropic effects via calcium mobilization. Cardiovascular monitoring appropriate in known cardiac disease.
Thyroid function: GH axis activation can affect T4-to-T3 conversion; periodic TSH/free T4 monitoring may be appropriate under clinical supervision.
Body weight and appetite: GHRP-6's ghrelin-mimetic effect causes intense hunger within ~20–30 minutes of administration. Caloric intake and body weight should be tracked as part of any monitored use.
No long-term human safety data: all available human data is from small, short-duration single-dose PK or mechanistic studies. Long-term and chronic-use safety in humans is uncharacterized.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy: elevated GH and IGF-1 signaling is theoretically proliferative; no human safety trials have assessed GHRP-6 in oncology patients. Use in active cancer is contraindicated.

Known or suspected pituitary tumor (other than treated/stable GH-deficient states managed by a specialist): GHRP-6 stimulates pituitary GH release and could theoretically exacerbate tumor growth or complicate diagnosis.
Pregnancy and breastfeeding: no human safety data; reproductive toxicology not established in the public literature.
Not FDA-approved for any indication: GHRP-6 is a research peptide; use outside a formal clinical research context or supervised medical practice is unsupported by regulatory approval.
relative_contraindications: Uncontrolled type 2 diabetes or pre-diabetes: GH-driven insulin resistance combined with intense appetite stimulation may worsen glycemic control.
Significant insulin resistance or metabolic syndrome: same mechanism; requires close glucose monitoring.
History of malignancy (within 5 years or with ongoing cancer surveillance): theoretical IGF-1-driven proliferative risk; risk-benefit assessment required by the treating oncologist.
Obstructive sleep apnea: GH axis activation can worsen fluid retention and potentially exacerbate sleep-disordered breathing.
Untreated hypothyroidism: GH axis stimulation may alter T4-to-T3 conversion; thyroid status should be optimized first.
Concurrent corticosteroid use: GHRP-6-mediated cortisol elevation may compound glucocorticoid effects.
Prolactinoma or hyperprolactinemia: GHRP-6 transiently elevates prolactin.
Significant cardiomyopathy or heart failure: GHRP-6 has reported inotropic effects; use in severe cardiac disease requires specialist oversight.
common_side_effects: Pronounced appetite stimulation / intense hunger: the most distinctive side effect among GH secretagogues, attributable to GHS-R1a-mediated activation of hypothalamic NPY/AgRP orexigenic neurons. Onset within ~20–30 minutes of injection.

Transient cortisol elevation: GHRP-6 activates the HPA axis; transient ACTH and cortisol increases documented. More pronounced than ipamorelin at equivalent GH-releasing doses (animal comparative data).
Transient prolactin elevation: co-stimulation of prolactin alongside GH; documented in the GHRP class.
Water retention / mild peripheral edema: attributable to GH-mediated renal sodium retention.
Injection site reactions: transient redness, swelling, or discomfort (common to subcutaneous peptide injections).
Fatigue or somnolence: reported in association with GH pulse elevation.
Flushing or warmth: reported transiently after administration in some subjects.
serious_adverse_events: No serious adverse events were reported in the published Phase I single-dose IV PK study in 9 healthy male volunteers (100–400 mcg/kg). This study was small, single-dose, and short-term — it does not characterize long-term or chronic-use safety.

Theoretical oncogenic risk via the IGF-1/GH pathway: not documented in clinical GHRP-6 trials but inferred from broader GH axis biology and epidemiological associations of elevated IGF-1 with proliferative risk.

Hypoglycemia risk (indirect): intense appetite stimulation combined with post-GH-peak insulin sensitization may create a reactive-hypoglycemia risk window, particularly in individuals with diabetes or on insulin/sulfonylureas.

Compounding/sourcing regulatory risk: GHRP-6 is not an FDA-approved drug and has been treated as not eligible for compounding by licensed pharmacies; products obtained outside regulated channels carry risks of incorrect dosing, contamination, and unknown excipients.

dose_references.frequency: Single bolus doses across three sessions (100, 200, 400 mcg/kg)

citation.source: PubMed / European Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences, 2013

dose_references.notes: VERIFIED against source: three intravenous bolus doses (100, 200, 400 mcg/kg) administered sequentially in a dose scale-up design, n=9 healthy males. Distribution half-life 7.6 ± 1.9 min; elimination half-life 2.5 ± 1.1 h. Atypical concentration spikes during elimination phase in 4 of 9 subjects. These are single-dose pharmacokinetic/safety characterization doses — NOT therapeutic or body-composition doses, and NOT a subcutaneous route. Do not extrapolate to subcutaneous self-administration.

dose_references.context: GH-stimulation mechanistic human study — GHRP-6 to assess hypothalamic GHRH dependence, n=9 healthy men ages 20–30

citation.title: Growth Hormone (GH)-Releasing Peptide-6 Requires Endogenous Hypothalamic GH-Releasing Hormone for Maximal GH Stimulation

dose_references.notes: VERIFIED against source: 1 mcg/kg IV bolus, n=9, produced a peak GH increase of ~34 mcg/L (33.8 ± 4.8) in saline controls; GHRH antagonist pretreatment reduced peak to 6.2 mcg/L and AUC from 1701 to 376 mcg·min/L (~78% reduction). Mechanistic characterization dose only — not a therapeutic target and not a subcutaneous route.

dose_references.context: Topical wound-healing concentration in animal models (rat and rabbit) — no human topical data
dose_references.frequency: Twice daily (rats, 4–5 days); once daily (rabbits, 30 days)

citation.title: Growth Hormone-Releasing Peptide 6 Enhances the Healing Process and Improves the Esthetic Outcome of the Wounds

dose_references.notes: Numeric dose nulled per audit rule: the source reports a topical FORMULATION concentration (~400 mcg/mL, ~100 mcg per wound in rabbits), which the original entry mislabeled as a 400 mcg/kg body-weight dose — these are not interchangeable, so no validated per-weight dose is carried. Animal-only data: 400 mcg/mL accelerated wound closure and reduced hypertrophic scarring; 200 mcg/mL failed to prevent scarring; 800 mcg/mL delayed healing. No human topical data exists. Not a human dose.

dose_references.context: Subcutaneous self-administration — community and off-label anecdotal use; NO published controlled human SC dose-finding trial exists
dose_references.notes: No established human therapeutic subcutaneous dose exists in the peer-reviewed literature. Figures circulating in fitness and peptide communities (e.g., 100–300 mcg per injection) are entirely anecdotal and carry no controlled-trial support. These numbers must NOT be presented as validated dosing. Subcutaneous bioavailability, safety, and optimal dosing for GHRP-6 in humans have not been formally studied. Educational tracking only.

synergies.with_name: GHRH analogs (e.g., Sermorelin, CJC-1295, Modified GRF 1-29)
synergies.rationale: GHRH analogs act on the GHRH receptor (GHRHR) via cAMP/PKA signaling; GHRP-6 acts on GHS-R1a via Gq/IP3/Ca2+ signaling — pharmacologically distinct, complementary pathways converging on pituitary somatotrophs. A human mechanistic study (controls vs. patients with hypothalamopituitary disconnection; GHRH 100 mcg IV + GHRP-6 90 mcg IV) observed a synergistic GH response in controls that disappeared entirely in disconnected patients, indicating the synergy depends on intact hypothalamic circuitry. This is an educational description of a documented physiological interaction, not a protocol or dosing recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: Synergistic GH elevation also amplifies downstream IGF-1, cortisol, prolactin, and glucose counter-regulatory effects. Any monitored use would warrant tracking of IGF-1, fasting glucose, and HbA1c. Avoid in active malignancy, pituitary tumors, uncontrolled diabetes, or significant insulin resistance. Educational context only — not a protocol recommendation and not a dose.

synergies.rationale: Both compounds are GHS-R1a agonists (ghrelin mimetics); combining them is pharmacologically redundant at the receptor level and does NOT reproduce the complementary-pathway synergy seen with GHRH + GHRP-6. Ipamorelin is more GH-selective than GHRP-6, producing less cortisol and ACTH co-stimulation at equivalent GH-releasing doses in animal studies; the mechanistic note sometimes cited is that ipamorelin may partially blunt GHRP-6's off-target hormonal activation. Strictly a mechanistic/educational observation.
synergies.caution_notes: No controlled human study supports this combination. Redundant GHS-R1a agonism does not produce GH synergy by the same mechanism as GHRH + GHRP-6, and stacking two ghrelin mimetics offers no established benefit. Both compounds share the same contraindication profile. Mechanistic/educational note only — not a recommendation.

name: Hexarelin
short_description: Hexarelin (INN: examorelin) is a synthetic hexapeptide GH-releasing peptide (GHRP) and potent agonist of the ghrelin/GHS-R1a receptor. It is among the most potent acute GH releasers in the GHRP class in humans. It also binds the cardiac scavenger receptor CD36, conferring GH-independent cardiotropic effects in human pharmacodynamic studies. Hexarelin reached early-phase clinical investigation for GH deficiency and heart failure but was never approved or marketed in any country. It is NOT FDA-approved for any indication and is treated as a research compound only. It is a WADA-prohibited substance. No controlled human trial has established a safe or effective dose for fat loss, muscle gain, body recomposition, or longevity.
mechanism: Hexarelin acts via two distinct receptor pathways. (1) GHS-R1a (ghrelin receptor): It binds and activates GHS-R1a receptors in anterior pituitary somatotrophs and the hypothalamus, triggering dose-dependent GH release via phospholipase C / IP3 / intracellular calcium signaling — a mechanism complementary to (and synergistic with) the GHRH/cAMP/PKA pathway. It also appears to suppress hypothalamic somatostatin tone, amplifying the net GH pulse. GH in turn stimulates hepatic IGF-1 production. (2) CD36: In cardiac tissue, hexarelin binds the scavenger receptor CD36 to exert direct cardiotropic effects that are independent of GH secretion, as observed in GH-deficient subjects in human studies. Hexarelin does not share sequence homology with ghrelin but functionally mimics it at GHS-R1a. At studied doses it did not measurably affect plasma glucose, LH, FSH, or TSH.
monitoring_notes: Serum IGF-1: obtain baseline before any use and monitor periodically; elevated IGF-1 is the primary biomarker of GH-axis over-stimulation and carries mitogenic concern at chronically supraphysiologic levels
Fasting glucose and HbA1c: GH is counter-regulatory to insulin and GH secretagogues can worsen insulin sensitivity (PMID 28400207); particularly important in pre-diabetic or T2D individuals

Cortisol (morning serum or 24h urinary free cortisol): hexarelin elevates ACTH/cortisol acutely at higher IV doses (PMID 8954038, PMID 15177700); the 16-week chronic SC study did not report clinically significant sustained cortisol elevation, but HPA over-stimulation is a concern with unsupervised high-dose use
Prolactin: dose-dependent prolactin elevation documented in human studies; sustained elevation can suppress the gonadal axis
GH response attenuation / desensitization: partial reversible tachyphylaxis documented at 1.5 mcg/kg SC BID over 16 weeks, fully recovering within ~4 weeks of stopping (PMID JCEM 1998)
Carpal tunnel symptoms and peripheral edema: classical GH/IGF-1-excess effects from sodium retention and soft-tissue changes
WADA prohibited-substance status (category S2): relevant for any athlete subject to anti-doping testing
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy of any type: GH and IGF-1 are mitogenic; hexarelin has not been studied in oncology patients and may theoretically promote tumor growth
Pregnancy and breastfeeding: no human safety data; GH-axis manipulation poses unknown fetal/neonatal risk
Pediatric use / open epiphyseal growth plates: unsupervised GH-axis stimulation in children or adolescents is contraindicated; premature epiphyseal closure is a known GH-excess risk
Known hypersensitivity to hexarelin or any formulation excipient
relative_contraindications: Uncontrolled type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance: GH is counter-regulatory to insulin and hexarelin-driven GH pulses can worsen glycemic control; only under endocrine supervision with glucose monitoring
History of cancer or high genetic cancer risk (e.g., BRCA1/2, MEN1, Li-Fraumeni): chronically elevated IGF-1 warrants individualized risk-benefit discussion with oncology and endocrinology
Acromegaly or pre-existing GH excess: hexarelin produces large GH responses (PMID 7588427); further GH-axis stimulation is inappropriate
Untreated hypothyroidism: can blunt GH response and confound monitoring; optimize thyroid status first
Obstructive sleep apnea: GH/IGF-1 elevation can worsen upper-airway soft-tissue hypertrophy
Competitive athletes subject to anti-doping testing: WADA-prohibited under S2; doping-violation risk
common_side_effects: Flushing and warmth at or after injection (reported with IV bolus in clinical studies)
Increased appetite / hunger (GHS-R1a activation mimics ghrelin's orexigenic signaling)
Transient water retention / mild peripheral edema (GH-driven renal sodium retention and IGF-1 effects)
Tingling or paresthesias in extremities (may represent early carpal tunnel symptoms with sustained use)
Headache (reported across the GHRP class)
Drowsiness or fatigue, particularly when dosed near sleep
Mild dose-dependent cortisol and prolactin elevation at higher IV doses (PMID 8954038); mitigated at lower doses or in GHRH combinations
serious_adverse_events: GH-axis over-stimulation with chronically supraphysiologic IGF-1: long-term GH excess can cause acromegalic features, carpal tunnel syndrome, organomegaly, and cardiac hypertrophy — no human safety data exist for hexarelin beyond ~16-week studies
Worsening insulin resistance and impaired glucose tolerance: GH is diabetogenic; significant concern in metabolic syndrome, pre-diabetes, and T2D (PMID 28400207)
Cortisol/HPA dysregulation with high-dose or chronic use: acute ACTH/cortisol elevation documented (PMID 15177700)
Potential tumor promotion: theoretical serious risk in occult or dormant malignancy; no human oncology safety data
WADA anti-doping violation: use in competitive sport can carry career-ending consequences
Unknown long-term cardiovascular effects of chronic CD36 stimulation in healthy individuals

Small acute pharmacodynamic dose-response study in healthy male volunteers; randomized design but single-dose mechanistic endpoints only, so graded HUMAN_OBS (downgraded from over-stated HUMAN_RCT). At ~0.5 mcg/kg IV, GH approached a maximal plateau; cortisol and prolactin rose dose-dependently. Low-dose combination (0.125 mcg/kg hexarelin + 1 mcg/kg GHRH) reduced the cortisol/prolactin effect. These are acute hormone-release findings, NOT a validated therapeutic or body-recomposition dose.

citation.title: Hexarelin decreases slow-wave sleep and stimulates the secretion of GH, ACTH, cortisol and prolactin during sleep in healthy volunteers

dose_references.notes: Verified citation (4 x 50 mcg SC over one night, N=7 healthy young men). Increased GH and prolactin through the night and ACTH/cortisol in the first half; significantly decreased slow-wave (stage 4) sleep and EEG delta power. This documents a sleep/HPA adverse-effect signal, NOT a recommended dosing schedule.

dose_references.context: Community/anecdotal use context — no controlled human trial establishing safety or efficacy for body recomposition, fat loss, muscle gain, or longevity
dose_references.notes: No established human therapeutic dose exists for body recomposition, fat loss, muscle gain, or longevity. Circulating community dose ranges are not derived from controlled trials, are unverified, and must not be treated as validated or as a recommendation. Ranges intentionally left null.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanism: GHRH analogs and GHRPs such as hexarelin act on complementary intracellular pathways in pituitary somatotrophs. GHRH activates Gs-coupled adenylyl cyclase (cAMP/PKA) and primes GH gene transcription, while hexarelin activates GHS-R1a (phospholipase C / IP3 / calcium) and appears to lower somatostatin tone. A human pharmacodynamic study (PMID 8954038) observed that a low dose of hexarelin combined with GHRH produced a larger GH response than either alone while blunting the cortisol elevation seen with higher-dose hexarelin. This explains why GHRH + GHRP combinations are mechanistically of interest; it is not a protocol or an endorsement to combine them.
synergies.caution_notes: Combining two GH-axis stimulants amplifies GH and IGF-1 exposure and the associated mitogenic and glucose-handling risks; tachyphylaxis to hexarelin's GHS-R1a component still applies. This is educational context only, not a recommendation. Any such combination is unproven for body composition, is WADA-prohibited in sport, and would require evaluation and monitoring (including IGF-1 and glucose) by a qualified clinician. Avoid in active malignancy, uncontrolled diabetes, pregnancy/lactation, and pediatric/adolescent use.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanism only: Ipamorelin is a more selective GHRP that, unlike hexarelin, tends not to elevate cortisol or prolactin appreciably at studied doses. Because both act at the same GHS-R1a receptor and intracellular pathway, same-class stacking offers diminishing additive GH-release return compared with cross-class (GHRH + GHRP) combinations and may accelerate receptor desensitization. This entry is mechanistic rationale, not evidence that the pair is safe or effective.
synergies.caution_notes: No controlled human trial tests hexarelin + ipamorelin co-administration. Stacking two GHS-R1a agonists may speed tachyphylaxis and offers little synergy versus a GHRH + GHRP pairing. Cortisol, prolactin, IGF-1, and glucose monitoring would be warranted. Educational context only — not a protocol or dosing recommendation; discuss any use with a qualified clinician.

name: MK-677 (Ibutamoren)
short_description: MK-677 (ibutamoren) is an orally active, non-peptide agonist of the ghrelin receptor (GHSR-1a) that stimulates pulsatile growth hormone secretion and sustains elevated IGF-1 levels for approximately 24 hours following a single dose. It is not FDA-approved, remains an investigational compound originally developed by Merck, and is banned by WADA and prohibited by the US Department of Defense. Multiple Phase 2 RCTs evaluated it in elderly adults, hip fracture patients, and Alzheimer's disease, but development was discontinued after a safety signal of congestive heart failure and an absence of clear efficacy benefit in most indications. Educational/tracking content only; not a treatment recommendation.
mechanism: MK-677 acts as a potent, selective, non-peptide agonist of the ghrelin receptor (GHSR-1a) with reported sub-nanomolar potency. By mimicking endogenous ghrelin, it stimulates somatotroph cells in the anterior pituitary to release growth hormone in amplified pulses, while also reducing somatostatin tone. This leads to downstream elevation of hepatic and peripheral IGF-1 production. Unlike exogenous GH administration, MK-677 largely preserves the pulsatile nature of GH secretion. Elevated GH and IGF-1 promote nitrogen retention, lean-mass accretion, bone remodeling, and changes in REM/slow-wave sleep architecture. The same GH elevation drives the key adverse effects: insulin resistance, fluid retention, and a theoretical tumor-growth promotion concern via IGF-1 signaling. The compound is orally bioavailable with a half-life sufficient to maintain 24-hour IGF-1 elevation with once-daily dosing.
monitoring_notes: Serum IGF-1: baseline and periodic measurement; supraphysiologic IGF-1 is a theoretical tumor-promotion concern and a marker of GH excess

Fasting glucose and HbA1c: GH elevation drives insulin resistance; Chapman 1996 documented a fasting glucose rise (~5.4 to ~6.8 mmol/L) within 4 weeks at 25 mg/day; prediabetes/T2D individuals are at heightened risk
Fasting insulin and HOMA-IR: to assess baseline and on-treatment insulin resistance
Blood pressure and fluid status: water retention and peripheral edema are reported class effects
Cardiovascular assessment: the Adunsky 2011 hip-fracture trial was stopped early for a CHF signal (6.5% vs 1.7%); particular caution with pre-existing cardiac disease or reduced ejection fraction
Cortisol: monitor where adrenal or hypothalamic-pituitary conditions are present
Prolactin: modest elevations reported, generally within normal range; monitor if symptoms of hyperprolactinemia arise
Lipid panel: GH-axis modulation can affect lipoprotein profiles; baseline and periodic monitoring
This is NOT an FDA-approved compound; all use outside an enrolled clinical trial is unapproved and lacks regulatory oversight. Discuss with a qualified clinician.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy or history of hormone-sensitive cancer: IGF-1 is a mitogen and supraphysiologic IGF-1 poses a theoretical tumor-promotion risk; a standard exclusion criterion across published MK-677 trials
Clinically significant congestive heart failure or reduced ejection fraction: the Phase IIb hip-fracture trial (Adunsky 2011) was terminated early for excess CHF incidence (6.5% vs 1.7%)
Pregnancy and lactation: no safety data; GH-axis stimulation during pregnancy carries unknown fetal risk
relative_contraindications: Type 2 diabetes or impaired fasting glucose: MK-677 consistently worsens insulin sensitivity and raises fasting glucose across RCTs; a confounded case report (combined with SARMs) documented severe hyperglycemia (Sotornik et al., Clinical Diabetes 2022)
Prediabetes or metabolic syndrome: increased risk of progression to overt diabetes via the same mechanism
History of cardiac disease, cardiomyopathy, or fluid-overload states: additive fluid-retention risk magnified by the trial CHF signal
Acromegaly or history of GH excess: MK-677 substantially elevates GH/IGF-1 and is inappropriate where GH is already oversecreted
Pediatric use without specialist supervision: growth-plate and long-term hormonal effects are not characterized
Concurrent insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents: GH-mediated insulin resistance may require medication adjustment under clinician supervision
Concurrent corticosteroid therapy: additive fluid retention and opposing metabolic effects
common_side_effects: Increased appetite/hyperphagia (most frequent adverse effect in Nass 2008)
Peripheral edema, especially lower extremity (transient, multiple RCTs)
Muscle pain/myalgias (Nass 2008)
Fatigue
Elevated fasting glucose and worsened insulin sensitivity (Chapman 1996, Nass 2008)
Mild prolactin elevation (within normal range in published trials)
Cortisol elevation (reported in some studies)
Joint pain or morning stiffness (GH-class effect)
Transient nausea
serious_adverse_events: Congestive heart failure: 6.5% vs 1.7% placebo in the Adunsky 2011 Phase IIb hip-fracture RCT; led to early termination

New-onset/severe hyperglycemia: documented in a published case report of a recreational bodybuilder (note: confounded by concurrent SARM use — RAD140 + andarine), with capillary glucose ~558 mg/dL and HbA1c 13.9%, partially reversible on cessation (Sotornik et al., Clinical Diabetes 2022)
Theoretical promotion of growth of pre-existing malignancies via sustained IGF-1 elevation (no direct human RCT evidence; all trials excluded active cancer)
Potential exacerbation of obstructive sleep apnea — a recognized GH-axis class effect

Most frequent adverse effects: increased appetite, transient lower-extremity edema, muscle pain. Worsened insulin sensitivity and increased fasting glucose also reported. Dose retained as cited human RCT data.

At 25 mg/day mean 24-hour GH increased substantially and IGF-1 was restored toward young-adult levels; fasting glucose rose (~5.4 to ~6.8 mmol/L) by 4 weeks; prolactin rose modestly within normal range; cortisol unchanged. Evidence level downgraded HUMAN_RCT -> HUMAN_OBS given small-n dose-ranging design. Dose range retained as cited human data.

dose_references.context: Bone turnover in elderly adults — pooled analysis of three RCTs

citation.title: Oral Administration of the Growth Hormone Secretagogue MK-677 Increases Markers of Bone Turnover in Healthy and Functionally Impaired Elderly Adults
citation.source: Journal of Bone and Mineral Research, 1999, 14(7):1182-1188, PMID 10404019

dose_references.notes: Murphy et al. 1999 (J Bone Miner Res, n=187 elderly adults, pooled across 3 RCTs): MK-677 10 and 25 mg increased markers of both bone resorption (urinary NTX) and bone formation (osteocalcin) plus IGF-1, indicating increased bone turnover rather than a clearly net-anabolic effect in this timeframe. Adequately-sized pooled RCT analysis; HUMAN_RCT retained. Dose range retained as cited human data.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanism note: MK-677 activates the ghrelin receptor (GHSR-1a) to trigger GH release, while CJC-1295 acts on a distinct upstream receptor (GHRH-R) on pituitary somatotrophs. These are pharmacologically complementary pathways within the GH axis, and physiology suggests additive GH output when both are engaged. No published human RCT has tested this specific combination.
synergies.caution_notes: Combining two GH-axis stimulants would be expected to amplify all class-level risks: greater insulin resistance, more fluid retention, higher IGF-1 elevation, and added cardiovascular fluid load. No long-term human safety data exist for this combination. Educational context only — not a protocol or dosing recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanism note: ipamorelin is a selective GH-releasing peptide that also acts at GHSR-1a with minimal cortisol/prolactin effect. Because it shares MK-677's receptor target, additive receptor engagement is pharmacologically plausible but may also produce diminishing returns through receptor saturation. No cited human study evaluates this pairing.
synergies.caution_notes: Two GHSR agonists together may yield limited incremental GH gain while compounding insulin resistance and edema. No published human RCT evaluates this combination. Educational context only — not a protocol or dosing recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanism note: tesamorelin is an FDA-approved GHRH analog (approved only for HIV-associated lipodystrophy) acting via the GHRH receptor — a pathway distinct from MK-677's ghrelin receptor. Co-activation of GHRH and ghrelin pathways is the physiological basis for synergistic GH pulse amplification. No published human study has evaluated tesamorelin combined with MK-677; prior prescriptive 'has been studied for this combination' framing was removed.
synergies.caution_notes: Tesamorelin is FDA-approved only for HIV-associated lipodystrophy; any other use is off-label. Combining it with MK-677 (an unapproved compound) compounds regulatory, metabolic, and cardiovascular risk — particularly glucose dysregulation and fluid overload. Educational context only — not a protocol or dosing recommendation.

name: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 Insulin-Like Growth Factor-1)
short_description: IGF-1 LR3 is a synthetic 83-amino-acid analog of endogenous IGF-1, engineered to have markedly reduced binding affinity for IGF-binding proteins (IGFBPs) and a substantially extended half-life. It is NOT FDA-approved for human use. Its primary evidence base consists of in-vitro cell-culture studies and animal models; no controlled human clinical trials have been conducted or registered. It is classified as a prohibited substance by WADA under Section S2 of the Prohibited List.
mechanism: IGF-1 LR3 acts as a full agonist at the IGF-1 receptor (IGF-1R), a receptor tyrosine kinase. Receptor activation leads to autophosphorylation and recruitment of insulin receptor substrate proteins (IRS-1/2), triggering two major downstream cascades: (1) PI3K/Akt/mTOR — driving ribosomal protein synthesis, suppressing FoxO-mediated E3 ubiquitin ligase transcription (anti-atrophy), and inhibiting GSK-3β; and (2) MAPK/ERK — stimulating cell proliferation and satellite cell activation. The LR3 modification (arginine substitution at position 3 plus a 13-residue N-terminal extension) reduces IGFBP binding versus native IGF-1, increasing the fraction of free, biologically active peptide and extending the functional half-life relative to native IGF-1 (reported elimination half-life estimates vary widely across non-clinical sources and have not been established in controlled human pharmacokinetic studies). It also cross-signals through the insulin receptor at high concentrations, producing insulin-mimetic glucose-lowering effects via PI3K-dependent glucose uptake in skeletal muscle and suppression of hepatic gluconeogenesis.
monitoring_notes: Blood glucose monitoring is essential given the insulin-mimetic glucose-lowering mechanism; the FDA-approved analog mecasermin (Increlex) caused hypoglycemia in a large fraction of clinical-trial participants, including hypoglycemic seizures (per Increlex FDA label)
Monitor for signs of intracranial hypertension: headache, visual changes, nausea/vomiting — funduscopic examination is advised with any sustained IGF-1-class exposure (per Increlex FDA label)
Monitor for lymphoid tissue hypertrophy: tonsillar/adenoidal enlargement with potential sleep apnea (documented in mecasermin trials)
Assess for acromegalic tissue changes with prolonged exposure: jaw, hand/foot size, organ enlargement (cardiac, hepatic, renal hypertrophy)

Baseline and periodic serum IGF-1 levels if monitoring the GH/IGF axis (supraphysiologic IGF-1 is associated with increased risk of prostate, colorectal, and premenopausal breast cancer in epidemiological meta-analyses)
Lipid panel and fasting glucose/HbA1c: chronic IGF-1R stimulation can affect insulin signaling and glucose homeostasis
Evaluate any active malignancy or personal/family cancer history before any IGF-1 axis manipulation — IGF-1R supports anchorage-independent tumor growth and metastatic spread
NOT FDA-approved; not evaluated in registered human clinical trials; WADA-prohibited for athletes. Any clinical monitoring framework should be developed by a qualified physician.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy or history of malignancy — IGF-1R signaling supports tumor anchorage-independent growth, metastatic invasion, and resistance to cancer therapies; per FDA-approved IGF-1 analog (mecasermin) labeling
Known hypersensitivity to IGF-1-class peptides (per mecasermin FDA label; anaphylaxis documented post-marketing)
Closed epiphyses in pediatric/adolescent patients (per mecasermin FDA label — irreversible skeletal consequences)
Pregnancy or breastfeeding — no safety data; potent mitogenic growth-factor signaling poses unknown fetal/neonatal risk
Concurrent exogenous insulin or insulin secretagogue use — high risk of severe, potentially fatal hypoglycemia
relative_contraindications: Diabetes mellitus or impaired glucose regulation — additive hypoglycemia risk with insulin and oral hypoglycemics; prolonged glucose-lowering duration
Pre-existing or suspected intracranial hypertension — GH/IGF axis manipulation is a known precipitant
Obstructive sleep apnea or tonsillar hypertrophy — IGF-1-class exposure may worsen lymphoid tissue hypertrophy
Acromegaly or gigantism history — additional IGF-1R stimulation contraindicated
Strong family history of prostate, breast, or colorectal cancer — meta-analyses link elevated IGF-1 to increased risk of these malignancies
Scoliosis — rapid soft tissue growth may accelerate progression
Cardiovascular disease, cardiomegaly, or ventricular hypertrophy — IGF-1 promotes cardiac and smooth muscle growth
common_side_effects: Hypoglycemia (most common and clinically significant — insulin-mimetic mechanism via IGF-1R and partial insulin-receptor cross-reactivity)
Injection site reactions: local swelling, lipohypertrophy, bruising
Headache (may signal intracranial hypertension)
Peripheral edema (renal sodium and water retention via IGF-1 signaling)
Fatigue and lightheadedness (secondary to hypoglycemia)
Arthralgias and myalgias (connective tissue and musculoskeletal growth effects)
serious_adverse_events: Severe hypoglycemia including hypoglycemic seizures and loss of consciousness — documented with FDA-approved native IGF-1 (mecasermin); the extended duration of IGF-1 LR3 may prolong and worsen episodes
Anaphylaxis / severe hypersensitivity reactions requiring hospitalization — reported post-marketing with mecasermin

Malignant neoplasia — IGF-1R is required for anchorage-independent tumor growth; epidemiological data associate chronically elevated IGF-1 with increased risk of prostate, colorectal, and premenopausal breast cancers
Intracranial hypertension — documented with GH/IGF-1 axis compounds; can cause permanent vision changes
Acromegalic tissue changes with prolonged supraphysiologic exposure: jaw enlargement, organomegaly (cardiac, hepatic, renal), peripheral neuropathy — may be irreversible
Cardiac hypertrophy and arrhythmias with chronic supraphysiologic IGF-1 signaling (mechanistic; documented with GH excess/acromegaly states)
Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (risk in skeletally immature individuals per mecasermin FDA label)

This is a harm-threshold caution, not a dosing recommendation. No safe or effective human dose has been established in controlled trials.

dose_references.context: Community off-label use — no controlled human trial data exists

IGF-1 LR3 has never been evaluated in a registered human clinical trial for any indication. Vendor sites and forum posts are not authoritative sources. No citation is provided because no legitimate human-dosing source exists; number is therefore null.

synergies.with_name: Growth Hormone (GH) / GH Secretagogues (e.g., CJC-1295, Ipamorelin, GHRP-2)
synergies.rationale: GH acts upstream of IGF-1 in the somatomedin axis: GH binds pituitary/hepatic GH receptors, stimulating hepatic synthesis and secretion of endogenous IGF-1. Exogenous IGF-1 LR3 acts downstream at the IGF-1 receptor, bypassing this regulatory step, so the two target different nodes of the same growth axis. Exogenous IGF-1 also exerts negative feedback on GH secretion (suppressing GHRH and stimulating somatostatin), which may attenuate a GH secretagogue's effect. This is a mechanistic/pharmacological observation only — no controlled human trial has evaluated this combination, and it is not presented as a protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: Additive hypoglycemia risk. Additive potential for acromegalic tissue changes (jaw, organ enlargement). Combined IGF-1R and GHR stimulation may amplify mitogenic/cancer-promotion risk. Not a recommended or proven combination; framed here as pharmacological context only. Both agents are unapproved for this use.

synergies.rationale: MGF (IGF-1 Ec) is an alternative splice variant of the IGF-1 gene upregulated in skeletal muscle after mechanical loading. Peer-reviewed work characterizes MGF as stimulating satellite cell proliferation while suppressing premature differentiation via pathways that are not primarily IGF-1R/Akt-dependent (more ERK-associated), whereas IGF-1 LR3 signals through IGF-1R/PI3K/Akt/mTOR. Any complementarity is a mechanistic inference from isoform biology, not a demonstrated synergy.
synergies.caution_notes: No controlled human trial has evaluated this combination. Because MGF can inhibit differentiation, simultaneous administration may be antagonistic rather than synergistic. Both peptides are unapproved for human use. Educational context only.

synergies.rationale: IGF-1 and insulin share structural homology and overlapping receptor signaling (PI3K/Akt for glucose uptake and anti-catabolism), and insulin/IGF-1 receptor crosstalk is well documented in cell-culture and animal literature. This receptor-level overlap is the basis for the documented additive glucose-lowering hazard described below; it is presented here strictly as a pharmacological/safety warning, not as a combination to use.
synergies.caution_notes: EXTREME RISK — DO NOT COMBINE. IGF-1 LR3 plus exogenous insulin carries a high risk of severe, potentially fatal hypoglycemia. IGF-1 LR3 alone already lowers glucose via IGF-1R and partial insulin-receptor cross-reactivity; the combination can produce rapid, profound, and prolonged hypoglycemia. Listed solely as a safety hazard for educational context. FDA-approved mecasermin (native IGF-1) labeling explicitly requires glucose monitoring due to severe hypoglycemic-seizure risk even as monotherapy.

name: Follistatin (FST-344 / FST-288)
short_description: Follistatin is an endogenous glycoprotein that neutralizes myostatin (GDF-8), activin A/B, and GDF-11 — members of the TGF-beta superfamily that suppress skeletal muscle growth. Follistatin-based approaches are being studied in gene therapy and engineered fusion-protein trials for muscular dystrophy and muscle-wasting diseases; no FDA-approved therapeutic form exists. Exogenous native recombinant protein injection (the form circulated in community/research-grade contexts) lacks any published human clinical trial data and is not approved for any use. The only human data come from distinct molecules — AAV-delivered follistatin gene therapy and the ACE-083 follistatin-Fc fusion protein — whose dosing cannot be extrapolated to native follistatin injection.
mechanism: Follistatin binds with high affinity to myostatin (GDF-8), activin A, activin B, and GDF-11, forming stable complexes that prevent these ligands from engaging their receptors (primarily ActRIIB/ACVR2B and ALK4/ALK5). By sequestering these inhibitory TGF-beta family members, follistatin removes a key brake on skeletal muscle growth: satellite cell proliferation increases, Smad2/3 inhibitory signaling declines, and muscle fiber hypertrophy proceeds predominantly through the IGF-IR/Akt/mTOR anabolic axis. The FST-344 gene construct produces the FS-315 circulating isoform (after C-terminal cleavage), which has low heparin-binding affinity and circulates systemically. The shorter FST-288 isoform binds heparan sulfate proteoglycans avidly, concentrates in reproductive tissues, and has approximately 10-fold higher activin affinity — raising greater concern for reproductive off-target effects. Follistatin also inhibits GDF-11, a pro-osteogenic factor; this dual inhibition of myostatin and GDF-11 can produce muscle hypertrophy accompanied by impaired bone formation and fracture risk in animal models.
monitoring_notes: Bone mineral density (DEXA): follistatin inhibits GDF-11, a pro-osteogenic TGF-beta family member. Transgenic follistatin-overexpressing mice showed reduced BMD, increased cortical porosity, and spontaneous tibial fractures (PNAS 2020, PMC7060712). Bone-health monitoring is warranted in any research context.

Serum FSH and reproductive hormones: follistatin was originally isolated as an FSH-suppressing protein via activin neutralization; FST-288 concentrates in reproductive tissue. Monitor FSH, LH, testosterone/estradiol, and menstrual-cycle regularity in any experimental context.

Oncologic surveillance: elevated follistatin expression is associated with poorer prognosis across multiple solid tumor types (ovarian, gastric, lung, hepatocellular, breast, melanoma) via activin antagonism, angiogenesis promotion, and BMP-pathway disruption (PubMed 27807065). Any use in individuals with known or suspected malignancy requires extreme caution.
IGF-1 levels: follistatin-induced hypertrophy appears to require intact IGF-IR/Akt/mTOR signaling. Monitor IGF-1 in the context of any co-administration with growth-hormone-axis peptides.

Liver and kidney function: standard biochemistry panels; the BMD gene-therapy Phase 1/2a monitored these without reported abnormalities, but recombinant-protein pharmacokinetics differ and renal/hepatic clearance is relevant.

Immune response: AAV-delivered follistatin can elicit anti-capsid and anti-transgene immune responses; for recombinant-protein injection, anti-follistatin antibodies are a theoretical concern that could diminish efficacy or cause hypersensitivity.

Injection-site assessment: ACE-083 (a follistatin-Fc IM agent) produced injection-site pain in 90% of Phase 1 participants plus muscle twitching/tightness in a minority. Local reactions should be monitored.
absolute_contraindications: Active or prior solid malignancy (especially ovarian, gastric, hepatocellular, breast, lung, melanoma): follistatin is upregulated in multiple tumor types and associated with worse prognosis; exogenous follistatin could theoretically promote tumor growth/metastasis via activin/BMP-pathway antagonism (PubMed 27807065).
Pregnancy and breastfeeding: follistatin is critical to placentation and embryonic development via activin signaling; systemic disruption is contraindicated.
Known AAV capsid immunity or prior AAV gene therapy (gene-therapy form only): pre-existing neutralizing antibodies preclude effective transduction and increase immune-reaction risk.
relative_contraindications: Osteoporosis or low bone density: GDF-11 inhibition by follistatin can impair osteogenesis and was associated with increased fracture risk in animal models (PNAS 2020).
Infertility or active fertility treatment: follistatin suppresses FSH via activin neutralization and could disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis.
Hypogonadism on hormone replacement: reproductive hormone axis may be further disrupted.
Pre-malignant conditions or strong family history of the cancers listed above.
Immunocompromised state: increased risk from potential immune reactions to a non-self recombinant protein or viral vector.
Use of immunosuppressants: could mask an immune reaction to a non-self protein or gene vector.
common_side_effects: Injection-site pain (90% of participants in the ACE-083 fusion-protein IM Phase 1 trial)
Pain in the injected extremity (43% in the ACE-083 Phase 1 trial)
Muscle twitching at injection site (19% in the ACE-083 Phase 1 trial)
Muscle tightness or stiffness (14% in the ACE-083 Phase 1 trial)
Potential shift toward fast-twitch (glycolytic) fiber type — observed in animal models; human functional implications unknown
FSH suppression via activin neutralization — theoretical in humans, based on mechanism
serious_adverse_events: Bone fractures: spontaneous tibial fractures observed in follistatin-overexpressing transgenic mice due to GDF-11 inhibition impairing osteogenesis (PNAS 2020, PMC7060712). Human risk from exogenous administration is unknown but biologically plausible.

Tumor promotion or accelerated cancer progression: follistatin has pro-tumorigenic roles (angiogenesis, metastasis) across multiple solid tumors; systemic elevation is associated with shorter overall survival in ovarian, lung, and gastric cancers (PubMed 27807065).
Reproductive endocrine disruption: FST-288 concentrates in gonadal tissue; systemic overexpression could impair fertility or HPG-axis function.
Immune/anaphylactic reaction: recombinant protein or viral vector could trigger an immune response; anti-follistatin antibody formation is a theoretical risk.

Unknown long-term oncologic safety: no long-term human safety data exist for any form of exogenous follistatin. Absence of observed adverse events in short Phase 1/2a trials (n=6) provides very limited reassurance for chronic use.

citation.title: Locally acting ACE-083 increases muscle volume in healthy volunteers

dose_references.notes: ACE-083 is a modified follistatin-Fc fusion protein — NOT native FST-344 or FST-288 recombinant protein. n=58 postmenopausal women (42 active, 16 placebo); Phase 1, graded HUMAN_OBS. Delivered via EMG-guided IM injection into a single target muscle, producing 8.9–14.5% LOCALIZED muscle-volume increase at peak dose with no significant strength gain. Most common adverse event: injection-site pain (90%); also pain in extremity (43%), muscle twitching (19%), muscle tightness (14%). No serious adverse events. Published in Muscle & Nerve 2018 (PMC5969095). Doses CANNOT be extrapolated to native recombinant follistatin.

dose_references.context: Recombinant FST-288 subcutaneous injection in mice — dose-ranging study of muscle hypertrophy and fat loss (ANIMAL data only)

citation.title: Systemic administration of Follistatin288 increases muscle mass and reduces fat accumulation in mice

dose_references.notes: Daily subcutaneous injection of 10, 30, or 100 mcg/animal in mice. FST-100 group showed ~11–23% increased muscle fiber size; levator ani +34%, gastrocnemius +41%, EDL +29%. Dose-dependent fat-mass reduction and a shift toward fast-twitch fiber type. ANIMAL DATA ONLY — cannot be extrapolated to human therapeutic dosing. Published in PLoS ONE 2013 (PMC3743061).

dose_references.context: AAV1-FS344 intramuscular gene delivery in cynomolgus macaque nonhuman primates (ANIMAL data only)

citation.title: Follistatin gene delivery enhances muscle growth and strength in nonhuman primates

dose_references.notes: Specific vector dose not publicly reported in the abstract; intramuscular injection into quadriceps of cynomolgus macaques produced ~10–15% quadriceps volume increase within 8 weeks. No abnormal organ morphology over 5-month observation; no significant immune response to transgene or capsid reported. Published in Science Translational Medicine 2009 (PMID 20368179). Gene therapy modality — NOT recombinant protein dosing.

dose_references.context: Community/forum-reported recombinant FST-344 subcutaneous or intramuscular injection for muscle building — NO published human trial exists for native follistatin protein injection
dose_references.notes: NO established human therapeutic dose exists for injectable recombinant native follistatin protein. Community sources circulate numeric doses (e.g., 100 mcg/day) with no peer-reviewed backing — these are deliberately NOT listed here as numbers. Recombinant follistatin protein is research-grade only, not approved for human use, and subcutaneous injection of this compound has never been tested in a published human clinical trial. Any dose figures encountered in bodybuilding or peptide community contexts are anecdotal extrapolations from animal data and are not validated or safe.

synergies.with_name: Myostatin inhibitors (e.g., anti-myostatin antibodies, MIF1/MIF2 peptides)
synergies.rationale: Follistatin and direct myostatin inhibitors share the same primary target (GDF-8/myostatin via ActRIIB), but follistatin additionally neutralizes activin A, activin B, and GDF-11 — broader TGF-beta family inhibition. Animal data (transgenic follistatin on a myostatin-null background) showed roughly four-fold greater muscle mass vs. wild-type, exceeding myostatin knockout alone, which is consistent with contribution from non-myostatin ligands (particularly activin). Educational/mechanistic context only.
synergies.caution_notes: Not a protocol or recommendation. Combining broad TGF-beta inhibition could amplify bone-density loss (GDF-11 suppression). No human combination data exists. Theoretical oncogenic risk from dual activin suppression.

synergies.rationale: Mechanistically, follistatin-induced muscle hypertrophy appears to require a functional IGF-IR/Akt/mTOR axis: in hypophysectomized animals, severely reduced IGF-1 blunted the follistatin hypertrophic response and low insulin attenuated it. The pathways are described as complementary — follistatin removes the TGF-beta inhibitory brake while IGF-1/insulin drive the anabolic mTOR arm. Mechanistic animal data (PMC4572457). Educational only.
synergies.caution_notes: Not a recommendation. Exogenous IGF-1 is itself not FDA-approved for muscle building and carries serious risks (hypoglycemia, acromegalic effects, potential tumor promotion). Described mechanistically for education only; no human combination data.

synergies.rationale: Endogenous follistatin rises with resistance exercise and the follistatin-to-myostatin ratio increases with training; exercise independently activates Akt/mTOR. This describes the behavior of ENDOGENOUS follistatin with training, not a tested exogenous combination. A 12-week RCT in elderly adults showed combined aerobic+resistance training significantly raised circulating follistatin vs. controls (PMC11788494).
synergies.caution_notes: Not a recommendation. This refers only to ENDOGENOUS follistatin and exercise; it is NOT evidence that injecting exogenous follistatin plus exercise is safe or beneficial in humans, for which no data exist.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 has been reported to promote angiogenesis (VEGF upregulation) and satellite-cell activation in animal muscle-crush models, mechanistically distinct from follistatin's removal of TGF-beta inhibitory signaling on satellite-cell proliferation. The two are described as addressing different nodes (vascular/connective-tissue healing vs. myostatin-driven growth suppression). Mechanistic inference only — no study has combined them.
synergies.caution_notes: Not a recommendation. No published co-administration data in any species. Both compounds lack FDA approval and human dose-response data. Rationale is speculative mechanistic inference.

name: Thymosin Alpha-1
short_description: Thymosin Alpha-1 is a synthetic 28-amino-acid peptide identical to an endogenous thymic hormone. It is an immunomodulator with comparatively robust human evidence among peptides studied in the body-recomposition/longevity space — evaluated across many clinical trials for viral hepatitis, sepsis, cancer adjunct therapy, and COVID-19. It is approved in roughly 35 countries under the brand name Zadaxin (thymalfasin) but is NOT FDA-approved in the United States. As of late 2024 the FDA proposed that it not be placed on the 503A compounding list, making US compounded access legally uncertain. There is no clinical-trial evidence for body recomposition, fat loss, muscle preservation, or healthy-aging/longevity endpoints; all such use is off-label or research-only. Educational/tracking content only — not a prescription or dosing recommendation.
mechanism: Thymosin Alpha-1 is an endogenous thymic peptide that promotes differentiation of precursor T-cells into mature CD4+ helper and CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells. It acts as a toll-like receptor (TLR)-9 and TLR-2 agonist on myeloid and dendritic cells, upregulating Th1 cytokines including IFN-gamma, IL-2, and IL-12. It can simultaneously reduce pro-inflammatory mediators such as IL-1-beta and TNF-alpha in inflammatory contexts, suggesting bidirectional immunomodulatory activity. In the context of aging, thymic involution reduces endogenous Talpha1 production; exogenous supplementation is hypothesized to partially compensate for immunosenescence by restoring thymic output, enhancing dendritic cell maturation, reducing thymocyte apoptosis via protein kinase C signaling, and increasing IL-7 expression important for thymocyte survival. It has also been reported to exhibit antioxidant properties by neutralizing reactive oxygen species in thymic epithelial cells. The aging/longevity mechanisms are extrapolated from disease-population trials and preclinical data and have not been validated in healthy-aging human endpoints.
monitoring_notes: No mandatory laboratory monitoring was specified in trial protocols for Talpha1 monotherapy in viral hepatitis or vaccine adjuvant settings
In patients with active inflammatory or autoimmune conditions: monitor clinical disease-activity markers before and during use
In patients being evaluated for potential immunosuppressive therapy: document baseline immune status; co-administration with immunosuppressants is relatively contraindicated
Rotate and monitor injection sites for persistent local reactions
Liver function tests and viral load monitoring were standard in hepatitis B/C trials — these are disease-specific, not Talpha1-specific
Confirm US regulatory status at time of use given evolving 503A compounding-list decisions (proposed exclusion as of the December 2024 PCAC meeting)
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to thymosin alpha-1 or any component of the formulation

Organ transplant recipients on active immunosuppressive regimens (Talpha1's immune-stimulating activity risks disrupting graft tolerance; contraindicated unless benefit clearly outweighs risk per Zadaxin prescribing information)
relative_contraindications: Active autoimmune disease with high disease activity (theoretical risk of worsening Th1-driven autoimmunity; net effect in an individual patient is uncertain)
Patients on immunosuppressive medications for any non-transplant indication (risk of pharmacodynamic antagonism)
Pregnancy and lactation (insufficient safety data; not recommended per available prescribing references)
Active hematologic malignancy (role of immune stimulation uncertain; consult oncology before use)
US jurisdiction: FDA proposed exclusion from the 503A compounding list in December 2024; legal access via compounding pharmacy is currently uncertain
common_side_effects: Local injection-site reactions: redness, mild pain, irritation, or discomfort (most commonly reported adverse event across trials)
Transient erythema at injection site
Rare: transient muscle atrophy at injection site (post-marketing reports)
Rare: polyarthralgia with hand edema (reported in fewer than 1% across indications)
Rare: skin rash
serious_adverse_events: No drug-related serious adverse events attributable to Talpha1 were identified in the TESTS phase 3 sepsis trial (n=1,106); serious adverse event rates did not differ from placebo in controlled trials
Theoretical: immune activation in transplant recipients risking graft rejection (contraindicated setting; no trial data in this population)
Theoretical: autoimmune flare in susceptible individuals (not reported in trials but pharmacologically plausible given the Th1-polarizing mechanism)

A large COVID-19 cohort (n=2,282, non-randomized) found an association between Talpha1 use and higher non-recovery rate — interpreted as confounding by indication rather than drug toxicity, but underscores the need for randomized data in novel indications
