dose_references.context: Chronic hepatitis B — pivotal RCT (Hepatology, 1998). Standard therapeutic dose used across multiple chronic hepatitis B trials.

citation.title: Efficacy of thymosin alpha1 in patients with chronic hepatitis B: a randomized, controlled trial
Efficacy of thymosin alpha-1 and interferon alpha in treatment of chronic viral hepatitis B: A randomized controlled study

dose_references.notes: 1.6 mg (approximately 900 mcg/m2) subcutaneous twice weekly is the dose established in published RCTs for chronic hepatitis B. One pivotal RCT (n=98) reported 40.6% complete virological response at 26 weeks vs 9.4% in controls. A second RCT (n=62) comparing Talpha1 vs IFN-alpha reported 48.3% complete response for Talpha1 vs 27.3% for IFN-alpha, with Talpha1 better tolerated. Disease-specific dosing for a treated medical condition; not validated for recomposition or longevity. Educational reference only — not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: Sepsis — Phase 3 RCT (TESTS trial, BMJ, 2025). Largest randomized controlled trial of Talpha1 to date.

citation.title: The efficacy and safety of thymosin alpha1 for sepsis (TESTS): multicentre, double blinded, randomised, placebo controlled, phase 3 trial

dose_references.notes: Exact mg dose per injection is not specified in the BMJ publication (verified against the article: dosing stated only as subcutaneous injection every 12 hours for 7 days). Conducted in 1,106 adults with sepsis at 22 Chinese centers (2016-2020). 28-day all-cause mortality was 23.4% vs 24.1% placebo (HR 0.99, 95% CI 0.77-1.27; p=0.93) — no significant benefit. No safety outcomes differed statistically. Exploratory subgroups suggested possible differential effects in older adults and those with diabetes. Number left null because the per-injection dose is not given in the cited source.

dose_references.context: COVID-19 — observational/retrospective cohort data. Range of doses reported across studies pooled in a meta-analysis.
dose_references.frequency: Daily, every other day, or 3x/week; minimum 3-7 days

citation.title: Thymosin alpha1 use in adult COVID-19 patients: A systematic review and meta-analysis on clinical outcomes
Efficacy of Thymosin Alpha 1 in the Treatment of COVID-19: A Multicenter Cohort Study

dose_references.notes: Meta-analysis of 9 studies (5,352 patients; 7 retrospective cohort + 2 small RCTs) found no statistically significant effect on mortality overall (RR 1.03, 95% CI 0.60-1.75, p=0.92). Signals in severe/critical and over-60 patients were exploratory. A separate large cohort (n=2,282) found Talpha1 use associated with a higher non-recovery rate (OR 1.5), likely confounding by indication. Evidence is inconclusive; no definitive benefit established. Graded HUMAN_OBS because the pooled range is dominated by retrospective cohorts, not adequately powered RCTs. Educational reference only.

dose_references.context: Influenza vaccine adjuvant in elderly men — double-blind placebo-controlled RCT. Dose used alongside trivalent influenza vaccine.
dose_references.frequency: Twice weekly for 8 doses (concurrent with vaccination)

citation.title: Augmentation of influenza antibody response in elderly men by thymosin alpha one. A double-blind placebo-controlled clinical study

dose_references.notes: Corrected unit: the cited study administered 900 mcg/m2 subcutaneously twice weekly for 8 doses (verified against PubMed 2642497), NOT 900 mcg/kg as originally listed. The original mcg/kg label was a unit error producing a misleading per-kg figure. Converted here to the actual absolute administered dose of approximately 1.6 mg at average body surface area (the same standard dose used in hepatitis B trials). Studied in 90 men aged 65-99 (mean 77.3). No toxicity observed; enhanced antibody responses to influenza antigens vs placebo. Indication-specific (immunosenescent vaccine adjuvant); not a recomposition or longevity dose. Educational reference only.

dose_references.context: Longevity / immunosenescence — no established human therapeutic dose in healthy aging populations. Mechanistic and preclinical data only.

citation.title: Aging and Thymosin Alpha-1
citation.source: PMC / International Journal of Molecular Sciences

citation.title: Thymosin alpha 1: A comprehensive review of the literature

dose_references.notes: No published RCT or controlled human trial has evaluated Talpha1 for healthy aging or longevity endpoints. All mechanistic rationale (thymic involution compensation, immunosenescence reversal, antioxidant effects on thymic epithelial cells) is extrapolated from disease-population trials and preclinical mouse data. Human data in elderly individuals is limited to vaccine adjuvant settings, not longevity or recomposition outcomes. No dose can be responsibly cited for this context.

dose_references.context: Off-label / compounding / body-recomposition community use — no trial data for this population or purpose.
dose_references.notes: Community use for immune optimization, recovery, and longevity stacking is entirely anecdotal. No clinical trial has evaluated Talpha1 for body recomposition, fat loss, muscle preservation, or recovery in healthy adults. Numbers circulating in peptide communities (e.g., 1-1.6 mg 2-3x/week for 4-12 weeks) appear extrapolated from hepatitis B dosing without controlled evidence in healthy-aging or athletic populations; they lack peer-reviewed sourcing and are therefore not cited or given numeric values here.

synergies.rationale: Educational rationale only: Talpha1 modulates systemic adaptive immune responses via Th1 polarization and T-cell differentiation, whereas BPC-157 (a gastric pentadecapeptide) is studied mainly for local tissue-level effects (fibroblast stimulation, angiogenesis, anti-inflammatory activity at injury sites). The two act on largely non-overlapping pathways, which is why they are sometimes discussed together. No human clinical trial has tested this combination.
synergies.caution_notes: No combined human safety or efficacy data exists. BPC-157 itself lacks FDA approval and human RCT data. This is a mechanistic discussion point, not a validated or recommended combination. Discuss with a clinician before combining anything.

synergies.rationale: Educational rationale only: Talpha1 and Thymosin Beta-4 are structurally distinct peptides from the same thymosin family with divergent roles — Talpha1 acts on lymphocyte maturation and systemic immune signaling, while Tbeta4/TB-500 is studied for actin regulation, cell migration, and angiogenesis associated with tissue remodeling. Complementary immune-plus-repair effects have been theorized but remain mechanistic speculation. No human trial supports combined use.
synergies.caution_notes: TB-500 (synthetic thymosin beta-4 fragment) is not FDA-approved and lacks human RCT safety/efficacy data. No published clinical trial has evaluated the combination. Mechanistic only; not validated or recommended.

synergies.rationale: Educational rationale: the best-documented co-administration context for Talpha1 is alongside vaccines in immunosenescent or immunocompromised populations. A double-blind placebo-controlled RCT in elderly men (n=90, ages 65-99) reported enhanced antibody responses to influenza vaccine when Talpha1 was co-administered. Its Th1-polarizing and dendritic-cell-activating effects are thought to support antigen presentation and T-cell-dependent antibody responses.
synergies.caution_notes: Evidence is specific to elderly/immunocompromised patients and should not be extrapolated to healthy younger adults. Use remains off-label in the US. Should not be combined with vaccines in individuals on immunosuppressive therapy due to risk of disrupting transplant tolerance or triggering autoimmune flare. Not a treatment recommendation — discuss timing and appropriateness with a clinician. Combination observed as background therapy in trials rather than the direct randomized intervention; graded HUMAN_OBS pending a direct citation.

synergies.rationale: Educational/historical context: multiple RCTs studied Talpha1 combined with IFN-alpha for chronic hepatitis B and C, hypothesizing additive immunostimulation (IFN-alpha direct antiviral activity plus Talpha1-enhanced T-cell-mediated viral clearance). Some trials reported improved sustained response vs either agent alone.
synergies.caution_notes: This combination is specific to chronic viral hepatitis and is largely obsolete given direct-acting antivirals. IFN-alpha carries significant toxicity (flu-like syndrome, fatigue, cytopenias). It has no relevance to body-recomposition or longevity use and is included only for mechanistic/historical completeness, not as a recommendation. Combination observed as background therapy in trials rather than the direct randomized intervention; graded HUMAN_OBS pending a direct citation.

name: ARA-290 (Cibinetide)
short_description: ARA-290 (cibinetide) is a synthetic 11-amino acid peptide derived from the helix B surface of erythropoietin (EPO). It selectively activates the innate repair receptor (IRR) — a heterodimer of the EPO receptor (EPOR) and the beta-common receptor (CD131/betacR) — without stimulating red blood cell production. It has completed Phase 2 RCTs for sarcoidosis-associated small fiber neuropathy and exploratory studies in diabetic neuropathy. NOT FDA-approved (holds FDA Orphan Drug and Fast Track designations for sarcoidosis neuropathic pain, which are not approvals); Araim Pharmaceuticals (its developer) has ceased operations with no active IND or Phase 3 program as of 2026. Educational/tracking entry only — not a recommendation to use.
mechanism: ARA-290 is an engineered 11-residue peptide corresponding to the helix B surface of EPO. It binds selectively to the innate repair receptor (IRR), a heteromeric complex of the erythropoietin receptor (EPOR) and the beta-common receptor (CD131/betacR). The IRR is upregulated on stressed, injured, or inflamed tissue rather than expressed constitutively. IRR activation initiates JAK2/STAT3 and PI3K/Akt cytoprotective cascades, reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines, inhibits apoptosis via Bcl-2 family upregulation, and is associated with peripheral nerve fiber regeneration. Because the peptide lacks the structural elements responsible for EPO's classical erythropoietic signaling (homodimer EPO receptor activation), it produced no measurable increase in hemoglobin, hematocrit, or red blood cell mass in Phase 2 trials. Plasma half-life is approximately 2 minutes, but downstream cytoprotective effects are reported to persist for hours to days through intracellular signaling. In animal aging models, it attenuated cardiac inflammation, preserved left ventricular ejection fraction, and reduced systemic frailty markers (animal data only).
monitoring_notes: ARA-290 is NOT FDA-approved. Any use outside clinical trials is off-label and unsanctioned. Clinician oversight is essential.
Baseline CBC to document pre-treatment hematocrit and hemoglobin. Although ARA-290 showed no erythropoietic effect in Phase 2 trials, this should be verified given EPO receptor involvement.
Baseline and follow-up comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) to assess renal and hepatic function — Phase 2 trials showed no liver enzyme elevations, but long-term data are absent.
Periodic blood pressure monitoring in the absence of long-term human data.
Injection site inspection at each dose — mild injection site reactions reported in some trial subjects.
Symptom diary for neuropathic pain scores, functional capacity, and sensory thresholds to track response, consistent with trial endpoints.
No anti-drug antibodies were detected in Phase 2 trials; this has not been studied in long-term or repeat-cycle use.
Patients with any history of malignancy should have oncology consultation before use — see contraindications.
Long-term safety beyond ~12-16 weeks has not been established in humans. Phase 3 was never completed; Araim Pharmaceuticals has ceased operations with no active IND.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy: ARA-290 activates EPOR/CD131 (IRR) signaling through JAK2/STAT3 and PI3K/Akt pro-survival pathways shared with many cancers, and EPO receptor expression on some tumor cells raises a theoretical risk of promoting tumor cell survival. All Phase 2 trials excluded patients with active cancer. Precautionary absolute exclusion pending long-term oncology safety data.

Pregnancy: No reproductive or developmental safety data in humans; IRR-mediated anti-apoptotic signaling during organogenesis is an uncharacterized teratogenic risk. Excluded from all clinical trials.
Known hypersensitivity to ARA-290, cibinetide, or any component of the formulation.
relative_contraindications: Personal history of malignancy (discuss with oncologist; theoretical proliferative risk via EPOR signaling warrants individualized risk-benefit assessment).
Breastfeeding/lactation: No data on excretion in breast milk or infant safety.
Severe renal impairment: Clearance and downstream effects not characterized in renal-failure populations.
Severe hepatic impairment: Hepatic metabolism not formally studied.
Polycythemia vera or other myeloproliferative conditions: Although no erythropoietic effect was seen in trials, EPO receptor involvement warrants caution.
Concurrent use of erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs): Potential receptor competition or additive signaling not characterized.
common_side_effects: Injection site reactions (mild, transient) — reported at low incidence in Phase 2 trials; some cohorts reported no local pain or irritation
Headache — reported as a mild, spontaneously resolving event in trial populations
Minor gastrointestinal discomfort — reported at low incidence
No anti-drug antibody formation was detected in the controlled Phase 2 trials
serious_adverse_events: No serious adverse events were reported in the Phase 2 RCTs (n=22 pilot, n=38, and n=64 dose-ranging) across doses of 1, 4, and 8 mg SC daily for 28 days.
No hematocrit elevation or thromboembolic events were reported in any clinical trial — a key safety differentiator from native EPO.

IMPORTANT CAVEAT: Total human exposure in controlled trials is small (~100-125 treated subjects) with maximum follow-up of roughly 12-16 weeks. Long-term SAE profile is unknown; Phase 3 was never completed.

Theoretical concern: EPOR/CD131 pathway activation shares mechanisms with pro-survival oncogenic signaling (JAK2/STAT3, PI3K/Akt). No related SAEs were observed in short-term trials, but this represents an unquantified long-term risk.

Design was exploratory/observational rather than a fully powered RCT; graded HUMAN_OBS accordingly. Educational reference; not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.notes: Intraperitoneal injection in rats, 30 or 120 mcg/kg daily; both doses prevented mechanical allodynia. Animal data only — not translatable to human dosing. Listed as animal pharmacology, not a human dose.

dose_references.notes: Aged Fischer 344 x Brown Norway rats. 100 mcg/kg IP three times weekly mitigated age-related LVEF decline and cardiac inflammation. Animal data only — no human longevity dose established. Listed as animal pharmacology, not a human dose.

dose_references.context: Community/off-label use outside clinical trials — no human clinical trial data support any dose or route outside the controlled studies above.
dose_references.notes: ARA-290 is sold by research-chemical vendors and used off-label by some individuals/clinics. No established human therapeutic dose exists outside the 1-8 mg SC daily regimens studied in Phase 2 trials. Any use outside those trials is unsanctioned, lacks regulatory approval, and has unknown long-term safety. Not for human therapeutic use under current FDA/EMA status. Ranges intentionally null (anecdotal, uncited).

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 is studied (largely in animals) for tissue repair via angiogenesis and growth-factor signaling, while ARA-290 targets the innate repair receptor on injured/inflamed tissue to modulate neuroinflammation. The proposed pairing rests on mechanistically distinct but anatomically overlapping repair pathways. Rationale is mechanistic only; no published human combination study exists.
synergies.caution_notes: No clinical trial data support this combination in any species. Additive anti-apoptotic signaling from dual pathways has not been characterized for long-term safety. Educational reference only — not a protocol recommendation.

synergies.rationale: TB-500 is studied for actin regulation, cell migration, and matrix remodeling; ARA-290 modulates IRR-mediated neuroinflammation and anti-apoptotic signaling. The proposed pairing is framed as complementary mechanisms (structural scaffolding vs. inflammatory/apoptotic environment). Rationale is mechanistic only; no published human combination study exists.
synergies.caution_notes: No clinical trial data support this combination. Combined effects on cell survival and migration pathways have not been assessed. Educational reference only — not a protocol recommendation.

synergies.rationale: KPV (a C-terminal tripeptide of alpha-MSH) is studied for NF-kB-mediated anti-inflammatory effects; ARA-290 suppresses tissue inflammation via the IRR/EPOR-betacR axis. The proposed pairing is framed as anti-inflammatory action through distinct receptor systems. Rationale is mechanistic/pharmacologic only; no published human or animal combination study was identified.
synergies.caution_notes: No published combination data in any species. Dual modulation of inflammation via independent pathways has not been characterized for overlapping immunosuppression or toxicity. Educational reference only — not a protocol recommendation.

name: LL-37 (Cathelicidin)
short_description: LL-37 is the only human cathelicidin — a 37-amino acid cationic host-defense peptide derived from the precursor hCAP-18. It is expressed endogenously by neutrophils, epithelial cells, macrophages, and keratinocytes in response to infection or injury. Research interest spans antimicrobial activity, wound healing, immunomodulation, and anti-biofilm effects. NOT FDA-approved for any indication. The FDA has placed cathelicidin (LL-37) on its 503A Category 2 bulk-drug list (substances with significant safety concerns for compounding). The most clinically advanced application is topical wound healing (trade name ropocamptide, Promore Pharma), which has completed a Phase IIb RCT but is not approved. Only the topical wound-healing context has human dose data; no systemic (subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous) dose is established, and injectable wellness/body-recomposition use is anecdotal and unsupported by clinical evidence.
mechanism: LL-37 is a 37-amino acid amphipathic alpha-helical cationic peptide (net charge +6 at physiological pH) encoded on chromosome 3p21.3. Its antimicrobial mechanism relies on electrostatic attraction to negatively charged bacterial membranes, followed by a carpet-like membrane-disruption model that permeabilizes and lyses pathogens without forming stable pores. Beyond direct killing, LL-37 exerts broad immunomodulatory effects via multiple receptor pathways: (1) chemotaxis of neutrophils, monocytes, and T-cells through formyl peptide receptor-like 1 (FPRL1/FPR2); (2) activation of EGFR and ErbB receptors, stimulating keratinocyte proliferation and migration relevant to wound re-epithelialization; (3) promotion of angiogenesis through endothelial cell activation; (4) neutralization of bacterial LPS, suppressing septic inflammatory cascades; (5) stimulation of plasmacytoid dendritic cells to produce type I interferons, contributing to antiviral and antitumor immune responses. At high concentrations LL-37 can disrupt eukaryotic cell membranes, conferring concentration-dependent cytotoxicity and hemolysis — a key therapeutic limitation. LL-37 also forms complexes with self-DNA/RNA that activate TLR7, TLR8, and TLR9, which drives autoimmune cascades in conditions such as psoriasis. Cathelicidin expression is upregulated by vitamin D3 (calcitriol/calcipotriol) via vitamin D response elements in the hCAP-18 promoter.
monitoring_notes: This compound is NOT FDA-approved and NOT established for human systemic use. Monitoring guidance below pertains only to research or investigational contexts and is not an endorsement of use.
For any investigational topical use: monitor wound site for signs of contact dermatitis, excessive erythema, or edema

Given pro-coagulant preclinical findings: baseline coagulation panel (PT, aPTT, fibrinogen, CBC with platelets) would be prudent if systemic use were ever to be investigated under clinician supervision
Given theoretical cancer-risk in susceptible tissues: thorough personal and family history of hormone-sensitive or epithelial cancers should be documented before any experimental use
LL-37 expression is upregulated by vitamin D3; correcting vitamin D deficiency is a safer endogenous induction strategy — monitor 25-OH vitamin D levels
Autoimmune disease surveillance: any patient with personal or family history of psoriasis, rosacea, or lupus should not receive experimental LL-37 outside of formal IRB-approved research
No pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic monitoring parameters are established for any non-topical route of administration
absolute_contraindications: Active or history of hormone-sensitive or LL-37-overexpressing cancers (lung, breast, ovarian, prostate, or malignant melanoma) — protumorigenic effects documented at molecular/animal level for these cancer types; clinician assessment required
Known hypersensitivity to cathelicidin or any formulation component
relative_contraindications: Pre-existing autoimmune diseases (especially psoriasis, rosacea, systemic lupus erythematosus, inflammatory bowel disease) — LL-37 is implicated in pathogenesis of these conditions via TLR-mediated immune amplification
Known coagulopathy or hypercoagulable states — LL-37 activates platelets and enhances coagulation factor activity in preclinical models
History of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease — LL-37 IgG autoantibody levels positively associated with future MI risk in observational data
Patients on anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy — theoretical additive risk of altered hemostasis
Males of reproductive age or those concerned about fertility — FDA-cited preclinical fertility risk
Concurrent immunosuppressive therapy — unpredictable immune interaction
Pregnancy or breastfeeding — insufficient safety data
common_side_effects: Mild local skin irritation at topical application site (redness, edema) — reported in clinical trials
Concentration-dependent cytotoxicity to mammalian cells at higher doses (in vitro and animal data)
Hemolytic activity at supraphysiological concentrations — observed in vitro; dose-dependent
Excessive immune stimulation (theoretical at systemic doses — not established clinically)
Possible mild contact dermatitis from topical application
serious_adverse_events: Dose-dependent cytotoxicity to human leukocytes and T-lymphocytes at concentrations >=25 microM — observed in vitro

Platelet activation and pro-thrombotic effects: LL-37 activates platelets via glycoprotein VI/Src/Syk/PLC pathway and enhances activity of coagulation factors FXa and thrombin — established in in-vitro and animal studies; clinical significance of exogenous administration unclear

Potential protumorigenic effects in tissues expressing EGFR, ErbB2, or FPRL-1: LL-37 has been shown to promote proliferation, angiogenesis, and metastatic signaling in lung, breast, ovarian, and prostate cancers at the molecular and animal level
Dermatologic toxicity reported in one case during intra-tumoral melanoma injection trial (NCT02225366) — clinicopathologic features documented in PubMed PMID 29665030

Autoimmune cascade amplification in psoriasis: LL-37 complexes with self-DNA to activate TLR9 on plasmacytoid dendritic cells, driving type I interferon production and autoimmune inflammation — well-characterized in human psoriatic skin studies
FDA-cited concern: potential negative effects on male fertility based on preclinical data (basis for 503A Category 2 classification)
Proteolytic instability in serum limits bioavailability; uncontrolled systemic delivery may result in unpredictable exposure

dose_references.context: Topical wound healing — chronic venous leg ulcers (Phase I/IIa RCT, Gronberg et al. 2014, n=34): twice-weekly topical application for 4 weeks. Reported optimal healing at 0.5 mg/mL with improvement at 1.6 mg/mL; the 3.2 mg/mL arm showed no benefit, consistent with concentration-dependent cytotoxicity (inverted U-shaped dose-response). Small early-phase trial.

citation.title: Treatment with LL-37 is safe and effective in enhancing healing of hard-to-heal venous leg ulcers: a randomized, placebo-controlled clinical trial

dose_references.notes: Evidence level downgraded HUMAN_RCT -> HUMAN_OBS: this is a small early-phase (Phase I/IIa) trial with n=34, not adequate to be graded as a definitive RCT. Concentrations expressed as mg/mL in solution; applied at 25 microliters per cm2 of wound area. Note the 3.2 mg/mL arm showed NO benefit due to cytotoxicity — the upper number reflects a tested ceiling that failed, not a recommended target. This dose range is specific to the topical wound-healing context (Promore Pharma, ropocamptide) and CANNOT be extrapolated to systemic or injection use. Educational/research reference only; not a dosing recommendation. NOT FDA-approved.

dose_references.context: Topical wound healing — chronic venous leg ulcers (Phase IIb multicentric RCT, ropocamptide, n=148): 0.5 mg/mL and 1.6 mg/mL twice weekly for 13 weeks applied at 25 microliters per cm2. No statistically significant difference in the total population; a subgroup of patients with large wounds (>=10 cm2) showed benefit for 0.5 mg/mL (OR 4.454, p=0.046). Well-tolerated; no treatment-related serious adverse events.
dose_references.frequency: Twice weekly topical application for 13 weeks

citation.title: Evaluation of LL-37 in healing of hard-to-heal venous leg ulcers: A multicentric prospective randomized placebo-controlled clinical trial

dose_references.notes: Retained as HUMAN_RCT: this is the pivotal, adequately powered (n=148) multicentric Phase IIb trial — the most rigorous human dose data available. Primary endpoint was NOT met in the total population; the positive signal was a secondary large-wound subgroup finding (interpret with caution as exploratory). Concentrations in mg/mL; topical route only. No regulatory approval has been granted. These doses are NOT established for any systemic route and are presented for education only, not as a recommendation.

dose_references.context: Topical wound healing — diabetic foot ulcer (small RCT, n=25, Jakarta, 2020-2021): LL-37 cream at 0.5 mg/g applied at 0.025 mL/cm2 twice weekly for 4 weeks. Reported significantly greater granulation index improvement vs. placebo (p<0.05). No treatment-related adverse events.

citation.title: Efficacy of LL-37 cream in enhancing healing of diabetic foot ulcer: a randomized double-blind controlled trial

dose_references.notes: Evidence level downgraded HUMAN_RCT -> HUMAN_OBS: very small single-center pilot RCT (n=25), insufficient to be graded as definitive randomized evidence. Concentration expressed as mg/g cream; applied with spatula and covered by transparent film dressing. Topical route only; not generalizable to other routes or indications. Educational reference, not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: Intra-tumoral injection for melanoma — Phase 1 dose-escalation clinical trial (NCT02225366) in patients with cutaneous melanoma metastases. Mechanism: activation of plasmacytoid dendritic cells to mediate tumor destruction. Specific dose levels from the published trial record are not fully available in open literature; the trial is completed. One published case report documents dermatologic toxicity from 8 weekly injections with interval clinical shrinkage.

citation.title: Intratumoral Injections of LL37 for Melanoma — ClinicalTrials.gov NCT02225366
Dermatologic toxicity from novel therapy using antimicrobial peptide LL-37 in melanoma

dose_references.notes: Dose values correctly null — exact escalation levels are not publicly confirmed in a peer-reviewed publication, so no number is cited. Route is intra-tumoral (classified as 'other'). This is oncology research, not a body-recomposition or wellness indication, and is included only for completeness of the human evidence picture.

dose_references.context: Subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous injection for wellness/body-recomposition use — NO clinical trial data. Community use is anecdotal; no peer-reviewed dose-finding studies exist for these routes. The FDA lists LL-37 in 503A Category 2 (cannot be legally compounded for human use). No dose can be cited.

citation.title: FDA 503A Bulk Drug Substances Compounding List — Cathelicidin LL-37 Category 2

dose_references.notes: NO ESTABLISHED HUMAN DOSE exists for subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous routes. Any circulating dose figures for injection use are anecdotal and unsupported by clinical evidence. This is the route relevant to body-recomposition/wellness interest, and it has NO dose support. The compound is research-only by regulatory designation (FDA 503A Category 2) in the United States.

synergies.rationale: Vitamin D3 and its active metabolite calcitriol upregulate transcription of the hCAP-18/LL-37 gene via vitamin D response elements (VDREs) in the promoter region. Topical calcipotriol (a vitamin D3 analogue) has been shown in human skin studies to enhance wound-induced hCAP-18/LL-37 mRNA and protein expression. This describes a well-characterized endogenous regulatory axis — correcting vitamin D deficiency may indirectly support physiological LL-37 production. This is an educational, mechanistic/observational relationship, not a co-administration protocol or a dosing recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: Endogenous regulatory relationship only — NOT a studied co-administration protocol and not a recommendation to combine products. Vitamin D toxicity (hypercalcemia) is possible with excessive supplementation. Any supplementation decision requires clinician guidance and 25-OH vitamin D monitoring. Concerns endogenous LL-37 induction, not exogenous LL-37 dosing.

synergies.rationale: In the context of cutaneous wound biology, LL-37 and GHK-Cu are discussed in wound-care literature as addressing complementary phases: LL-37 the early antimicrobial/anti-biofilm and re-epithelialization phase (keratinocyte migration, EGFR signaling), and GHK-Cu the later proliferative/remodeling phase (collagen synthesis, angiogenesis, matrix remodeling). This is an educational mechanistic rationale extrapolated from independent studies of each agent; no head-to-head or combination trial has been published.
synergies.caution_notes: No combination trial data exist; rationale is mechanistic extrapolation only and must not be construed as a recommended protocol. GHK-Cu has its own unestablished systemic dose profile. Both compounds lack FDA approval for systemic use.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 has reported anti-inflammatory, mucosal-protective, and angiogenic effects in animal/in-vitro models (growth-factor receptor upregulation, NO-mediated vascular effects). In a conceptual wound-healing context, LL-37 could address microbial/biofilm burden while BPC-157 is independently studied for vascular and connective-tissue repair. This is an educational mechanistic rationale only; no co-administration clinical data exist.
synergies.caution_notes: No human clinical data on this combination exist; rationale is extrapolated from independent animal/in-vitro data and is not a validated or recommended protocol. BPC-157 is also not FDA-approved.

synergies.rationale: In preclinical (animal) ovarian cancer models, LL-37 combined with CpG-ODN (a TLR9 agonist) showed greater antitumor effect than monotherapy; LL-37 forms electrostatic complexes with CpG-ODN that enhance cellular uptake and innate immune activation of plasmacytoid dendritic cells. This is an animal-level mechanistic finding relevant exclusively to an oncology research context.
synergies.caution_notes: Relevant only in experimental oncology research, NOT for wellness or body-recomposition use. CpG-ODN is a research reagent, not a therapeutic agent for routine use. Combination use in humans has not been evaluated in peer-reviewed clinical trials. Not a recommended protocol.

name: VIP (Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide)
short_description: VIP is a 28-amino-acid endogenous neuropeptide and potent vasodilator with anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, and neuroregulatory properties. Its synthetic form, aviptadil, has been studied in trials for pulmonary arterial hypertension, ARDS, and COVID-19 respiratory failure but has NOT received FDA approval for any indication. Compounded intranasal use in the CIRS community rests on small, uncontrolled observational data. Educational tracking entry only — not a dosing recommendation.
mechanism: VIP binds two class B G-protein-coupled receptors, VPAC1 (predominant in lung, liver, T-lymphocytes) and VPAC2 (predominant in smooth muscle, brain, pancreas). Receptor activation stimulates adenylyl cyclase, raises intracellular cAMP, and activates protein kinase A downstream signaling. Key effects include: (1) smooth-muscle relaxation and vasodilation via nitric-oxide-dependent pathways; (2) suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IFN-gamma, IL-6, IL-12) from macrophages and dendritic cells; (3) promotion of CD4+CD25+ T-regulatory cells; (4) inhibition of Th1 immune responses and facilitation of Th2/Treg balance; (5) neuromodulation in the enteric nervous system, including gut motility regulation and intestinal gluconeogenesis via VPAC1 in enterocytes; (6) neuroprotective and circadian-rhythm-regulatory roles in the central nervous system. VIP's plasma half-life is approximately 1-2 minutes due to rapid enzymatic degradation by dipeptidyl peptidase IV (DPPIV) and neutral endopeptidases, which substantially limits systemic bioavailability from non-IV routes.
monitoring_notes: Blood pressure must be monitored continuously during IV infusion; dose-dependent hypotension is a recognized class effect of VPAC agonists (though in the COVID RCT hypotension rates did not differ significantly from placebo, 26.0% vs 21.5%).
Heart rate monitoring during IV use; compensatory tachycardia may accompany vasodilatory hypotension.
Pulmonary artery pressure and hemodynamic parameters should be monitored in patients with pulmonary arterial hypertension.

Screen for active malignancy before any VIP administration: VPAC1/VPAC2 receptors are expressed at high density in many breast and prostate cancers, and in vitro evidence shows VIP can induce VEGF secretion and proliferation in VPAC-receptor-positive cancer cell lines.
Screen for baseline hypotension (systolic BP <100 mmHg) before initiating any VIP protocol; the vasodilatory mechanism can precipitate clinically significant blood pressure drops.

Baseline and periodic electrolyte panel is prudent given VIP's stimulation of intestinal secretion; chronic high-dose diarrhea can cause hypokalemia and metabolic acidosis (mirroring VIPoma syndrome).

For intranasal compounded VIP in the CIRS context, the published Shoemaker protocol positions VIP as a final step after prerequisite measures (removal from biotoxin exposure, binders, MARCoNS eradication), not as a standalone intervention. This is described for context, not endorsed.
No established therapeutic drug-monitoring assay for plasma VIP exists in routine clinical use; assessment relies on clinical endpoints and inflammatory biomarkers.
VIP is not FDA-approved; any clinical use involves compounded or investigational formulations. Confirm the regulatory status of the specific formulation used.
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to vasoactive intestinal peptide, aviptadil, or any excipient in the formulation.
Active VIPoma or other VIP-secreting neuroendocrine tumor (exogenous VIP would be pharmacodynamically redundant and could exacerbate secretory diarrhea, hypokalemia, and vasodilation).

Active or suspected VPAC-receptor-positive malignancy (e.g., breast, prostate, small cell lung cancer) where exogenous VIP could theoretically stimulate tumor growth or angiogenesis via VEGF upregulation — based on in vitro and in vivo evidence of VIP-driven tumor cell proliferation.
relative_contraindications: Baseline systolic hypotension (SBP <100 mmHg) or hemodynamic instability: vasodilatory mechanism increases risk of clinically significant blood pressure drop.
Severe aortic stenosis or other fixed cardiac output states: vasodilation without compensatory cardiac reserve can precipitate hemodynamic collapse.
Concurrent use of other vasodilators (phosphodiesterase inhibitors, nitrates, alpha-blockers): additive hypotension risk.
Active gastrointestinal disease with high-output diarrhea or electrolyte derangement: VIP stimulates intestinal secretion and can worsen diarrhea and hypokalemia.
History of syncope or orthostatic intolerance: vasodilatory side effects may exacerbate.
Pregnancy and lactation: no human safety data for exogenous VIP administration.
Personal or family history of cancer with unknown VPAC receptor status: caution warranted given preclinical data on VPAC-mediated tumor signaling.
Uncontrolled bleeding disorders: vasodilation can exacerbate bleeding risk in certain clinical contexts.
common_side_effects: Diarrhea (dose-dependent; 32.8% vs 1.5% placebo in the Phase 2b/3 IV RCT, P<0.0001)
Facial flushing (vasodilatory mechanism; reported across inhaled and IV studies)
Transient hypotension (vasodilatory; in the IV RCT, rates were not significantly above placebo and caused no discontinuations)
Nausea (gastrointestinal motility stimulation; reported in aviptadil studies)
Mild tachycardia (compensatory response to vasodilation)
serious_adverse_events: Clinically significant hypotension potentially requiring vasopressor support (theoretical/dose-dependent risk in IV infusion context; not observed above placebo in the COVID ICU RCT).
Severe hypokalemia and metabolic acidosis from prolonged secretory diarrhea at high doses (mirrors VIPoma syndrome; caution with high-dose or prolonged administration).

Potential stimulation of VPAC-receptor-positive tumor growth (breast, prostate, small cell lung): in vitro evidence of VIP-induced VEGF secretion and proliferation; no long-term oncologic safety data in humans with pre-existing occult malignancy.
Anaphylaxis/hypersensitivity reaction (class risk with any peptide formulation).

dose_references.notes: Eight patients with idiopathic primary pulmonary hypertension received 200 mcg/day inhaled VIP. Mean pulmonary artery pressure fell, cardiac output increased, and 6-min walk capacity improved significantly at 12 weeks (P<0.01) with further gains at 24 weeks; no side effects reported. Very small sample (n=8), open-label, no control arm — observational evidence only, NOT an RCT. Educational reference; not a dosing recommendation.

No side effects reported. Open-label acute study, no control arm. First author corrected to Leuchte (entry had erroneously cited Petkov). Educational reference only.

dose_references.notes: CORRECTION: original entry listed low/high 50-150 with unit mcg/kg, but the trial dosed in pmol/kg/hr (50 -> 100 -> 150 pmol/kg/hr over 3 days). pmol/kg/hr is not expressible in the allowed unit enum, and 50-150 mcg/kg would be a wildly different (and dangerous) figure, so the numeric range is nulled and the true regimen is preserved here in text. Genuine double-blind, multicenter, placebo-controlled RCT (131 aviptadil / 65 placebo) — HUMAN_RCT grade retained. Primary endpoint (alive and free of respiratory failure at day 60) was NOT met (OR ~1.6; 95% CI 0.86-3.11 when controlling for baseline ventilation). Diarrhea 32.8% aviptadil vs 1.5% placebo (P<0.0001). Hypotension 26.0% vs 21.5% (P=0.6, not significant); no discontinuations from hypotension. IV use requires continuous hemodynamic monitoring. First author corrected to Youssef (entry had erroneously cited 'Pertsovskiy'). Investigational only; not FDA-approved.

dose_references.frequency: Initial: 4 doses per day (50 mcg per dose = up to 200 mcg/day); self-titrated down to minimum effective dose over up to 18 months

citation.title: Vasoactive intestinal polypeptide (VIP) corrects chronic inflammatory response syndrome (CIRS) acquired following exposure to water-damaged buildings

dose_references.notes: 50 mcg aviptadil (Bachem AG) per metered nasal spray dose, starting 4x/day, self-titrated downward. Open-label, unblinded, no concurrent placebo control, n=20 (compared to historical controls). Authors explicitly acknowledged no comparable long-term VIP studies exist. Inflammatory markers (C4a, TGF-beta1, VEGF, MMP9) normalized; CD4+CD25+ Treg increased from mean 8.9 to 22.5; one transient lipase elevation without symptoms. Compounded VIP nasal spray is NOT FDA-approved; any such use is individual off-label clinician prescribing. Educational reference only; not an endorsement of this protocol. SOURCE CAUTION: published via SCIRP (a publisher listed in predatory-publisher directories); the study is open-label and uncontrolled. Included only to document the source of a community-circulating range, not as credible clinical evidence.

dose_references.context: Subcutaneous injection of VIP — community/off-label use with NO supporting human clinical trial data
dose_references.notes: No published human clinical trial has established a subcutaneous dose for VIP. Community/vendor/forum reports describe SC use (figures of 10-100 mcg circulate) but are entirely unsupported by peer-reviewed human trials. VIP's ultra-short plasma half-life (~1-2 min) raises serious pharmacokinetic doubts about SC delivery. Row included ONLY to flag the existence of anecdotal community use, not to endorse or quantify it. Range left null; no citation available.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic overlap only: VIP modulates enteric nervous system signaling and suppresses intestinal pro-inflammatory cytokines via VPAC1, while BPC-157 has shown mucosal-healing and angiogenic effects in preclinical gut-injury models. Both act within gut-brain-axis physiology. The complementarity is between distinct pathways (neuroimmune modulation via VIP and mucosal repair via BPC-157), not additive potency. No human co-administration study exists.
synergies.caution_notes: No human RCT or observational study has evaluated BPC-157 + VIP together. Both are research-only compounds without FDA approval. Combined vasodilatory and gut-motility effects are theoretical; additive hypotension cannot be excluded. Not a recommendation — discuss with a clinician before any use.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic overlap only: KPV (a C-terminal alpha-MSH tripeptide) exerts anti-inflammatory effects via NF-kB inhibition and melanocortin (MC1R/MC3R) signaling, whereas VIP suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines through a VPAC/cAMP/PKA pathway. These are mechanistically distinct anti-inflammatory axes that have each been studied in intestinal inflammation models. Co-targeting two separate cascades is the theoretical rationale, not evidence of combined benefit.
synergies.caution_notes: No human trial has evaluated KPV + VIP co-administration. Both are research compounds without FDA approval for systemic use. Additive effects on blood pressure and immune regulation are uncharacterized. Not a recommendation — clinician consultation required.

synergies.rationale: Educational receptor-biology note: PACAP shares the VPAC1 and VPAC2 receptors with VIP and additionally binds PAC1 (predominant in the nervous system). Both activate cAMP/PKA; PACAP adds PAC1-mediated neuroprotective signaling that VIP cannot provide. They have been co-studied as endogenous sister neuropeptides in animal neuroinflammation and multiple-sclerosis models. This is a receptor-profile contrast for understanding, not a combination protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: PACAP is a research compound with no FDA approval for systemic administration. Shared VPAC1/VPAC2 activity with VIP makes additive hypotension, tachycardia, and diarrhea plausible pharmacodynamic interactions. No human combination data exist. Not a recommendation.

name: Pentadeca Arginate (PDA)
short_description: Pentadeca Arginate (PDA) is a synthetic 15-amino-acid peptide (sequence GEPPPGKPADDAGLV) that is the di-L-arginine salt form of BPC-157 (Body Protection Compound 157), a peptide originally isolated from human gastric juice. It is not FDA-approved for any indication. All efficacy evidence derives from animal (primarily rat) studies conducted on the parent acetate form of BPC-157; no peer-reviewed human efficacy trials exist for PDA specifically, and no peer-reviewed publications are indexed on PubMed under "pentadeca arginate." The arginate salt formulation was introduced in the context of FDA placing BPC-157 on the Category 2 compounding list under section 503A in 2023; the arginate counterion is claimed to confer improved acid stability for oral delivery, but this has not been validated in published human pharmacokinetic studies. Regulatory status remains in flux (the Category 2 listing was revised in 2026) but BPC-157/PDA remains an unapproved, investigational compound. Clinician supervision and a full medical history review are essential before any use.
mechanism: PDA shares the full 15-amino-acid sequence of BPC-157 (GEPPPGKPADDAGLV); all proposed mechanisms are extrapolated from BPC-157 animal and in vitro research. Key characterized pathways include: (1) VEGFR2 upregulation — BPC-157 increases vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 2 expression on endothelial cells, promoting angiogenesis and blood flow restoration to injured tissue independent of elevating VEGF-A itself (ANIMAL/MECHANISTIC evidence); (2) Src-Caveolin-1-eNOS axis activation — BPC-157 phosphorylates Src kinase, which disrupts inhibitory eNOS/caveolin-1 binding, releasing nitric oxide synthase to produce NO and cause concentration-dependent vasodilation (ANIMAL/MECHANISTIC evidence, rat aortic ring and HUVEC models); (3) FAK-paxillin pathway — dose-dependent phosphorylation of focal adhesion kinase and paxillin promotes endothelial cell migration, a necessary step in angiogenesis (MECHANISTIC); (4) Growth hormone receptor upregulation — BPC-157 dose- and time-dependently increases GHR expression in rat tendon fibroblasts at both mRNA and protein levels, potentiating GH-stimulated cell proliferation (ANIMAL/in vitro); (5) COX-2, nNOS, iNOS, eNOS mRNA modulation at injury sites, counteracting oxidative stress and normalizing nitric oxide (ANIMAL); (6) Broad cytoprotection — BPC-157 is stable in gastric juice and activates an endogenous cytoprotective program extending beyond the GI tract. The arginine counterion in PDA does not alter the amino acid sequence; claimed advantages (enhanced gastric acid stability vs. acetate) rest on patent filings, not published peer-reviewed pharmacokinetic studies in any species.
monitoring_notes: No established human monitoring protocol exists; the following are precautionary points based on mechanism, for clinician discussion only
Blood pressure monitoring before and during any use, given NO-mediated vasodilation effects
Skin examination at baseline and periodically if personal or family melanoma/skin cancer history — angiogenic peptides carry a theoretical dermatologic risk
CBC and hepatic function panel at baseline for any extended use, given absence of chronic safety data
Injection-site inspection at each dose for signs of infection, excessive inflammation, or abscess formation
Thorough cancer-screening history before initiation given the absolute contraindication for active/history of malignancy
Source verification: peptide purity and sterility cannot be assured from compounding or gray-market sources; HPLC purity certificates should be requested
Report any systemic symptoms (fever, rash, myalgia) immediately — may indicate immune response or contamination
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy or history of malignancy — pro-angiogenic mechanism poses a theoretical risk of supporting tumor vascularization and growth
Pregnancy — no reproductive toxicology data in any species; fetal safety entirely unknown
Breastfeeding — unknown excretion into breast milk; no safety data
Known hypersensitivity to the peptide or to excipients/reconstitution vehicle
relative_contraindications: Personal or strong family history of cancer — heightened concern given the angiogenic mechanism
Bleeding disorders or concurrent anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy — vasodilatory and angiogenic effects may interact
Autoimmune conditions — NO-system immunomodulatory effects are bidirectional and unpredictable
Concurrent use of drugs with known NO-system interactions (e.g., PDE5 inhibitors, nitrates) — additive vasodilation possible
Uncontrolled hypertension or severe cardiovascular disease — hemodynamic effects via eNOS/NO have not been characterized in this population
Use of any product from an unverified or gray-market source — sterility and purity cannot be assured
common_side_effects: Injection-site redness, transient swelling, or stinging (reported in community/anecdotal accounts; no controlled incidence data)
Mild nausea (anecdotal, reportedly more common at higher self-administered doses)
Transient dizziness or orthostatic symptoms, plausibly related to NO-mediated vasodilation (mechanistic/anecdotal)
Headache (anecdotal reports)
Mild injection-site itching or localized lump (may reflect pH of reconstitution vehicle rather than the peptide itself)
serious_adverse_events: Theoretical risk of accelerating growth of occult malignancy via pro-angiogenic (VEGFR2) mechanisms — no confirmed human case but mechanistically plausible and cited as a primary safety concern
Potential immunogenicity: FDA cited risk of immune response among reasons for restricting BPC-157 in compounding; no characterized human incidence data
Impurity-related adverse events: gray-market and unregulated sources carry risk of contamination, endotoxin exposure, and unknown degradation products
Allergic or anaphylactic reaction to peptide or excipients — rare, case reports only
Unknown long-term safety profile: no chronic-exposure human data exists

dose_references.context: Animal efficacy studies — BPC-157 acetate form, rat models (tendon, muscle, gut, neurological injury). Representative of the entire published preclinical evidence base; no equivalent PDA-specific study exists.
dose_references.frequency: Once daily (intraperitoneal or oral in drinking water) in rodents

Pentadecapeptide BPC 157 Enhances the Growth Hormone Receptor Expression in Tendon Fibroblasts

dose_references.notes: The Sikiric group (University of Zagreb) used 10 mcg/kg and 10 ng/kg doses across hundreds of rat studies; both tiers produced tissue-repair benefit. Oral delivery used ~0.16 mcg/mL or 0.16 ng/mL in drinking water. Numeric low/high values have been nulled in this field because PDA is a research-only, non-FDA-approved peptide with no cited human efficacy data: rodent mg/kg doses CANNOT be extrapolated to a human dose and must not be surfaced as a usable number. No human equivalent dose has been established in any published study.

dose_references.context: Phase I safety study of BPC-157 (as PL 14736) in healthy male volunteers — the only available human safety data; results published only as a conference abstract (Digestive Disease Week / Gut, 2003), never as a full peer-reviewed paper.

citation.title: Safety, tolerability and pharmacokinetics of PL 14736, a novel agent for treatment of ulcerative colitis, in healthy male volunteers

dose_references.notes: Veljaca et al. (2003) reported BPC-157 (as enema formulation PL 14736) was safe and well tolerated in healthy volunteers. No dose-response or pharmacokinetic values were published in a peer-reviewed journal. Graded HUMAN_OBS (not HUMAN_RCT) because this is a conference abstract only and was never published as a full controlled trial. It does not establish a therapeutic dose for any indication.

dose_references.context: Community and compounding-clinic use of BPC-157 / PDA in humans — subcutaneous injection. No peer-reviewed human trial supports any dose range; figures circulate in wellness-clinic literature and are based on practitioner extrapolation from animal studies.
dose_references.notes: Practitioner and community sources frequently cite figures in the low-hundreds of mcg/day subcutaneously. These are NOT derived from any published human clinical trial and cannot be verified against safety or efficacy data. No low_value or high_value is populated because there is no credible cited human number. Reported here only to flag that such anecdotal dosing exists; it must not be treated as an established or recommended dose.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157/PDA and TB-500 act on mechanistically distinct repair pathways. BPC-157 primarily drives VEGFR2-mediated angiogenesis, NO-system modulation, and fibroblast GHR upregulation at local injury sites (animal/in vitro). TB-500 sequesters G-actin monomers and promotes cell migration in preclinical models. Their mechanistic complementarity is sometimes cited as a theoretical basis for co-administration, but no published study has directly tested PDA plus TB-500 in combination in any species. This is educational context, not a protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: No combination safety data exists in humans or animals. Additive angiogenic effects are plausible; avoid in individuals with active or suspected malignancy. Educational framing only — not a recommendation to combine.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157/PDA stimulates early angiogenesis and fibroblast activity via VEGFR2 and FAK-paxillin pathways (animal/in vitro). GHK-Cu, a copper-binding tripeptide, is associated in preclinical work with collagen/elastin synthesis and delivers copper as a cofactor for lysyl oxidase, superoxide dismutase, and cytochrome c oxidase. The two are sometimes discussed as targeting different stages of the repair cascade (vascular initiation vs. matrix maturation), offering a mechanistic rationale to study them together. No co-administration study exists for either BPC-157 or PDA with GHK-Cu.
synergies.caution_notes: No peer-reviewed combination study exists. Mechanistic rationale is plausible but unvalidated in vivo. Educational framing only.

synergies.rationale: KPV is the C-terminal tripeptide fragment of alpha-MSH with anti-inflammatory activity in preclinical models; it has been reported to inhibit NF-kB signaling and reduce TNF-alpha, IL-1beta, and IL-6 in vitro, and is transported into intestinal epithelial cells via PepT1. BPC-157 is associated with mucosal and tissue repair via angiogenesis and cytoprotection in animal models. The two are sometimes discussed together in the context of gut injury (anti-inflammatory vs. reparative roles). Synergy is mechanistically plausible but no co-administration study has been published.
synergies.caution_notes: KPV human data is also limited to animal and in vitro models. Combining immunomodulatory and pro-angiogenic peptides carries theoretical risk in immunocompromised patients or those with active infection or malignancy. Educational framing only.

name: Larazotide Acetate
short_description: Larazotide acetate (AT-1001) is a synthetic 8-amino-acid oral peptide (sequence GGVLVQPG) that acts as a tight junction regulator and zonulin antagonist, reducing intestinal paracellular permeability. Investigational — NOT FDA-approved. Primary research context is celiac disease; the Phase 3 trial (CeDLara, 9 Meters Biopharma) in celiac disease was discontinued in 2022 due to futility. Small Phase 2a data exists in pediatric MIS-C/post-COVID (n=12). No established therapeutic use outside clinical investigation. Educational/tracking entry only — not a prescription or protocol.
mechanism: Larazotide acetate was derived from research on zonula occludens toxin (ZOT), an enterotoxin secreted by Vibrio cholerae, and corresponds to an 8-amino-acid sequence (GGVLVQPG) that competes with zonulin at its receptors (proposed EGFR and PAR2) on intestinal epithelial cells. Zonulin is an endogenous protein that signals tight junction disassembly, increasing paracellular permeability. By occupying these receptors, larazotide is proposed to prevent zonulin-triggered actin polymerization and tight junction protein internalization (specifically occludin and claudin family proteins), thereby maintaining or restoring epithelial barrier integrity. A second proposed mechanism is inhibition of myosin light chain kinase (MLCK), reducing cytoskeletal tension on actin filaments and further stabilizing tight junctions. The compound has minimal systemic absorption; no detectable plasma levels were found in Phase I studies at therapeutic doses, suggesting a predominantly luminal, local mechanism of action.
monitoring_notes: Larazotide is investigational (not FDA-approved); any use outside a registered clinical trial lacks regulatory oversight and quality assurance of the compound.

In RCTs, standard safety monitoring included medical history, physical examination, vital signs, clinical laboratory tests (hepatic, renal, bone markers), and 12-lead ECG — no clinically significant changes were observed at therapeutic doses.
Tissue transglutaminase (tTG-IgA) antibody titers were monitored in celiac disease trials as a surrogate marker of mucosal immune activation.
No plasma levels were detectable at therapeutic doses in Phase 1 studies; systemic pharmacokinetic monitoring is not established.

Clinical trials excluded patients with refractory celiac disease, other chronic inflammatory GI disease, diabetes, or autoimmune/neurological disease that could confound assessment — these populations have no safety data.
Phase 3 futility analysis (2022, CeDLara) found no statistically meaningful separation from placebo in the larger trial; efficacy in unselected populations should not be assumed from Phase 2 data.
Any numeric dose shown for this compound is a trial-tested value for educational reference only and is not a recommendation to dose; consult a qualified clinician.
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to larazotide acetate or any component of the formulation (standard peptide allergy precaution; not formally characterized in trials due to investigational status).
Pregnancy — excluded from all RCTs; no reproductive safety data available.
Breastfeeding — excluded from all RCTs; no data on excretion into breast milk.
relative_contraindications: Concomitant use of systemic or intestinal immunosuppressants (exclusion criterion in trials; interaction unknown).
Continuous antibiotic use (excluded from trials; may alter intestinal microbiome and confound barrier effects).
Chronic NSAID use (excluded from trials; NSAIDs independently increase intestinal permeability).
Medications that significantly alter gastric pH or intestinal motility (excluded from trials; may affect drug delivery from the formulation).
Refractory celiac disease or severe celiac complications (excluded from efficacy trials; safety and benefit uncertain).
Pediatric use outside the MIS-C research context — limited data; the MIS-C Phase 2a pilot enrolled only 12 children.
Use of compound sourced outside a regulated clinical supply chain — purity, sterility, and dose accuracy are unverified.
common_side_effects: Headache (most commonly reported event, ~20% in one dose-ranging trial; not clearly dose-dependent and comparable to placebo).
Urinary tract infection (~5.8% in one trial; not clearly drug-related).
Fatigue (reported in some trials at rates comparable to placebo).
Gastrointestinal symptoms (expected in the celiac disease population studied; not clearly distinguished from disease activity in all trials).
serious_adverse_events: No drug-related serious adverse events were reported across Phase 1, Phase 2a, or Phase 2b trials (cumulative exposure in approximately 500+ research subjects at doses from 0.25 mg to 36 mg).
No clinically significant findings of hepatic, renal, or bone toxicity in clinical laboratory monitoring across trials.
No significant changes in vital signs or ECG parameters at any studied dose.

Absence of serious adverse events in completed trials should not be extrapolated to populations excluded from those trials (pregnancy, refractory disease, immunocompromised) or to unapproved off-label formulations sourced outside regulated clinical supply.

dose_references.context: Phase 2b RCT — persistent symptoms in celiac disease patients on gluten-free diet (n=342 randomized, 340 mITT, 12-week treatment, NCT01396213, 74 North American sites). Three doses tested orally three times daily.

dose_references.notes: Only the 0.5 mg TID dose met the primary endpoint (CeD-GSRS; p=0.022 ANCOVA, p=0.005 MMRM). The 1 mg and 2 mg doses showed no significant benefit versus placebo, demonstrating an unusual inverse dose-response. Safety was comparable to placebo at all doses. Published in Gastroenterology 2015 (PMC4446229). Verified against source. NOT a prescribing recommendation — efficacy was not confirmed in the larger Phase 3 (CeDLara) trial, which was discontinued for futility in 2022.

dose_references.notes: Lower doses (notably 0.25 mg and 1 mg) appeared to limit gluten-induced GI symptom worsening (GSRS); the highest dose (8 mg) did not. Primary endpoint (urinary lactulose/mannitol ratio) was inconclusive due to outpatient measurement variability. Generally well tolerated; no serious adverse events. Most common events were headache and UTI. Range reflects the full set of doses tested in-trial, not a recommended dose. Note corrected from prior draft (source attributes protective effect to 0.25 mg and 1 mg, not 0.25 mg and 4 mg). Published Am J Gastroenterol 2012.

dose_references.notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT: this is a Phase 1 dose-escalation safety/tolerability study, not a controlled efficacy RCT, so the 36 mg figure is a maximum tested single-dose ceiling — NOT a therapeutic or repeat dose and not an efficacy-supported number. Doses up to 36 mg single dose in healthy volunteers did not produce severe drug-related adverse events; no detectable plasma levels at therapeutic doses, confirming local GI action. High value retained because it is human and cited, but must not be read as a usable dose.

dose_references.context: Phase 2a RCT (small pilot) — pediatric MIS-C related to SARS-CoV-2, n=12, median age 5.7 yrs, four times daily for 3 weeks (PMID 40737433).

citation.title: Viral spike antigen clearance and augmented recovery in children with post-COVID multisystem inflammatory syndrome treated with larazotide

dose_references.notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS per small-pilot rule (n=12). Exact dose per administration not publicly specified in reviewed sources, so no numeric value is asserted. Study (double-blind, placebo-controlled pilot) reported faster GI symptom resolution, faster clearance of circulating SARS-CoV-2 spike antigen, and faster return to activity, with no larazotide-related adverse events. Published in Science Translational Medicine, July 2025. Hypothesis-generating only given n=12.

dose_references.context: Off-label / anecdotal use in 'leaky gut,' longevity, or body recomposition contexts — no clinical trial data supports these uses.
dose_references.notes: Community and direct-to-consumer wellness sources describe larazotide use for 'leaky gut,' metabolic health, and anti-aging. No peer-reviewed trial data supports these applications. No established dose exists for any off-label indication. Sources are vendor and wellness blogs only — not authoritative and not cited.

synergies.rationale: Educational/mechanistic comparison only: larazotide acts extracellularly to block zonulin-mediated tight junction opening, while BPC-157 (studied in rodent models) is proposed to promote mucosal repair, angiogenesis, and nitric oxide pathway modulation in the gut wall. The two mechanisms target distinct nodes (barrier protection vs. proposed mucosal healing). This combination has never been evaluated in any animal or human study; the rationale is inferred from separate preclinical lines of research.
synergies.caution_notes: No co-administration data exists in humans or animals. BPC-157 is itself an unapproved research peptide. Combining two investigational compounds without clinical data is not a validated approach and is not a recommended protocol. Educational framing only; discuss any compound use with a qualified clinician.

synergies.rationale: Educational/mechanistic comparison only: KPV (Lys-Pro-Val), a C-terminal tripeptide of alpha-MSH, shows anti-inflammatory activity in gut epithelial models (reduced TNF-alpha and NF-kB signaling in vitro). Because larazotide is proposed to limit paracellular antigen translocation and KPV is proposed to dampen downstream mucosal inflammation, the two could theoretically address different points in the permeability-inflammation cycle. Entirely mechanistic; no co-administration study has been conducted.
synergies.caution_notes: KPV has no human clinical trial data for gut indications. Combination with larazotide is purely theoretical, neither agent is FDA-approved, and this is not a clinical recommendation. Educational framing only.

synergies.rationale: Educational context: in all larazotide celiac RCTs, the compound was studied strictly as an adjunct to a gluten-free diet (GFD), never as a replacement. The GFD removes the primary trigger (gliadin) while larazotide was hypothesized to reduce symptom burden from inadvertent residual gluten exposure. This is the only co-intervention with direct Phase 2 RCT context, though the larger Phase 3 trial was discontinued for futility.
synergies.caution_notes: Relevant only in the context of diagnosed celiac disease / gluten sensitivity under clinician care — not a general wellness recommendation. A gluten-free diet remains the standard of care; larazotide did not demonstrate confirmed added benefit at Phase 3 scale. Not a prescribing recommendation. Combination observed as background therapy in trials rather than the direct randomized intervention; graded HUMAN_OBS pending a direct citation.

name: Epitalon
short_description: Epitalon is a synthetic tetrapeptide (Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly) derived from epithalamin, a polypeptide extract of the bovine pineal gland. It has been studied primarily by Russian researchers for over 25 years for its putative telomerase-activating, antioxidant, circadian-restorative, and geroprotective properties. It is NOT FDA-approved, has no established systemic human therapeutic dose from controlled trials, and remains a research-only compound outside regulated clinical systems. Educational/tracking use only — not a prescription or recommendation.
mechanism: Epitalon is proposed to act through multiple complementary pathways: (1) Telomerase activation — it is reported to bind DNA promoter regions and upregulate hTERT expression in human somatic cells, resulting in telomere elongation and extension of replicative lifespan beyond the Hayflick limit in cell-culture models; (2) Epigenetic remodeling — proposed binding to linker histones H1/3 and H1/6 at DNA-interacting sites, increasing chromatin accessibility and modulating gene expression in aging cells; (3) Antioxidant defense — proposed activation of the Keap1/Nrf2 pathway, upregulating SOD-1, catalase, and NQO1, and reducing intracellular ROS; (4) Pineal/circadian restoration — proposed upregulation of AANAT and pCREB in pinealocytes, enhancing melatonin synthesis and restoring circadian melatonin rhythm in aged organisms; (5) Immune modulation — reported increases in IL-2 mRNA expression and normalization of CD4+/CD8+ T-cell ratios in aged organisms. Most mechanistic data derive from in vitro cell culture and animal studies. Clinical relevance in humans has not been established in adequately powered randomized controlled trials. A 2025 in vitro study (Biogerontology) found Epitalon activates ALT (Alternative Lengthening of Telomeres) in breast cancer cell lines while activating telomerase in normal cells — a differential effect whose human clinical significance remains unresolved and which raises a theoretical oncologic concern. The complete mechanism and stereochemical requirements are not fully characterized — eight stereoisomeric forms exist, only the all-L form has been studied.
monitoring_notes: No validated clinical monitoring protocol exists for Epitalon; the following are precautionary notes given the mechanism and evidence gaps, not a treatment plan.

Telomerase/ALT activation: theoretical concern for promotion of pre-existing malignant or pre-malignant cells. Age-appropriate baseline cancer screening is prudent before any use; ongoing oncologic surveillance advisable during prolonged use.
Melatonin pathway: if co-administering exogenous melatonin or other circadian-active agents, monitor for excessive somnolence or disrupted circadian entrainment.
Immune modulation: with proposed IL-2 upregulation, individuals with autoimmune conditions or on immunosuppressants may warrant immune-marker monitoring (CBC, CRP, relevant panels).
No human PK data exist for subcutaneous Epitalon — half-life, bioavailability, and clearance are not established.
No drug-interaction studies have been published. Clinician review is required before combining with immunosuppressants, immunostimulants, oncologic agents, or hormonal therapies.
Stereoisomeric purity matters — only the all-L form has been studied; impure or racemized preparations have unknown safety profiles.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy — telomerase activation in cancer cells is a hallmark of tumor immortalization; a 2025 in vitro study (Biogerontology) demonstrated Epitalon activates ALT (Alternative Lengthening of Telomeres) in breast cancer cell lines, which is mechanistically concerning in the context of active cancer.
Pregnancy and breastfeeding — no safety data exist; avoid in the absence of any human reproductive toxicology studies.
Pediatric populations — no pediatric data; growth and developmental implications of telomerase activation in children are unknown.
relative_contraindications: Personal or strong family history of cancer, particularly cancers with known telomerase reactivation — theoretical risk warrants clinician discussion before any use.
Active autoimmune disease — proposed IL-2 upregulation/immune modulation could theoretically exacerbate autoimmune activity; no human data characterize this risk.
Concurrent immunosuppressive therapy (e.g., post-organ transplant) — immune-modulating effects are unstudied in this context.
Hereditary cancer syndromes (e.g., BRCA1/2, Lynch syndrome, Li-Fraumeni) — heightened theoretical oncologic concern from telomerase activation.
Known hypersensitivity to any component of the peptide preparation.
common_side_effects: No systematic adverse-event data from adequately powered human studies exist. The 2025 IJMS review (Araj et al.) explicitly states safety information is missing and recommends short- and long-term toxicity studies.
Injection-site reactions (erythema, swelling, mild pain) — reported anecdotally for subcutaneous administration; not characterized in clinical trials.

No significant adverse events were reported in the retinitis pigmentosa study (n=162, parabulbar) or melatonin-normalization study (n=75, sublingual), but these were small, non-blinded, single-institution studies with limited follow-up.
serious_adverse_events: Theoretical oncologic risk from telomerase/ALT activation in pre-malignant or malignant cells — a 2025 Biogerontology in vitro study found Epitalon activated ALT in breast cancer cell lines (and hTERT/telomerase in normal cells). Human clinical significance is unknown but the concern is mechanistically grounded.
No serious adverse events have been formally documented in published literature; however, the absence of large-scale human safety studies means serious adverse events cannot be ruled out.
Unknown long-term effects of systemic telomerase activation in humans — genotoxicity and carcinogenicity studies have not been published, as explicitly noted by Araj et al. (2025).

dose_references.context: Parabulbar (periocular) injection in a human study of 162 patients with retinitis pigmentosa. Dose 5.0 mcg per eye for 10 consecutive days, as cited in the 2025 IJMS review of Epitalon literature (which cites the original Khavinson-group publication). Positive effect on visual acuity/field reported in ~90% of treated patients; no adverse effects reported. Non-blinded, single-institution, not independently replicated.
