Applies exclusively to ophthalmic use and cannot be extrapolated to systemic use. Educational reference only — not a recommendation.

dose_references.context: Sublingual administration in a human study of 75 women examining circadian melatonin restoration. Dose 0.5 mg/day for 20 days as cited in the 2025 IJMS review; reported ~1.6-fold increase in urinary 6-sulfatoxymelatonin. Design and blinding status not fully characterized in available abstracts.

citation.title: Overview of Epitalon — Highly Bioactive Pineal Tetrapeptide with Promising Properties

dose_references.notes: Sublingual route. Small (n=75), single-institution, blinding unconfirmed, not independently replicated. Source: cited within Araj et al. 2025 IJMS review. Applies to a melatonin-normalization context only and cannot be generalized to systemic longevity or telomere endpoints. Educational reference only — not a recommendation.

dose_references.context: Epithalamin (the parent polypeptide EXTRACT, a distinct compound from synthetic tetrapeptide Epitalon) given IM in a human observational cohort of ~70 elderly adults with cardiovascular disease — 10 mg as 5 injections over ~10 days, repeated every 6 months for 3 years, with 9-year follow-up; reported 28% lower total and ~2-fold lower cardiovascular mortality (Korkushko et al. 2006, via Araj et al. 2025). Numeric dose NULLED for the Epitalon entry because it applies to a different compound (epithalamin extract), not synthetic AEDG Epitalon.
dose_references.frequency: 5 injections over ~10 days, every 6 months for 3 years (epithalamin extract, not Epitalon)

citation.title: Overview of Epitalon — Highly Bioactive Pineal Tetrapeptide with Promising Properties

dose_references.notes: AUDITOR NOTE: dose number nulled and is_human_data set false. This human data describes EPITHALAMIN (complex polypeptide extract), NOT the synthetic tetrapeptide Epitalon (AEDG). The two are distinct compounds; extrapolation to Epitalon is not scientifically validated. Non-blinded, single-institution, unreplicated. Retained only as a cross-reference, not as an Epitalon dose. Source: Korkushko et al. 2006 via Araj et al. 2025 IJMS review.

dose_references.context: Community-reported subcutaneous protocols circulating in longevity/biohacker communities. No clinical trial supports these regimens. Reports describe ~5–10 mg/day SC for 10–20 consecutive days, 1–2 cycles per year. NOT derived from peer-reviewed human dose-finding studies.
dose_references.frequency: reportedly daily for 10–20 days, 1–2 cycles per year (anecdotal)
dose_references.notes: No established human therapeutic dose exists from controlled trials. Community SC regimens are widely circulated but unsupported by peer-reviewed human dose-finding or safety data. No validated subcutaneous PK profile exists. Do NOT present as a recommendation — educational/tracking context only.

synergies.rationale: Thymalin is a thymic polypeptide preparation with proposed immunomodulatory effects on T-lymphocyte maturation. It is sometimes discussed alongside Epitalon because the two are proposed to act on complementary systems — thymic immune reconstitution versus pineal/melatonin and telomere maintenance. Khavinson-group observational human cohort data (266 subjects >60 y) reported lower multi-year mortality with combined peptide use; however, that human data used the parent EXTRACT epithalamin (not synthetic Epitalon), was non-blinded and unreplicated, and the mechanism of interaction is not characterized. This is presented for education only, not as a protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: The supporting human mortality data used the parent extract epithalamin, NOT synthetic Epitalon, and is single-institution, non-blinded, and unreplicated — so it is not valid human evidence for an Epitalon+Thymalin pairing. Combined immune modulation could be relevant in autoimmune or post-transplant contexts. Clinician review required. Not a recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Epitalon is proposed to upregulate endogenous melatonin synthesis via AANAT/pCREB activation; exogenous melatonin shares antioxidant (ROS-reducing) mechanisms. The pairing is mechanistically plausible for circadian-rhythm discussion in aged individuals with reduced melatonin output. No human or animal co-administration study has been published.
synergies.caution_notes: Additive melatonin-pathway effects are theoretical only. Excessive melatonin signaling can affect reproductive hormones and circadian entrainment. No pharmacokinetic interaction data exist. Educational rationale, not a recommendation.

synergies.rationale: GHK-Cu (copper tripeptide) is reported to activate Nrf2-pathway antioxidant genes and promote tissue remodeling; Epitalon is also proposed to activate Nrf2 targets (SOD-1, catalase, NQO1). Both are short peptides with putative regulatory activity, making parallel antioxidant/anti-senescence mechanisms a coherent discussion point. No human or animal co-administration study has been published.
synergies.caution_notes: Parallel Nrf2 activation from two agents has not been studied for additive or synergistic toxicity. Evidence is entirely mechanistic — no in vivo pairing data exist. Educational rationale, not a recommendation.

synergies.rationale: NAD+ precursors support sirtuin activity, mitochondrial function, and DNA repair — distinct aging-hallmark pathways from Epitalon's proposed telomere/epigenetic roles. The non-overlapping-mechanism framing is the common educational rationale for discussing them together. No co-administration human or animal trial data exist.
synergies.caution_notes: Entirely mechanistic rationale. Long-term safety of combining a putative telomerase activator with NAD+ precursors is unevaluated. In cancer biology, both NAD+ elevation and telomerase/ALT activation can theoretically support tumor-cell survival — this theoretical oncologic risk is unresolved. Clinician review advised. Not a recommendation.

name: Humanin
short_description: Humanin is a 24-amino acid mitochondrial-derived peptide (MDP) encoded within the 16S rRNA gene of mitochondrial DNA. Discovered in 2001, it is an endogenously produced cytoprotective and anti-apoptotic signaling peptide. Circulating levels decline with age in multiple species and are inversely associated with Alzheimer's disease, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease in observational human studies. All interventional efficacy and dose data derive from preclinical (animal and cell culture) models. No FDA-approved therapeutic application exists, and no human clinical trials of exogenous Humanin administration have been completed as of mid-2026. This is a research-only compound; no validated human dose exists.
mechanism: Humanin acts through both extracellular and intracellular pathways. Extracellularly, it binds a heterotrimeric receptor complex (CNTFR/WSX-1/gp130), activating JAK2/STAT3, AKT/PI3K, and ERK1/2 pro-survival signaling cascades. It also binds formyl peptide receptor-like 1 (FPRL1/FPR2), further activating ERK1/2 survival pathways. Intracellularly, it inhibits pro-apoptotic proteins BAX, tBID, and BimEL, preventing mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization and cytochrome c release. It also blocks IGFBP-3 nuclear translocation, suppressing a parallel apoptosis pathway. Additional mechanisms include: enhancement of chaperone-mediated autophagy for protein quality control, reduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and restoration of mitochondrial membrane potential, stimulation of AMPK and endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) in cardiac tissue, and modulation of hypothalamic signaling to improve peripheral insulin sensitivity via STAT3. The peptide interacts with amyloid-beta, directly inhibiting toxic fibril formation in vitro. Overexpression in C. elegans extended lifespan through FOXO/daf-16-dependent signaling, suggesting conservation of longevity-related insulin/IGF-1 pathway interaction.
monitoring_notes: No validated clinical monitoring protocol exists for exogenous Humanin administration in humans, as no human therapeutic trials have been completed.

Given Humanin's antagonistic relationship with IGF-1 signaling (reduced circulating IGF-1 in animal models), IGF-1 is a theoretically relevant biomarker if exogenous use is undertaken under medical supervision.
Fasting glucose and HbA1c are relevant given the peptide's role in insulin sensitivity and pancreatic beta-cell biology — particularly in individuals with pre-existing insulin resistance or T2DM.

Baseline and periodic cancer screening is prudent given documented pro-tumorigenic effects of exogenous Humanin in triple-negative breast cancer animal models and its role in gastric cancer chemoresistance.
Native Humanin has an estimated plasma half-life of approximately 5-20 minutes in animal models; analogs like HNG exhibit extended activity. Human pharmacokinetic data does not exist.
CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel at baseline are reasonable precautionary measures before initiating any experimental peptide, under clinician supervision.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy of any type: animal studies demonstrate Humanin promotes tumor progression and lung metastasis in triple-negative breast cancer models, and Humanin overexpression is associated with chemoresistance in gastric cancer and glioblastoma. Exogenous Humanin in any patient with active cancer is contraindicated on preclinical evidence.

Concurrent cytotoxic chemotherapy: Humanin has been shown in animal models to impair the anti-tumor and anti-metastatic efficacy of doxorubicin and bortezomib in cancer-bearing subjects, even while protecting non-malignant tissue. The risk of compromising chemotherapy response is unacceptable.
relative_contraindications: History of malignancy (especially breast cancer, gastric cancer, glioblastoma): the pro-tumorigenic signal in preclinical cancer models warrants extreme caution even in remission, where occult residual cells may exist.
Strong family or personal history of hormone-sensitive or treatment-resistant cancers: theoretical risk from sustained anti-apoptotic signaling across rapidly dividing tissue compartments.
Pregnancy and lactation: no safety data exists; anti-apoptotic and IGF-1 pathway effects during fetal development are unknown.
Pediatric and adolescent use: Humanin overexpression during developmental periods impedes growth and reproductive output in animal models (via IGF-1 pathway antagonism).
Severe hepatic or renal impairment: peptide clearance and metabolite safety are uncharacterized in organ dysfunction.
Concurrent immunosuppressive therapy: Humanin's anti-inflammatory and immune-modulating effects may compound immunosuppression in unpredictable ways.
common_side_effects: No human clinical trial data documents side effects. The following are theoretical or derived from animal models.
Injection site reactions (redness, minor swelling, bruising): common to peptide injections generally, though not specifically documented for Humanin in humans.
Potential appetite or metabolic changes: Humanin modulates hypothalamic STAT3 and IGF-1 signaling, which could theoretically affect appetite regulation.
serious_adverse_events: Tumor promotion and acceleration of metastasis: documented in triple-negative breast cancer mouse models at 10 mcg/mouse IP every other day; Humanin significantly accelerated tumor growth, promoted spontaneous lung metastases, and impaired doxorubicin efficacy (Ristic et al., Scientific Reports, 2020).

Chemotherapy resistance: overexpression of Humanin isoforms in gastric cancer clinical samples is associated with chemoresistance (DARU, 2014); Humanin facilitates chemoresistance in glioblastoma cells in vitro (Cancers/MDPI, 2023).

Growth suppression in developmental contexts: transgenic Humanin overexpression in animal models caused reduced body size and impaired reproductive output — relevance to human adolescent/pediatric exposure is unknown but concerning.
Unknown long-term consequences of sustained exogenous anti-apoptotic signaling on immune cancer surveillance in humans.

citation.title: Humanin Prevents Age-Related Cognitive Decline in Mice and is Associated with Improved Cognitive Age in Humans

dose_references.notes: HNG (S14G Humanin analog), 4 mg/kg IP bi-weekly in aged mice. Verified against Yen et al., Aging Cell (2018). Route recorded as 'other' (IP). No human dose established. The same paper includes human OBSERVATIONAL genetic data (SNP rs2854128 associated with cognitive aging) but involved NO Humanin intervention in humans — this dose row reflects the animal arm only.

dose_references.context: Community / self-experimentation use — subcutaneous injection of synthetic Humanin or HNG in humans. NO clinical trial data supports this route or any dose in humans.
dose_references.notes: No established human therapeutic dose. No completed human clinical trial of exogenous Humanin administration exists as of mid-2026. Any dose circulating in community forums is unsourced. Subcutaneous is the presumed self-administration route based on peptide-class convention, not on any pharmacokinetic data specific to Humanin in humans. FLAG: research-only compound; ranges intentionally null.

synergies.rationale: MOTS-c and Humanin are both mitochondrial-derived peptides (MDPs) encoded in the mitochondrial genome but operate through largely complementary mechanisms. Humanin emphasizes neuroprotection and anti-apoptosis via gp130/STAT3 and BAX inhibition; MOTS-c functions as a metabolic regulator activating AMPK and SIRT1 and is described as an 'exercise mimetic' associated with improved insulin sensitivity in preclinical work. Both show age-dependent plasma decline in human observational studies. This complementarity is mechanistically plausible and supported only by animal data. This is educational context describing biological rationale, not a protocol or dosing recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: Combined anti-apoptotic signaling from multiple MDPs has not been assessed for safety in humans. Compound anti-apoptotic burden is theoretically concerning in the context of existing or occult malignancy. No human dosing guidance exists for either compound alone, let alone in combination. Educational only — not a protocol recommendation.

synergies.rationale: SHLP2, also encoded in the mitochondrial 16S rRNA locus, has been described with chaperone-like activity and reduction of mtDNA oxidation in preclinical models. Review literature on MDPs in aging discusses the possibility that these peptides act within a coordinated mitochondrial stress-response program. This is a biological-rationale description drawn from animal and review data, not a recommendation to combine them.
synergies.caution_notes: SHLP2 has a reported inverse association with prostate cancer risk in observational human data, but the therapeutic implications of exogenous SHLP2 administration are unknown. No human clinical trial data for SHLP2 administration exists. Combination safety in humans is entirely uncharacterized. Educational only.

synergies.rationale: NAD+ precursors (nicotinamide mononucleotide, nicotinamide riboside) support NAD-dependent sirtuin activity and mitochondrial biogenesis via PGC-1alpha — a node of mitochondrial biology distinct from but converging with pathways Humanin engages downstream (STAT3/AKT cytoprotection). The shared target of age-related mitochondrial dysfunction is the basis for theoretical interest. This is speculative mechanistic extrapolation; no study has combined exogenous Humanin with NAD+ precursors.
synergies.caution_notes: Human RCT data supports NMN/NR for some metabolic endpoints, but not in combination with Humanin. The combination rationale is speculative extrapolation only. No joint safety or efficacy data exists. Educational only — not a protocol recommendation.

name: SS-31 (Elamipretide)
short_description: Elamipretide (SS-31, brand name FORZINITY) is a synthetic tetrapeptide that selectively concentrates in the inner mitochondrial membrane, where it binds cardiolipin to stabilize cristae architecture, enhance electron transport chain supercomplex assembly, and reduce reactive oxygen species production. It received FDA accelerated approval on September 19, 2025 as the first mitochondria-targeted therapeutic approved in the United States, indicated for Barth syndrome in adult and pediatric patients weighing at least 30 kg. Additional indications — heart failure, primary mitochondrial myopathy, dry AMD, and aging-related mitochondrial dysfunction — have been or are being studied in human trials, but elamipretide is NOT approved for any of these and remains investigational outside Barth syndrome. Off-label use in longevity and performance contexts is investigational and not supported by phase 3 efficacy data.
mechanism: Elamipretide (D-Arg-Dmt-Lys-Phe-NH2) is a cell-permeable tetrapeptide that selectively accumulates in the inner mitochondrial membrane driven by electrostatic attraction to negatively charged cardiolipin. Binding stabilizes cardiolipin against oxidative peroxidation, preserves mitochondrial cristae curvature, and supports assembly and stability of respiratory supercomplexes (Complexes I, III, IV). These actions collectively reduce electron leak and reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, maintain cytochrome c association with the inner membrane (limiting apoptotic signaling), and improve ATP synthesis efficiency. Additional demonstrated mechanisms include inhibition of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP), reduction of age-related protein S-glutathionylation on the adenine nucleotide translocator (ANT), and consequent improvement of ADP uptake and mitochondrial ADP sensitivity. Unlike mitochondria-targeted antioxidants (e.g., MitoQ), elamipretide does not carry a large lipophilic cationic adduct, which may limit membrane disruption at higher concentrations.
monitoring_notes: Renal function (eGFR): dose reduction to 20 mg/day required if eGFR <30 mL/min (not on dialysis). Insufficient data for patients on dialysis — no dosing recommendation per FDA label.

Eosinophil count: eosinophilia observed in clinical trials; absolute eosinophil counts peaked at approximately 90 days and returned to baseline within 6-12 months. Monitor CBC periodically in long-term use.

Hypersensitivity surveillance: skin reactions (rash, eczematous dermatitis) and respiratory hypersensitivity reactions have been reported, ranging from mild to serious. Reactions may occur within minutes to months of initiation. Educate patients to report hives, rash, or respiratory symptoms promptly.
Injection site assessment: erythema, induration, pruritus, and pain are the most common adverse events. Inspect injection sites at follow-up; rotate daily per label.

Mitochondrial disease-specific monitoring: patients with primary mitochondrial myopathy or Barth syndrome should have cardiac function assessed periodically (echocardiogram), as cardiomyopathy is a primary disease feature.
Pregnancy status: no human safety data in pregnancy. Confirm pregnancy status in individuals of childbearing potential before initiating.
Product storage and handling: opened FORZINITY vials must be used within 8 days; store refrigerated (2-8C) or at room temperature (20-25C) after first opening.
absolute_contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity to elamipretide or any excipient in the formulation (per FDA label)
Neonates: FORZINITY contains benzyl alcohol 20 mg/mL, which has caused fatal gasping syndrome and metabolic acidosis in low-birth-weight and preterm neonates (per FDA label)
relative_contraindications: Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30 mL/min, not on dialysis): requires dose reduction to 20 mg/day; use with caution and monitor
Patients on dialysis: insufficient pharmacokinetic data; no dosing recommendation available
Pregnancy: no adequate human safety data; human risk unknown
Lactation: insufficient data on excretion in breast milk; clinical risk unknown
Pediatric patients weighing <30 kg: not studied; outside the FDA-approved weight band
History of significant allergic reactions to peptide drugs or injectable biologics: heightened monitoring warranted given hypersensitivity potential
common_side_effects: Injection site erythema (very common in elamipretide-treated patients vs placebo in TAZPOWER)
Injection site pain
Injection site induration
Injection site pruritus
Injection site bruising
Headache
Eosinophilia (transient, typically resolving within 6-12 months of treatment)
serious_adverse_events: Serious hypersensitivity reactions (including urticaria, eczematous dermatitis, respiratory symptoms): may require emergency intervention and permanent discontinuation

Benzyl alcohol toxicity in neonates: an absolute contraindication — gasping syndrome, metabolic acidosis, CNS depression, and cardiovascular collapse have been fatal in premature/low-birth-weight neonates exposed to benzyl alcohol-containing products

dose_references.notes: Number nulled per audit policy: this is animal (mouse) data and the mg/kg value (3 mg/kg in original) cannot be extrapolated to human dosing, so no numeric range is surfaced. Improved cardiac ejection fraction, global longitudinal strain, and skeletal muscle force in aged mice without detectable change in epigenetic/transcriptomic aging clocks. Referenced for mechanistic context only.

synergies.rationale: Complementary, non-overlapping mitochondrial support pathways discussed in an educational context. Elamipretide stabilizes cardiolipin and electron transport chain supercomplex architecture (a structural/membrane target), while NAD+ precursors replenish the NAD+ pool that serves as an electron carrier and cofactor for Complex I. Both converge on oxidative phosphorylation efficiency and ROS reduction through distinct mechanisms.
synergies.caution_notes: No human combination trial has been published; rationale is theoretical. Monitor for additive GI effects (NAD+ precursors may cause flushing, nausea). Educational context only — NOT a prescriptive recommendation, and elamipretide is only FDA-approved for Barth syndrome.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic pairing: CoQ10 is the electron carrier between Complexes I/II and Complex III. Elamipretide stabilizes the supercomplex architecture through which CoQ10 operates, while exogenous CoQ10/ubiquinol supports the substrate pool. The two act at different points (membrane architecture vs. electron carrier).
synergies.caution_notes: No human combination RCT data. Additive antioxidant effects are plausible but unquantified. Blood CoQ10 levels are not routinely monitored. Educational context only — not a protocol recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Educational framing: exercise is the best-validated stimulus for mitochondrial biogenesis (via PGC-1alpha) and is a complementary non-pharmacologic intervention. Elamipretide targets existing dysfunctional mitochondria (structural stabilization) while exercise promotes de novo mitochondrial content.
synergies.caution_notes: No human trial has prospectively paired elamipretide with supervised exercise. Patients with Barth syndrome or primary mitochondrial myopathy may have exercise intolerance; any exercise program in these populations requires physician supervision and individualization.

synergies.rationale: Speculative, community-discussed pairing presented only for educational completeness. BPC-157 is an investigational cytoprotective pentapeptide with proposed effects on NO-synthase pathways, angiogenesis, and tissue repair; animal models suggest possible indirect mitochondrial benefit via improved perfusion and reduced ischemia-reperfusion injury. Any mechanistic overlap with elamipretide is hypothetical.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded to ANECDOTAL: this pairing is discussed in community contexts and is NOT supported by clinical literature. BPC-157 lacks FDA approval and has no human RCT data for any indication. Combining two investigational/off-label agents compounds safety and interaction uncertainty. Not a recommendation; discuss any such use with a clinician.

name: Thymalin
short_description: Thymalin is a low-molecular-weight polypeptide complex extracted from calf thymus, developed in the 1970s at the Soviet Military Medical Academy. It is registered as a pharmaceutical drug in Russia for immune restoration and is studied for longevity effects via thymic-axis rejuvenation. It is NOT FDA-approved and is classified as an unapproved new drug in the United States. Human efficacy and dosing evidence comes only from non-blinded, non-randomized Russian clinical observation programs plus mechanistic/in vitro work; no Western-standard RCT exists.
mechanism: Thymalin is a thymic extract containing active dipeptides KE (Lys-Glu) and EW (Glu-Trp). These dipeptides have been proposed to bind specific DNA promoter sequences (KE to GCGC; EW to GGAG) and modulate transcription of target genes including AKT1, AKT2, ACE2, CYSLTR1, and CHUK. In vitro, thymalin reduced pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α (reported 1.4–6-fold) in LPS-stimulated human PBMCs. In an in vitro human hematopoietic stem cell study, thymalin reduced CD44 and CD117 (stem/intermediate differentiation markers) ~2–3-fold while increasing CD28 (mature T-lymphocyte marker) ~6.8-fold, interpreted as promotion of naïve T-cell maturation. Reported mechanistic roles include KE-driven activation of macrophages, thymocytes, and neutrophils with induction of CD4/CD5 expression on thymic cells, and EW-associated ACE2-regulatory/vasoprotective properties. The proposed net effect is restoration of thymic immune competence that declines with age-related thymic involution. NOTE: most mechanistic claims derive from in vitro/cell work by a single research group and have not been independently replicated.
monitoring_notes: Baseline immunogram (CBC with differential, lymphocyte subset panel including CD4/CD8 ratio) is reasonable before any course to characterize immune status and provide a comparator, consistent with the practice described in Russian labeling.
Repeat immunogram some weeks after a course to assess response and inform a clinician's judgment on timing — not an instruction to re-dose.
Monitor for allergic/hypersensitivity reaction (urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis) at first injection and thereafter — thymalin is animal (bovine/calf-thymus)-derived.
In persons with known or suspected autoimmune disease, obtain rheumatology/immunology evaluation before any use; T-cell upregulation could theoretically destabilize autoimmune equilibrium.
No IGF-1 or insulin monitoring is specifically indicated (not a GH-axis compound); a general metabolic panel at baseline is reasonable for elderly users in longevity programs.

Thymalin is NOT FDA-approved; any US use occurs outside regulatory oversight. Clinician documentation of investigational/off-label rationale is essential. This app educates and tracks only and does not prescribe.
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to bovine-derived proteins or thymic peptide preparations (anaphylaxis risk from animal-source extract)
Pregnancy — no safety data; insufficient evidence to rule out fetal immunological effects
Active organ transplant recipients on immunosuppressive regimens — theoretical T-cell upregulation may counteract required immunosuppression and risk rejection
relative_contraindications: Active autoimmune disease (e.g., SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease) — T-cell stimulation may theoretically exacerbate activity; requires specialist risk-benefit assessment
Lactation — insufficient safety data
Concurrent immunosuppressive medications (calcineurin inhibitors, biologics, corticosteroids) — pharmacodynamic antagonism or unpredictable immune dysregulation possible
Active malignancy with immune-evasion biology — immune-stimulation effects incompletely characterized in oncology; consult oncologist
Children under 6 months — pediatric figures appear in Russian labeling but safety data are very limited by Western standards
common_side_effects: Injection-site reactions (pain, redness, swelling at IM site) — most commonly reported effect in Russian clinical experience
Mild allergic reactions (urticaria, pruritus) — listed in Russian prescribing information
serious_adverse_events: Anaphylaxis / severe hypersensitivity — theoretical given animal-protein source; no published controlled-study incidence rate
Autoimmune flare — theoretical; T-lymphocyte stimulation in subclinical/active autoimmune disease could trigger exacerbation; no controlled-trial incidence data
NOTE: Absence of large FDA-standard controlled safety trials means the true serious-adverse-event profile is not established by Western regulatory evidence standards

dose_references.notes: Numeric value nulled by auditor. The entry itself acknowledged the cited primary article does not state mg doses; auditor verified the PubMed abstract (14523363) states no milligram dose and describes a non-blinded, non-randomized open observation, not an RCT. The mg figure originates from secondary summaries, so no cited human study backs the number. dose_references.notes: No dose range establishable from anecdotal sources. SC route is not in the Russian prescribing information (IM only) and has no clinical trial support. Flagged ANECDOTAL; do not weight clinically.

synergies.rationale: Educational rationale: Epitalon is a synthetic tetrapeptide (Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly) related to the pineal bioregulator Epithalamin. Thymalin and pineal peptides target distinct age-related axes — Thymalin the thymic-immune/T-cell compartment, Epithalon/Epithalamin the pineal-neuroendocrine axis. In the Khavinson/Morozov elderly observation cohort, a combined annually-repeated arm reported larger mortality reduction (~4.1-fold vs controls) than Thymalin alone (~2.0–2.1-fold). The proposed mechanistic basis is thymus–pineal cross-talk during aging. This describes observed associations and a hypothesis, not a recommended regimen.
synergies.caution_notes: Evidence is exclusively from a single non-blinded, non-randomized Russian observation program with no extractable per-compound dosing in the primary publications and no independent RCT replication. Neither compound is FDA-approved. The mortality figures cannot be treated as a validated protocol. Educational context only — not a recommendation to combine or dose.

synergies.rationale: Educational/mechanistic rationale only: Thymosin Alpha-1 acts via TLR signaling on mature peripheral T-cell and antigen-presenting-cell activity, whereas Thymalin is proposed to act upstream at intrathymic hematopoietic/thymocyte differentiation (CD117→CD28 maturation in vitro). The two are sometimes discussed as addressing different stages of the thymic-immune axis. No clinical trial examines the combination.
synergies.caution_notes: No combination clinical data exist. Thymosin Alpha-1 is approved in some countries (not FDA-approved in the US). Stacking two immunostimulatory agents carries theoretical risk of immune overstimulation, especially with autoimmune predisposition. Educational context only — not a recommendation.

name: 5-Amino-1MQ
short_description: 5-Amino-1MQ is a selective, membrane-permeable small-molecule inhibitor of nicotinamide N-methyltransferase (NNMT). It is NOT a peptide by strict definition but is commonly cataloged alongside metabolic research compounds. It has demonstrated anti-obesity and muscle-regenerative effects in rodent models through NAD+ salvage pathway restoration. No human clinical trials have been published. Not FDA-approved for any indication.
mechanism: 5-Amino-1MQ competitively inhibits NNMT, a cytoplasmic enzyme highly expressed in white adipose tissue that normally consumes S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) to methylate nicotinamide, producing 1-methylnicotinamide (MNA) and S-adenosylhomocysteine. By blocking this reaction, the compound preserves intracellular pools of both SAM (a universal methyl donor supporting epigenetic regulation) and nicotinamide (the rate-limiting NAD+ precursor in the salvage pathway). The net result in preclinical models is elevated intracellular NAD+, suppressed de novo lipogenesis in adipocytes, increased energy expenditure, and improved insulin sensitivity. In aged skeletal muscle models, NNMT inhibition reactivated senescent muscle stem cells (satellite cells), improving fiber cross-sectional area and force output. Evidence base is animal and mechanistic only; human translation is unestablished.
monitoring_notes: No human clinical trial monitoring protocols exist. The following are precautionary, mechanism-based considerations to discuss with a clinician — not directives.
Baseline and periodic liver function tests (ALT, AST) may be prudent given that NNMT is predominantly expressed in the liver and systemic inhibition could affect hepatic NAD+ metabolism.
Baseline fasting glucose and insulin (or HOMA-IR) to establish metabolic context; NNMT inhibition affects insulin sensitivity in animal models.

Homocysteine levels: NNMT inhibition affects the SAM/SAH ratio and methylation flux; theoretical risk of altered homocysteine metabolism warrants baseline measurement, particularly with cardiovascular risk.
Lipid panel at baseline: preclinical studies showed plasma cholesterol reduction; monitoring for unexpected lipid changes is reasonable.
No validated therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) assay exists for 5-Amino-1MQ in human clinical use.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy or personal history of cancer: NNMT is overexpressed and functions as an oncogene in multiple cancer types (renal clear cell carcinoma, pancreatic adenocarcinoma, glioblastoma, diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, sarcoma). NNMT inhibition is under investigation as an anti-cancer strategy in these contexts, but NNMT downregulation paradoxically promotes tumor progression in hepatocellular carcinoma and pheochromocytoma. The net effect of systemic NNMT inhibition in known or occult malignancy is unknown and potentially harmful. Source: PMC8956669 (Cell Death & Disease, 2022).
Pregnancy and breastfeeding: no reproductive or developmental safety data exists in any species for this compound.
Use outside of formal Institutional Review Board (IRB)-approved research protocols is not endorsed by any regulatory authority; not FDA-approved for any indication.
relative_contraindications: Pre-existing hepatic disease (hepatitis, cirrhosis, significantly elevated baseline transaminases): NNMT is predominantly expressed in the liver; systemic inhibition effects on hepatic function are insufficiently characterized.
Chronic kidney disease: renal clearance of this compound and any metabolites has not been characterized in humans.
Personal or family history of pheochromocytoma: NNMT downregulation may promote tumor progression in this context (mechanistic concern from cancer biology literature).
Concurrent use of other NAD+ pathway modulators (NMN, NR, niacin): additive effects on NAD+ homeostasis are unstudied.
Immunocompromised states: NNMT modulates immune-related pathways and the tumor microenvironment; effects in immunocompromised individuals are unknown.
common_side_effects: No human clinical trial adverse event data exists. The following are derived from preclinical animal studies (where no adverse effects were observed at tested doses) and community anecdotal reports (lowest evidentiary weight).
Mild gastrointestinal discomfort (nausea, abdominal cramping) — anecdotally reported with oral use, particularly on an empty stomach. Not documented in any peer-reviewed human or animal study.
Headache — anecdotally reported by community users. No clinical trial data.
serious_adverse_events: Unknown — no human safety study has been conducted. Serious adverse events cannot be characterized or excluded.

Theoretical oncologic risk: systemic NNMT inhibition in individuals with certain occult or active malignancies (particularly hepatocellular carcinoma or pheochromocytoma) may have paradoxical tumor-promoting effects based on NNMT's context-dependent role in cancer biology (PMC8956669).

Theoretical methylation disruption: NNMT inhibition increases intracellular SAM; excess SAM availability could theoretically alter genome-wide DNA and histone methylation. Long-term epigenetic consequences are unstudied.
Long-term safety in humans is completely unknown; no studies beyond short-term rodent protocols (11 days to 3 weeks) have been published.

dose_references.notes: Oral bioavailability reported as 38.4% with a half-life of 6.9 hours (oral) and 3.8 hours (IV) in rats. This PK data supports oral route feasibility in rodents only. No equivalent human PK data exists. Source: Development and Validation of LC-MS/MS Assay for 5-Amino-1-Methyl Quinolinium in Rat Plasma, Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis, 2021.

dose_references.context: Community anecdotal oral use — NOT derived from any clinical trial or peer-reviewed human study. Dose figures circulate in biohacking and compounding wellness communities only and carry the lowest evidentiary weight.
dose_references.notes: Community sources informally cite figures derived from unvalidated allometric scaling of the 20 mg/kg rodent dose. This extrapolation has NOT been validated in any published human PK, safety, or efficacy study. No established human dose exists. Numeric range deliberately nulled because it is not backed by any cited human study. Educational/flagging context only; must NOT be presented as a recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Mechanistically complementary within the NAD+ and mitochondrial bioenergetics axis. MOTS-c is a mitochondria-derived peptide that activates AMPK, upregulates NAMPT (the rate-limiting enzyme in NAD+ salvage), promotes GLUT4 translocation, and increases mitochondrial biogenesis. 5-Amino-1MQ acts upstream by inhibiting NNMT-mediated nicotinamide drain, thereby preserving the NAD+ precursor pool. The two compounds theoretically address adjacent but non-overlapping nodes in the same NAD+ salvage pathway. Purely mechanistic hypothesis — no co-administration data in any species.
synergies.caution_notes: No co-administration safety or efficacy data exists in any published study. Additive NAD+ elevation could in theory affect sirtuin and PARP activity unpredictably at higher combined exposures. Educational framing only; not a protocol recommendation. Discuss any combination with a qualified clinician.

synergies.rationale: NMN is an NAD+ precursor that increases NAD+ synthesis via the salvage pathway, while 5-Amino-1MQ reduces consumption of the same pathway's substrate (nicotinamide) by inhibiting NNMT. In theory, pairing an NAD+ precursor with an NNMT inhibitor addresses both synthesis and catabolism nodes. This dual-mechanism hypothesis is discussed mechanistically in an NNMT metabolic-syndrome review (Frontiers in Pharmacology, 2024, PMC11196770).
synergies.caution_notes: No co-administration study exists. Supraphysiologic NAD+ levels have unknown long-term consequences. Some literature raises a theoretical concern that excessive NAD+ availability could support tumor bioenergetics in occult malignancy — warranting physician oversight. Educational framing only; not a protocol recommendation.

synergies.rationale: GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce caloric intake via central satiety signaling and delayed gastric emptying, whereas 5-Amino-1MQ acts peripherally in adipose tissue by inhibiting NNMT to affect energy expenditure and lipogenesis. These are mechanistically orthogonal pathways — one reduces caloric input, the other affects metabolic output — which is why they are sometimes discussed as theoretically complementary. There is no published co-administration data for this combination in any species.
synergies.caution_notes: No safety or efficacy data for this combination exists. GLP-1 agonists carry class-specific risks (gastroparesis, personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2 contraindication, pancreatitis). 5-Amino-1MQ has no human safety data. Combining a prescription drug with a research-stage compound requires direct physician supervision. Educational framing only; not a protocol recommendation.

name: Selank
short_description: Selank is a synthetic heptapeptide anxiolytic and nootropic derived from the endogenous immunomodulator tuftsin. Approved in Russia (2009) for generalized anxiety disorder and neurasthenia; not FDA-approved. Evidence base is primarily Russian observational and small controlled human studies, with robust preclinical (animal/mechanistic) support. No established role in body recomposition or fat loss.
mechanism: Selank (Thr-Lys-Pro-Arg-Pro-Gly-Pro) is a metabolically stabilized synthetic analog of tuftsin, acting through multiple CNS pathways: (1) Modulation of GABAergic neurotransmission — affects gene expression of GABAergic components without direct benzodiazepine-site receptor agonism, distinguishing it from benzodiazepines (PMC4757669); (2) Enkephalinase inhibition — prolongs the half-life of endogenous enkephalins (met-enkephalin, leu-enkephalin), increasing endogenous opioid peptide activity and contributing to anxiolytic effect (PubMed 30255741); (3) BDNF upregulation — elevates hippocampal BDNF expression in rodent models (PubMed 31625062); (4) Monoaminergic modulation — influences serotonin metabolism and dopaminergic tone in preclinical models; (5) Immunomodulation inherited from parent peptide tuftsin: modulates IL-6, IFN-alpha expression and Th1/Th2 cytokine balance (PubMed 19882898). Plasma half-life is extremely short (approximately 2–10 minutes), which favors intranasal delivery for direct CNS access.
monitoring_notes: Intranasal administration: monitor for local mucosal irritation, epistaxis, or allergic rhinitis symptoms at the application site.

No established biomarker monitoring protocol exists for Selank in clinical use outside Russia. In the absence of FDA approval and standardized guidelines, a clinician may baseline and periodically reassess validated anxiety scales (e.g., HAM-A, GAD-7) if tracking therapeutic response.
No specific laboratory monitoring (hepatic, renal, hematologic) has been associated with Selank in published human data; absence of data does not imply absence of risk for long-term use.

Immunomodulatory effects on IL-6 and IFN-alpha have been documented in animal and in-vitro studies; clinical significance in humans is unknown. Relevant for patients with autoimmune conditions or on immunosuppressive therapy.

Given the extremely short plasma half-life (approximately 2–10 minutes), systemic accumulation is unlikely with intranasal use; however, CNS effects may persist beyond measurable plasma levels due to receptor-level changes.
Long-term safety data beyond 14-day courses (the primary studied duration) do not exist in published literature.
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity or allergic reaction to Selank or any of its excipients (per Russian prescribing document).
Pregnancy — no safety data exist; contraindicated due to unknown fetal risk.
Breastfeeding — no safety data exist; contraindicated.
Patients under 18 years of age — not studied in pediatric populations.
relative_contraindications: Active or history of malignancy — theoretical concern given immunomodulatory effects on cytokine balance (IL-6, IFN-alpha); not studied in oncology populations.

Concurrent use of serotonergic agents (SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, tramadol) — Selank influences serotonin metabolism in preclinical models; additive serotonergic activity is mechanistically plausible. Serotonin syndrome has not been documented but cannot be excluded. Clinician oversight required.

Concurrent use of prescribed benzodiazepines — complex, potentially unpredictable GABA-A allosteric interaction (preclinical data show both enhancing and blocking effects on benzodiazepine activity depending on conditions); clinician oversight required for any dose adjustment, which must be managed by the prescriber.
Severe hepatic or renal impairment — no pharmacokinetic data in organ-impaired populations.
Autoimmune or inflammatory conditions on active immunosuppressive therapy — immunomodulatory cytokine effects are unstudied in this context.
Seizure disorders — GABAergic modulation is the primary mechanism; effect on seizure threshold is not established in humans.
Nasal pathology (polyps, deviated septum, active sinusitis) — may impair intranasal bioavailability and cause local irritation.
common_side_effects: Transient nasal irritation or burning at application site (intranasal route)
Mild headache
Dysgeusia (metallic or unusual taste) during or shortly after intranasal administration
Mild sedation or fatigue in some individuals (reported at clinical doses, less than benzodiazepines)
Mild nasal congestion
serious_adverse_events: Allergic hypersensitivity reactions — rare; anaphylaxis theoretically possible with any peptide and listed in precautionary labeling.
Serotonin syndrome — not documented in published literature but mechanistically plausible with concurrent serotonergic drugs; flagged as a monitoring concern.
Quality and sterility risks from unregulated compounded or research-grade formulations — not inherent to the molecule but a real-world safety hazard outside licensed pharmacy supply chains.
No serious adverse events were reported in the published 14-day human studies reviewed; however, long-term safety data are absent.

dose_references.notes: Subcutaneous injection of Selank is reported in online research communities. No published human clinical trial has studied this route; systemic bioavailability via sc injection is unstudied in humans. No dose can be reported. synergies.rationale: Semax (an ACTH4-7 analog) upregulates BDNF and acts primarily through dopaminergic/nootropic pathways, while Selank provides anxiolytic GABAergic modulation. The mechanistic complementarity — anxiolysis from Selank balancing potential stimulatory effects from Semax — is described in comparative preclinical literature, and both peptides were developed at the same Russian research institute. This is educational pharmacologic context only; no human combination trials exist.
synergies.caution_notes: No human RCT data on this combination. Both compounds affect monoaminergic/serotonergic tone; additive serotonergic effects are theoretically possible but unquantified. Not a protocol or recommendation — educational context only. Obtain clinician guidance before any use.

name: Semax
short_description: Semax is a synthetic heptapeptide (Met-Glu-His-Phe-Pro-Gly-Pro) derived from the ACTH(4-10) fragment of adrenocorticotropic hormone. It is approved in Russia for ischemic stroke and certain optic nerve conditions and has decades of Russian clinical use as a nootropic and neuroprotective agent. It is NOT approved by the FDA or EMA and is not an established therapeutic in the United States. All clinical evidence originates from Russian-language trials, many of which have methodologic limitations. It is not a scheduled substance in the US but is sold only as a research chemical or via compounding pharmacy under a licensed prescriber's order. This entry is educational only and does not constitute a dosing recommendation.
mechanism: Semax is an ACTH(4-10) fragment analogue with a Pro-Gly-Pro C-terminal extension that increases proteolytic stability and enables CNS delivery via intranasal administration. Its mechanism is not fully characterized but involves several complementary pathways: (1) Melanocortin receptor modulation — Semax interacts with central melanocortin receptors (MC4R and MC5R), the primary CNS receptors for ACTH-derived peptides; it does NOT activate MC2R (the adrenal ACTH receptor) and thus lacks corticosteroidogenic activity. Its precise agonist/antagonist profile at MC4R is debated, with some evidence of competitive antagonism vs alpha-MSH. (2) Neurotrophin upregulation — intranasal Semax rapidly increases BDNF mRNA (up to ~3-fold in hippocampus) and NGF in the hippocampus, brainstem, and cerebellum, along with phosphorylation of the TrkB receptor; these effects are region-specific (ANIMAL/MECHANISTIC data). (3) Monoaminergic modulation — increases striatal serotonin turnover and potentiates dopamine release under stimulant challenge in rodents, without directly elevating basal dopamine (ANIMAL data). (4) Enkephalin degradation inhibition — inhibits enkephalin-degrading enzymes, extending endogenous enkephalin activity. (5) Neuroprotection against ischemia — reduces nitric oxide over-generation in cortical ischemia and upregulates neurotrophins and their high-affinity receptors (TrkA, TrkC) in ischemic penumbral tissue. None of these mechanisms have been directly demonstrated in Western-standard human mechanistic studies.
monitoring_notes: Blood pressure: Semax has been reported to cause transient increases in heart rate and blood pressure in some subjects; baseline and periodic BP monitoring is prudent, especially with pre-existing hypertension.
Blood glucose: Semax may increase blood glucose; patients with diabetes or impaired fasting glucose should monitor glycemia during any use.

Neurological status: In the stroke/neurological-disease setting (the only human-evidence indications), source clinical trials used validated scales (NIHSS, Rankin, Rivermead). Self-monitoring is inadequate for clinical use.
Mood and anxiety: monitor for anxiety, agitation, or mood changes, particularly at initiation.

Seizure history: individuals with epilepsy or seizure disorders should not use Semax without close neurological supervision; preclinical data and translated product materials flag seizure history as a contraindication.
No established Western long-term safety data: Russian clinical data extends to roughly 10-30 day courses; no published Western studies evaluate safety beyond 30 days or in diverse populations.

Drug interactions with serotonergic agents: Semax modulates serotonin turnover in animal models (PMID 16362768); theoretical additive serotonergic activity with SSRIs, SNRIs, or MAOIs has not been formally characterized in humans.
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to Semax or any component of the formulation
Active seizure disorder or epilepsy (based on translated Russian prescribing materials and preclinical risk signals)
Pregnancy — no safety data; not evaluated in pregnant populations
Breastfeeding — no safety data; excretion into breast milk unknown
relative_contraindications: Uncontrolled hypertension (potential for transient BP elevation)
Diabetes mellitus or impaired glucose regulation (potential hyperglycemic effect)
Severe psychiatric conditions including acute psychosis or bipolar disorder with active mania (dopaminergic and serotonergic activation)
Concurrent use of serotonergic medications (SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs) — theoretical pharmacodynamic interaction based on animal serotonin-modulation data, not formally characterized in humans
Concurrent use of antipsychotics — melanocortin/dopamine pathway modulation could theoretically interact with dopamine receptor antagonists
Pediatric use — limited to Russian-approved formulations for age >=3 years (brand name Minisem); no Western pediatric data
common_side_effects: Nasal irritation or discomfort at the site of intranasal administration
Transient headache
Metallic or unusual taste (dysgeusia) following intranasal dosing
Mild anxiety or restlessness (particularly at higher doses or in susceptible individuals)
Transient blood pressure fluctuation
Minor mood changes (activation effect)
serious_adverse_events: Seizure activity in individuals with seizure predisposition (theoretical, based on preclinical signals and translated contraindication warnings — not documented in published Russian stroke trials at approved doses)
Severe hypertensive episodes (not documented in published trials at approved doses but plausible given reported BP-modulating effects)
Long-term (>30 day) safety is entirely unknown in non-Russian populations; no Western safety surveillance exists

dose_references.notes: Rat studies (PMID 16635254, PMID 17353092) reportedly used 50-250 mcg/kg intranasal, with BDNF increased in basal forebrain at 3 h and NGF/BDNF gene expression increased in hippocampus within 1 h. The numeric range has been nulled because it is animal-derived and cannot be extrapolated to a human dose; the original 50-250 mcg/kg figure is described here in prose only as study methodology, not as a usable dose. Educational/mechanistic reference only.

dose_references.context: Healthy human cognitive enhancement — figures cited from a Russian-language 15-year narrative review (not a systematic review or RCT).

citation.title: A nootropic adrenocorticotropin analog 4-10-semax (15 years experience in its design and study)

dose_references.notes: A dose range of 0.015-0.05 mg/kg intranasal was cited verbatim in a 15-year narrative review (PMID 9173745) claiming improvements in operative memory and attention in healthy adults under extreme conditions. Because the source is a Russian-language narrative summary with no controlled design, no defined cohort denominator, and no Western replication, the numeric range has been nulled and the evidence level downgraded from HUMAN_OBS to ANECDOTAL. The figure is retained in prose for transparency only and must not be treated as a validated human dose.

dose_references.context: Community/off-label intranasal use in the United States — no controlled trial data exists for community dosing patterns.
dose_references.notes: No established human dose exists outside Russian clinical literature. Community use patterns reported on forums and vendor sites carry no evidentiary weight and are not reproduced here as numbers. No dose should be inferred from these sources.

synergies.rationale: Selank is a synthetic analogue of the endogenous tetrapeptide tuftsin with reported anxiolytic properties attributed to GABAergic modulation and enkephalin-degradation inhibition (PMID 11550013). Educationally, Semax and Selank are sometimes discussed together because they are proposed to act on complementary CNS pathways: Semax is associated with neurotrophin (BDNF/NGF) and monoaminergic signaling, while Selank is associated with GABA and enkephalin tone. The mechanistic basis for any interaction is ANIMAL/MECHANISTIC only; no human study evaluates the combination, and this pairing is presented for understanding of mechanism, not as a protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: No human trial data for the combination. Both compounds may modulate serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems, so additive CNS effects are theoretically possible but unstudied. Neither is FDA-approved for any indication. This is not a recommended protocol; any use should be discussed with a licensed clinician.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 is a synthetic peptide reported in animal models to influence VEGF and growth-factor signaling in neural injury (ANIMAL evidence). The only basis for discussing it alongside Semax is mechanistic: the two are studied via distinct putative neuroprotective pathways (Semax via neurotrophin/melanocortin signaling; BPC-157 via VEGF/nitric-oxide modulation in animals). This is presented purely to explain mechanism. No human data exists for the combination, and no efficacy or safety conclusion can be drawn.
synergies.caution_notes: Purely mechanistic, animal-derived rationale. No human safety or efficacy data for this combination. Both compounds lack FDA approval for any indication. Not a recommended protocol; discuss with a licensed clinician before any use.

name: Dihexa
short_description: Dihexa (PNB-0408) is a synthetic, metabolically stabilized analog of angiotensin IV developed at Washington State University. It is a research-only compound — not FDA-approved, not approved by any regulatory agency, and never tested in registered human clinical trials. All efficacy data comes from rodent models. Its proposed mechanism involves potentiation of hepatocyte growth factor (HGF) binding to the c-Met receptor tyrosine kinase, promoting synaptogenesis in preclinical models. CRITICAL NOTE: The two foundational mechanistic papers (JPET 2012 and JPET 2014) establishing the HGF/c-MET mechanism were formally retracted in April 2025 due to data manipulation (image alteration) by a co-author. No established human therapeutic dose exists. Community dosing reports are anecdotal and carry no clinical validation.
mechanism: Dihexa is proposed to act as an allosteric potentiator of hepatocyte growth factor (HGF), enhancing its dimerization and binding affinity for the c-Met receptor tyrosine kinase. This amplification of HGF/c-Met downstream signaling (including the PI3K/AKT pathway) is hypothesized to promote synaptogenesis — the formation of new synaptic connections — in hippocampal and cortical neurons. Dihexa is a structural derivative of angiotensin IV, engineered for oral bioavailability and blood-brain barrier penetration via increased lipophilicity and substitution of proteolytically labile bonds. The compound demonstrates an unusually long serum half-life (~12.7 days in rodents following i.v. dosing), which creates accumulation risk with repeated use. INTEGRITY CAVEAT: Both the primary 2012 paper describing initial HGF/c-MET interactions (JPET 340:539-548) and the 2014 paper directly demonstrating c-Met phosphorylation by Dihexa (JPET 351:390-402) were formally retracted in April 2025; a third related 2013 paper (JPET 344:238-250) has an active Expression of Concern. The mechanistic basis thus rests on disputed data and independent replication by other groups is limited.
monitoring_notes: No human safety monitoring protocols exist for Dihexa; all items below are extrapolated from the compound's proposed mechanism and represent consensus-level precautions from clinician commentary, not evidence-based guidelines.

HGF/c-MET pathway activation: baseline and periodic cancer screening is prudent given the oncogenic potential of constitutive c-Met signaling. FDA-approved drugs capmatinib, tepotinib, and crizotinib work by blocking this exact pathway.
Liver function tests (LFTs): HGF is predominantly a hepatotrophic factor; monitor for hepatotoxicity, particularly at supratherapeutic exposures.
Complete blood count: one case of moderate neutropenia was observed in the fosgonimeton Phase 1 trial (deemed probably unrelated); monitor if using related HGF/c-MET-targeting compounds.

Long half-life (~12.7 days in rodents i.v.): drug accumulation is expected with repeated dosing; steady-state would not be reached for 6–8 weeks, and elimination after cessation may take 50–60 days. Any adverse effects could persist for months after stopping.
No established drug interaction data: avoid combination with other experimental growth-factor-pathway modulators without physician oversight.
No carcinogenicity studies have been conducted. Regular dermatologic and oncologic surveillance is prudent given the proposed proliferative mechanism.
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy of any type: HGF/c-MET signaling is a well-characterized driver of tumor proliferation, invasion, and metastasis across multiple cancer types (lung, gastric, head and neck, renal, breast, and others). Use in the setting of active cancer is contraindicated.
Known c-MET-amplified or c-MET-overexpressing tumors (e.g., c-MET exon 14 skipping mutations in NSCLC): direct mechanistic conflict with approved anti-c-MET cancer therapies.
Current treatment with c-MET inhibitors (capmatinib, tepotinib, crizotinib, cabozantinib): direct pharmacodynamic antagonism.
Pregnancy and breastfeeding: no safety data exist; HGF/c-MET signaling plays roles in placental development and lactation; risk unknown.
relative_contraindications: Personal history of malignancy: theoretical risk of promoting occult residual or recurrent disease via HGF/c-MET pathway upregulation.
Strong family history of c-MET-associated cancers (gastric, papillary renal cell, hepatocellular): hereditary MET mutation syndromes may increase susceptibility to pathway-driven tumor promotion.
Active autoimmune disease on immunosuppressive therapy: reduced immune cancer surveillance combined with HGF/c-MET-mediated proliferative signaling represents an unquantified compounded risk.
Hepatic impairment: HGF is hepatotrophic; pharmacokinetics in hepatic disease are unknown.
Pediatric use: no data; developing neuronal architecture may respond unpredictably to potent synaptogenic signaling.
common_side_effects: No controlled human adverse-event data exist for Dihexa itself.
Anecdotal community reports (not clinically validated) describe: headache, vivid or unusual dreams, fatigue, and transient mood changes.
Injection site pain and pruritus were the most common adverse events in the fosgonimeton (a structurally related prodrug) Phase 1 trial — not directly applicable to Dihexa.
serious_adverse_events: No formal serious adverse-event data for Dihexa in humans.

Theoretical serious risk: tumor promotion via sustained HGF/c-MET activation. This has not been observed in short-duration animal studies, but long-term carcinogenicity studies have not been conducted.
Drug accumulation toxicity: the exceptionally long half-life (~12.7 days in rodents) means repeated dosing without pharmacokinetic guidance could lead to unexpectedly high steady-state exposures.

The foundational mechanistic papers were retracted in 2025 due to data manipulation; the full safety and efficacy profile of the compound therefore rests on an incomplete and partially corrupted evidence base.

These are animal pharmacology figures only, not human therapeutic doses, and the source paper's data integrity is formally disputed.

citation.title: AngIV-Analog Dihexa Rescues Cognitive Impairment and Recovers Memory in the APP/PS1 Mouse via the PI3K/AKT Signaling Pathway

dose_references.notes: Two doses (1.44 and 2.88 mg/kg intragastric) in APP/PS1 mice for 3 months. Improved Morris water maze performance and reduced neuroinflammatory markers. No adverse events reported. Study did not assess carcinogenicity or long-term safety. Independent group; no retraction noted for this paper as of 2026. Animal data only — not a human dose.

dose_references.context: Community anecdotal use — no clinical trial basis
dose_references.notes: Various vendor and forum sources describe oral doses in the range of 5–20 mg, and subcutaneous doses of 2–5 mg, anecdotally. NONE of these figures derive from clinical trials or peer-reviewed pharmacokinetic studies in humans and they are intentionally not encoded as numeric ranges here because no cited human study supports them. No safe or effective human dose has been established. These reports are documented only to acknowledge that unsupervised human use occurs, not to endorse any dose. Consult a qualified clinician.

synergies.rationale: Semax (an ACTH 4-10 analog) upregulates BDNF and NGF via CREB-mediated transcription in rodent hippocampus and cortex, while Dihexa is proposed to potentiate HGF/c-MET-driven synaptogenesis. The two compounds act on distinct growth-factor pathways (neurotrophin axis vs. HGF axis). This is a mechanistic observation about non-overlapping targets, offered for educational context only — no co-administration studies in animals or humans exist.
synergies.caution_notes: Both compounds are research-only with no established human doses. Additive or synergistic CNS growth-factor signaling is uncharacterized and unstudied. This is not a protocol or a recommended combination — educational context only. Discuss any use with a qualified clinician.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157, a partial sequence of body protection compound, has shown angiogenic, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects in rodent models, including upregulation of VEGFR2 and EGR-1 pathways, whereas Dihexa is proposed to engage HGF/c-MET synaptogenic signaling. These are non-overlapping mechanistic targets. Presented as a mechanistic comparison for educational context only; no co-administration data in any species exists.
synergies.caution_notes: Neither compound has established human safety or dosing data. Both modulate growth-factor-related pathways, so the potential for additive proliferative or oncogenic signaling is unknown and unstudied. This is not a protocol or a recommended combination — educational context only. Discuss any use with a qualified clinician.

name: Cerebrolysin
short_description: Cerebrolysin is an injectable mixture of low-molecular-weight neuropeptides and free amino acids derived from enzymatic hydrolysis of purified porcine brain proteins. It is NOT FDA-approved (US use is investigational only) but is a prescription medicine in ~45+ countries for acute ischemic stroke, traumatic brain injury, and some dementias. Constituent peptides are below 10 kDa, proposed to allow partial blood-brain barrier penetration. Educational entry only; not a prescription or dosing recommendation.
mechanism: Cerebrolysin's active fraction is proposed to mimic endogenous neurotrophic factors (BDNF, NGF, GDNF, CNTF) via low-molecular-weight peptide fragments. Reported mechanisms (largely preclinical/translational): TrkB activation with PI3K/Akt and MAPK/ERK pro-survival signaling; anti-apoptotic effects (down caspase-3/Bax, up Bcl-2); anti-inflammatory modulation of TLR2/TLR4 and NF-κB; GSK-3β inhibition reducing tau hyperphosphorylation and β-amyloid; blood-brain barrier stabilization via ZO-1; and Nrf2-mediated antioxidant effects. The free amino acid fraction (~80% by weight) may serve as neuronal metabolic substrate. Molecular targets remain incompletely characterized.
monitoring_notes: Vital signs (BP, HR, respiratory rate, temperature, SpO2) were checked before and after each IV infusion in trial protocols; published protocols set infusion hold thresholds of systolic BP >200 or <90 mmHg, HR outside 45–110/min, or temperature >38.0°C.

In trials, Cerebrolysin was administered as a slow IV infusion diluted in ~100 mL normal saline over 15–60 minutes; longer infusion (30–60 min) reduced vasodilatory adverse effects (flushing, hypotension, nausea).

Monitor for allergic or anaphylactic reactions during and for at least 30 minutes after each infusion; porcine-derived biological preparations carry inherent hypersensitivity risk. Epinephrine and resuscitation equipment must be immediately available (case report: Grade III anaphylaxis at 30 min post-infusion; PMC11272398, 2024).
Screen for history of porcine allergy or allergic diathesis before any administration.
Screen for epilepsy history; Cerebrolysin is contraindicated in epilepsy per manufacturer prescribing information (EVER Neuro Pharma). Monitor for new-onset seizure activity.
Assess baseline and ongoing renal function; severe renal impairment is a contraindication per prescribing information.

Review concomitant medications: prescribing information warns against mixing Cerebrolysin with lithium-containing solutions in the same infusion line. Caution with concurrent antidepressants due to monoaminergic effects observed in preclinical data.
Given porcine tissue origin, counsel patients with religious or dietary restrictions on porcine products.
Not FDA-approved; all use in the US is investigational or unlicensed. Clinician oversight and informed consent are required.

A 2023 Cochrane review flagged a potential increase in non-fatal serious adverse events (RR 2.39–2.87) at the 30 mL/10-day regimen in stroke patients; weigh against the 2022 safety meta-analysis (n=2,202) finding no statistically significant overall SAE difference.
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to Cerebrolysin or any component of the formulation (porcine-derived peptides, excipients)
Known porcine (pork/pig product) allergy
Epilepsy or history of major seizure disorder (per EVER Neuro Pharma prescribing information)
Severe renal impairment (per EVER Neuro Pharma prescribing information)
relative_contraindications: Allergic diathesis or atopic history — increased hypersensitivity risk; enhanced monitoring required
Concurrent use of lithium therapy — preclinical data suggest potential potentiation of lithium CNS accumulation; do not mix in same infusion line
Concurrent antidepressants — Cerebrolysin elevates monoamine levels (norepinephrine, serotonin, dopamine) in preclinical models; potential for interaction
Pregnancy and lactation — no adequate safety data; use only if clearly necessary under direct physician supervision
Known hepatic impairment — limited data; use with caution
Patients with known religious or ethical objections to porcine-derived products — requires explicit informed consent
common_side_effects: Nausea (during or shortly after IV infusion)
Dizziness or lightheadedness (transient, during infusion)
Headache (10–15% incidence in clinical trials; typically mild-to-moderate, self-resolving within 2–4 hours)
Flushing or feeling of warmth (vasodilatory effect; reduced by slowing infusion rate)
Injection-site reactions (pain, redness, swelling) with direct IV push; minimized by dilution and slow infusion
Agitation or insomnia (neurostimulatory effects; more common with higher doses)
Sweating
Transient hypotension during infusion
serious_adverse_events: Anaphylaxis / life-threatening hypersensitivity (rare; at least one Grade III anaphylactic reaction requiring ICU-level vasopressor support documented in 2024 case report, PMC11272398; historical Chinese literature review identified 35 anaphylaxis cases 1994–2009 including one death from shock)
Seizures (rare; causality not established in controlled trials; absolute contraindication in patients with pre-existing epilepsy)

Non-fatal serious adverse events — Cochrane review (2023, CD007026) reported elevated RR (2.39–2.87) for non-fatal SAEs requiring hospitalization at the 30 mL/10-day dose in ischemic stroke patients; clinical significance debated against safety meta-analysis (PMC8708612) showing no overall SAE difference

Transmission risk: theoretical zoonotic/prion contamination risk inherent to all porcine-derived biologics, mitigated by manufacturing purification steps. No confirmed cases linked to Cerebrolysin in the literature.

dose_references.notes: 30 mL/day (equivalent to ~6.5 g peptide preparation) was the dose used in 11 of 14 RCTs included in the 2025 meta-analysis (Frontiers in Neurology, PMC12465088). The Cochrane review (CD007026) specifically flagged 30 mL x 10 days as the regimen associated with a non-fatal serious adverse event signal (RR 2.39–2.87). IMPORTANT: this reflects the dose used in published clinical trials and is reported for education only — it is NOT a recommendation. Unit shown as mg is approximate; clinical literature universally reports doses in mL of solution, not mg of active peptide.

dose_references.notes: 50 mL/day was tested in trials within the 14-trial meta-analysis (PMC12465088). The 12-trial safety meta-analysis (n=2,202; PMC8708612) noted the 50 mL dose cohort showed the lowest SAE rate vs. placebo (~40% relative reduction), described by the authors as a tendency requiring further study rather than a confirmed effect; overall SAE rates did not differ between groups. Reported for education only, not a recommendation. Unit shown as mg is approximate; clinical literature reports in mL.

Cycled regimen: 50 mL IV once daily for 10 days, followed by two further 10-day cycles at 10 mL/day, as add-on to standard care. Showed small-to-medium effect favoring Cerebrolysin at Day 30 and Day 90. Reported for education only, not a recommendation. Unit shown as mg is approximate; clinical literature reports in mL.
