citation.title: Heterogeneous treatment effects of Cerebrolysin as an early add-on to reperfusion therapy: post hoc analysis of the CEREHETIS trial

dose_references.notes: DOWNGRADED from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS: CEREHETIS was a prospective, randomized, OPEN-LABEL, active-control phase IIIb PILOT study (ITT n=341), and the cited symptomatic-hemorrhagic-transformation benefit comes from a POST-HOC subgroup analysis (HTI ≥1 strata, n=238), which the authors themselves flag as subject to post-hoc statistical limitations. This does not meet blinded-RCT evidence grade for the specific HT claim. 30 mL diluted in 100 mL normal saline once daily x 14 days, started concurrently with IV alteplase (0.9 mg/kg). Reported for education only. Unit shown as mg is approximate; clinical literature reports in mL.

dose_references.context: Mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease — RCT and meta-analysis evidence
dose_references.frequency: Once daily, 5 days/week for 4 weeks per cycle; most trials used 2 treatment cycles

citation.title: Cerebrolysin in mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled clinical trials

dose_references.notes: Range from published AD trials: 20 mL to 30 mL (5 days/wk x 4 wk cycles). The numeric range here is backed by the peer-reviewed meta-analysis of AD RCTs (PMID 25832905), which concluded an overall beneficial effect and favorable benefit-risk ratio. NOTE: the donepezil-combination 'synergy' claim previously attached here was supported only by a ResearchGate-hosted reference (no stable DOI/primary URL) and has been moved to the synergies section and downgraded. Reported for education only, not a recommendation. Unit shown as mg is approximate; clinical literature reports in mL.

dose_references.context: Self-directed or off-label nootropic community use (subcutaneous route) — no clinical trial data for this route
dose_references.notes: No published clinical trial or pharmacokinetic data supports subcutaneous administration of Cerebrolysin. All peer-reviewed human data use IV infusion. Subcutaneous use circulates in online nootropic communities but carries unknown bioavailability, sterility risks, and injection-site risks. Flagged ANECDOTAL with null dose; must not be presented as equivalent to IV-administered clinical data.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic framing: Cerebrolysin acts via proposed neurotrophic/neuroprotective pathways (TrkB/BDNF mimicry, GSK-3β inhibition) while donepezil enhances cholinergic neurotransmission via AChE inhibition — complementary mechanisms studied in Alzheimer's disease. A combination study reported greater BDNF elevation and cognitive improvement vs. monotherapy, but the supporting reference is hosted on ResearchGate without a stable primary DOI/URL, so the strength of this evidence cannot be independently confirmed at the RCT level.
synergies.caution_notes: DOWNGRADED from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS because the only available citation is a non-archival ResearchGate link with no verifiable primary source. Educational framing only — NOT a prescribing or stacking recommendation. Donepezil is an FDA-approved prescription drug; Cerebrolysin is NOT FDA-approved. Cholinergic plus neurotrophic stimulation requires physician oversight; additive GI effects (nausea, vomiting) may occur. Do not combine without clinician supervision.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic framing only: in a preliminary IN VITRO study (Neuro-2A mouse neuroblastoma cells under oxidative stress; PMC10960614), Cerebrolysin and citicoline showed additive BDNF gene upregulation via distinct pathways. A separate retrospective, underpowered TBI cohort reported a non-significant numerical trend favoring the combination. No prospective human RCT has tested this combination.
synergies.caution_notes: DOWNGRADED from HUMAN_OBS to MECHANISTIC: the primary additivity evidence is in-vitro mouse-cell data, and the only human signal was a retrospective, non-significant cohort. Educational framing only — NOT a recommendation. Citicoline is an OTC supplement in the US; Cerebrolysin is NOT FDA-approved. No safety or efficacy of the combination has been established in humans.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic framing only: Cerebrolysin is proposed to provide neurotrophic support and reduce excitotoxic neuronal death, while memantine non-competitively blocks pathological NMDA-receptor overactivation — distinct mechanisms relevant to Alzheimer's and vascular cognitive impairment. The pairing is used clinically in some countries where Cerebrolysin is approved, but dedicated combination RCT data in humans are lacking.
synergies.caution_notes: No dedicated human RCT exists for this specific combination. Memantine is FDA-approved for moderate-to-severe Alzheimer's disease; Cerebrolysin is NOT FDA-approved. Mechanistic educational framing only — NOT a prescribing or stacking recommendation.

name: P21 (Peptide 021)
short_description: P21 (Peptide 021) is a synthetic, BBB-permeable tetra-peptide derived from the biologically active region (residues 148–151) of ciliary neurotrophic factor (CNTF), with an adamantylated glycine added at the C-terminus to improve lipophilicity and resistance to exopeptidase degradation. All published efficacy and safety data are from rodent models. No human clinical trials have been completed or registered as of May 2025. It is a research-only compound with no FDA approval and no established human dose.
mechanism: P21 competitively inhibits leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) signaling — which tonically suppresses neurogenesis — and simultaneously upregulates brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) expression via PKAcα activation and CREB phosphorylation. Downstream BDNF-TrkB signaling activates the MAP-kinase/ERK and PI3K/Akt pathways, promoting hippocampal dentate gyrus neurogenesis, dendritic arborization, synaptic protein expression (synaptophysin, PSD-95, NR1), and inhibiting GSK-3β, a major tau-hyperphosphorylating kinase. The adamantylated glycine modification confers BBB permeability and oral bioavailability in rodents, distinguishing it from the parent CNTF molecule, which does not cross the BBB effectively. All mechanistic evidence is from rodent and in vitro models; none has been confirmed in humans.
monitoring_notes: No human monitoring protocols exist — P21 has not entered human clinical trials as of May 2025.

In animal studies, body weight, organ histology (brain, liver, kidney, heart, lung, spleen), hematological parameters, and liver/kidney function tests were evaluated and showed no abnormalities over up to ~18 months of oral dosing in mice (Baazaoui & Iqbal 2017).

Given the pro-neurogenic mechanism (stimulates neural progenitor proliferation), theoretical monitoring considerations in any future human trial would include neurological symptom tracking, MRI-based structural assessment, and long-term follow-up for any evidence of uncontrolled cell proliferation — though no such adverse events have been reported in published rodent studies.
The compound is sold by unregulated research-peptide vendors; purity, sterility, and identity of purchased material cannot be guaranteed.
The Alzheimer's Drug Discovery Foundation Cognitive Vitality Report classifies P021 as 'Insufficient Evidence' for human use.
absolute_contraindications: No human contraindications can be formally established in the absence of human clinical trial data; the following are precautionary given the compound's mechanism and research-only status.

Active or prior malignancy involving neural tissue: theoretical concern given the pro-neurogenic (cell-proliferative) mechanism — no tumor-promoting effect was observed in rodent studies up to ~18 months, but human oncological risk is entirely unknown.
Pregnancy and lactation: no safety data in any species for gestational or lactational exposure.
relative_contraindications: Any active neurological condition managed by an established treatment plan: P21 is uninvestigated in humans and should not displace evidence-based therapies.
Concurrent use of other pro-neurogenic or neurotrophic agents (e.g., Cerebrolysin, Semax): mechanistic overlap and additive risks are unknown.
Immunocompromised states: no data on infection risk from peptide administration.
Pediatric or adolescent populations: no developmental safety data; pro-neurogenic effects in a developing nervous system are unstudied.
common_side_effects: No controlled human safety data exists. The following are anecdotal community reports only and cannot be graded as clinically established: mild transient insomnia with evening dosing (intranasal use), mild headache (rare).
In rodent studies up to ~18 months of oral dosing: no adverse effects on body weight, behavior, organ histology, or hematological/metabolic parameters were detected in transgenic or wild-type mice.
serious_adverse_events: No serious adverse events have been reported in published animal studies.
No human adverse event data exists.

Theoretical serious risk: uncontrolled neural cell proliferation — not observed in any published rodent study, but the ~18-month maximum mouse follow-up does not fully exclude long-latency oncological risk in humans.

Risk of unknown impurities from unregulated research-peptide vendors: infections, endotoxin reactions, and contaminant toxicity — not attributable to P21 itself but inseparable from its current procurement context.

citation.title: Effects of a ciliary neurotrophic factor (CNTF) small-molecule peptide mimetic in an in vitro and in vivo model of CDKL5 deficiency disorder

dose_references.notes: Mottolese et al. (2024) used 750 nmol/mouse via intraperitoneal injection in adult mice (and 60 nmol/g diet in juveniles) in a CDKL5 disorder model. In vivo results were largely negative — chronic P021 failed to increase BDNF, did not improve neuroanatomical defects, and produced only limited behavioral benefit. low/high nulled because the molar quantity is not convertible to a standard human mass-based dose. Route coded as 'im' as the closest available option to IP.

dose_references.context: Community/self-experimentation — subcutaneous, intranasal (NO clinical trial data; flagged ANECDOTAL)
dose_references.notes: Online communities report doses such as 100–500 mcg subcutaneous daily or 500 mcg–2 mg intranasal daily. These figures originate entirely from uncontrolled self-reports on peptide forums and vendor sites — NOT from any peer-reviewed study, clinical trial, or regulatory source. No human pharmacokinetic, safety, or efficacy data exist to validate any human dose. low/high are null because no verified human dose exists; is_human_data is false because these are unverified anecdotes, not controlled human data.

synergies.rationale: Semax (an ACTH(4–10) analogue approved in Russia/Ukraine, not FDA-approved) independently upregulates BDNF and NGF expression in hippocampal and cortical tissue via adrenergic and melanocortin receptor pathways (PubMed 16635254). P21 elevates BDNF through CNTF/LIF pathway modulation. The two engage BDNF upregulation through mechanistically non-overlapping routes, which in principle could produce additive neurotrophic signaling. This is a pharmacologic rationale only — no combination study in animals or humans exists. Educational framing only; not a protocol recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: Both compounds promote neurogenesis and synaptic plasticity; additive effects on neuronal proliferation are unstudied and could carry unknown risks. Semax has no FDA approval. Neither compound has an established human dose. No combination safety data exists. Discuss any use with a qualified clinician.

synergies.rationale: Cerebrolysin is a porcine-brain-derived neuropeptide mixture containing low-molecular-weight fragments with CNTF-, BDNF- and NGF-related activity. P21 was derived from the CNTF active region, so there is mechanistic overlap at the CNTF/BDNF axis. This overlap means a combination may be partially redundant rather than additive. Some human RCT data exist for Cerebrolysin alone (Alzheimer's disease, stroke recovery; Cochrane reviews), but none for any P21 combination. Educational framing only.
synergies.caution_notes: Significant mechanistic overlap at the CNTF/BDNF axis means combining P21 and Cerebrolysin may be redundant rather than synergistic. Cerebrolysin is an IV product (porcine-derived, rare hypersensitivity); combining it with an unvalidated research peptide has no trial support. Not a recommendation — discuss with a clinician.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 (Body Protection Compound 157) is a pentadecapeptide with preclinical evidence for neuroprotection, peripheral nerve regeneration, and reduced neuroinflammation via VEGF, Akt/mTOR, and EGR-1 pathways in rodent models — mechanistically distinct from P21's CNTF/LIF/BDNF axis. The proposed rationale is complementary multi-pathway neuroprotection (acute neuroprotection/vascular repair vs. neurogenic/synaptic plasticity). This is animal-study and mechanistic reasoning only; no combination study exists. Educational framing only.
synergies.caution_notes: No combination study exists in any species. Both compounds are research-only with no established human dose. BPC-157 has no FDA approval. Any combined human use would be entirely without a safety or efficacy evidence base. Not a recommendation.

name: DSIP (Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide)
short_description: DSIP is an endogenous nonapeptide (Trp-Ala-Gly-Gly-Asp-Ala-Ser-Gly-Glu) first isolated from rabbit cerebral venous blood in 1977. It has been studied for sleep modulation, stress-axis dampening, opioid/alcohol withdrawal, antioxidant effects, and possible geroprotection — predominantly in small, dated human observational studies and animal models. It is NOT FDA-approved, not approved by any major regulatory agency for any indication, and lacks large-scale RCT evidence. As of 2026 it is reported to be under FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) review (July 2026) for possible inclusion on the 503A bulk drug substances list. Treat all dosing as research-context information, not a protocol.
mechanism: DSIP is an endogenous neuropeptide detected in the hypothalamus, pituitary, and peripheral tissues. Its receptor has never been cloned and its precise receptor mechanism remains uncharacterized (mechanistic uncertainty). Proposed mechanisms include: (1) inhibition of somatostatin (SRIF) release via a dopaminergic pathway, which would disinhibit pulsatile GH secretion during slow-wave sleep; (2) modulation of hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activity, with animal data showing attenuation of CRF-induced corticosterone release; (3) promotion of delta (slow-wave) sleep EEG activity through poorly understood central effects; (4) antioxidant activity via upregulation of superoxide dismutase, catalase, and ceruloplasmin in rodent models; (5) pleiotropic effects on circadian rhythmicity, locomotor activity, and neurotransmitter concentrations (serotonin, dopamine, glutamate). The peptide is reported to cross the blood-brain barrier via a saturable transport mechanism. A 2006 review concluded that the link between DSIP and sleep "has never been further characterized" due to absence of an identified receptor or gene, and that DSIP analogues rather than native DSIP itself may drive observed sleep-promoting effects in animals. Human GH and prolactin studies show conflicting results (positive in some rat models; null in healthy women at ~25 mcg/kg IV).
monitoring_notes: No established standard-of-care monitoring protocol exists given the absence of an approved indication. In a research context, monitor for CNS sedation, cardiovascular effects (DSIP increased heart rate and decreased HRV in one surgical study — PMID 19142086), and headache/nausea/vertigo as the most commonly reported transient human adverse effects.
If used alongside GH-axis peptides (GHRH analogs, GH secretagogues), monitor IGF-1, fasting glucose, and HbA1c at baseline and periodically, as GH-axis stimulation can impair insulin sensitivity.
Monitor for signs of immunogenicity (injection site reactions, urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis) given general FDA concern about compounded-peptide immunogenicity risk.
Blood pressure and heart rate monitoring is prudent given evidence of HRV changes in the anaesthesia study (PMID 19142086).
No long-term human safety data exists. All human studies were short-term (single injection to ~2-week courses). No chronic safety profile has been established.

Mental-health monitoring may be warranted given a study reporting elevated DSIP-like immunoreactivity in major depressive disorder with suicidality (PMID 9606527); clinical significance is unknown but suggests HPA-axis interaction.
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity or prior allergic reaction to DSIP or any component of the compounded formulation
Pregnancy (no safety data; no human reproductive or teratogenicity studies)
Breastfeeding (no human data on transfer into breast milk or neonatal effects)
relative_contraindications: Concurrent use of CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, barbiturates, Z-drugs, opioids, alcohol) — additive CNS depression plausible though unquantified
Active immunosuppressive therapy — DSIP shows NK-cell activation and cytokine-modulating activity in animal models; theoretical concern for opposing therapeutic immunosuppression
Active or history of malignancy — animal tumor-incidence data do not establish oncologic safety in humans with pre-existing cancer
Use of ACE inhibitors or other drugs metabolized by peptidases — theoretical interaction with peptide degradation pathways (no formal interaction study)
Severe hepatic or renal impairment — peptide clearance may be altered; no pharmacokinetic data in these populations
History of substance use disorder — the 1984 clinical trial noted long-standing drug addiction as a complicating factor (PMID 6391926)
Cardiovascular disease, arrhythmia, or autonomic dysfunction — DSIP increased heart rate and decreased HRV in surgical patients (PMID 19142086)
common_side_effects: Transient headache (reported in human studies; grade and incidence not systematically quantified)
Nausea (reported; frequency not quantified in published trials)
Vertigo or dizziness (reported in human-data reviews)
Transient stimulatory effect during the first hour post-IV injection before sleep-promoting effects manifest (Schneider-Helmert 1981, PMID 7028502)
serious_adverse_events: Immunogenicity: potential immunogenicity is a cited regulatory concern for compounded peptides. Compounded peptides of insufficient purity carry risk of antibody formation; theoretically could include anaphylaxis, though no published cases of severe immune reactions to DSIP appear in the peer-reviewed literature.

Cardiovascular effects at IV doses: DSIP significantly increased heart rate and decreased HRV in surgical patients (PMID 19142086); higher doses (50–100 nmol/kg) were tested in the same study with cardiovascular data not fully detailed in the abstract.
Potential CNS over-depression if combined with sedative or opioid medications (mechanistic concern; not quantified).
No LD50 has been established in any animal model; absence of an LD50 does not confirm human safety at higher or repeated doses.

dose_references.notes: Study tested 25, 50, and 100 nmol/kg IV in 24 female surgical patients (mcg/kg range here is the conversion of those nmol/kg figures). At the lowest dose, DSIP increased heart rate, decreased HRV, and paradoxically reduced delta-wave EEG rhythm and lightened depth of anaesthesia — opposite of the hypothesized effect. No efficacy dose-finding established. Research-context information only; note this is a cardiovascular-relevant signal, not a target dose.

dose_references.context: Animal (rat) geroprotection / antioxidant study — subcutaneous (NOT human-translatable)
dose_references.frequency: 5 consecutive days per month, chronic administration (rodent protocol)

citation.title: Mechanism of delta-sleep inducing peptide geroprotective activity

dose_references.notes: Dose reported as 10 mcg/100 g body weight SC in aged rats (PMID 21809625), ~100 mcg/kg. Suppressed lipid peroxidation and activated SOD, catalase, ceruloplasmin. Rodent-only; null human range because no human SC dose is established. Not applicable to humans without clinical study.

dose_references.context: Community/off-label subcutaneous use — no clinical trial data supports this route for DSIP in humans
dose_references.notes: Subcutaneous administration is widely discussed in online communities and vendor-adjacent sources but has no published human clinical trial data. All peer-reviewed human studies used intravenous administration. No established dose, frequency, or safety profile for SC use in humans exists in the peer-reviewed literature. Flagged ANECDOTAL with null range.

synergies.rationale: Complementary circadian/sleep mechanisms are proposed: melatonin acts on MT1/MT2 receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus to regulate sleep onset and circadian phase, while DSIP is proposed to deepen slow-wave (delta) sleep through hypothalamic pathways and neurotransmitter modulation. A 2024 mouse study reported a DSIP fusion peptide normalized brain melatonin levels in PCPA-induced insomnia, suggesting possible action on related but non-redundant pathways. No human combination study exists. Educational rationale only.
synergies.caution_notes: No human safety or efficacy data for this combination. Additive sedation is plausible but unquantified. Both compounds may affect HPA axis tone. Educational framing only — not a protocol recommendation; discuss any combination with a clinician.

synergies.rationale: In animal models DSIP inhibits somatostatin release via a dopaminergic mechanism (PMID 2886936), which could disinhibit pituitary GH secretion, whereas GHRH analogs act directly on pituitary GHRH receptors. The mechanistic overlap is at the level of upstream GH regulation, and slow-wave sleep is the physiologic window for pulsatile GH secretion. Importantly, one human study found DSIP at ~25 mcg/kg IV did NOT significantly alter GH in healthy women (PMID 8475226), so the GH-promoting rationale is animal-level and does not translate reliably to humans. Educational rationale only.
synergies.caution_notes: GH-axis combinations warrant monitoring of IGF-1, fasting glucose, and HbA1c — GH excess can cause insulin resistance, edema, and carpal tunnel syndrome. Human synergy is unconfirmed and the human GH effect of DSIP was null. Educational framing only — not a protocol recommendation; clinician discussion required.

synergies.rationale: Both peptides have been studied for CNS/autonomic and stress-axis modulation in animal models, and BPC-157 modulates dopaminergic and serotonergic pathways that overlap with DSIP's proposed dopaminergic somatostatin inhibition. The proposed complementarity (stress-axis regulation plus tissue protection) is mechanistic and theoretical, not clinically validated. Educational rationale only.
synergies.caution_notes: No co-administration human data. Neither peptide is FDA-approved. Theoretical additive CNS effects from dopaminergic overlap. Educational framing only — not a protocol recommendation.

name: PT-141 (Bremelanotide)
short_description: FDA-approved (2019) melanocortin receptor agonist (brand name Vyleesi) indicated for acquired, generalized hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) in premenopausal women. Acts centrally via MC3R/MC4R in the hypothalamus and limbic system. Not approved for men or for sexual performance enhancement. Widely investigated off-label for male erectile dysfunction in Phase 2 studies.
mechanism: Bremelanotide is a cyclic heptapeptide analog of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (alpha-MSH) that acts as a non-selective melanocortin receptor agonist with activity at MC1R, MC3R, MC4R, and MC5R. Activation of MC4R and MC3R in the hypothalamus and limbic system (medial preoptic area, amygdala) is believed to modulate sexual desire and motivation through dopamine and nitric oxide signaling pathways. The precise mechanism by which it improves HSDD is stated as unknown in the FDA label. MC1R activation on dermal melanocytes drives melanogenesis and is responsible for focal hyperpigmentation as an adverse effect. Unlike PDE5 inhibitors, bremelanotide acts centrally on desire rather than peripherally on genital blood flow. A 2022 fMRI study (n=31, JCI) demonstrated that MC4R agonism enhanced sexual brain processing by reducing self-monitoring and increasing limbic responsiveness to erotic stimuli in women with HSDD.
monitoring_notes: Blood pressure should be well controlled prior to initiating treatment; measure BP before starting therapy per FDA label guidance.
Assess cardiovascular risk factors before clinical use; the FDA label contraindicates use in uncontrolled hypertension or known cardiovascular disease.

Monitor for focal hyperpigmentation, particularly on the face, gingiva, and breasts; darker-skinned individuals are at higher risk. Risk increases substantially with more frequent than label dosing (about 38% incidence with 8 consecutive daily doses vs ~1% at label-approved monthly frequency). Resolution is not guaranteed upon discontinuation.

Bremelanotide may slow gastric emptying and delay oral medication absorption; consider timing of critical oral medications (e.g., antibiotics requiring threshold concentrations, analgesics requiring rapid onset).
Avoid co-administration with oral naltrexone-containing products used for opioid or alcohol use disorder — bremelanotide significantly reduces systemic naltrexone exposure.
Discontinue if no clinically meaningful improvement after 8 weeks of use per FDA label.
Liver function: a single case of acute hepatitis following repeated use over one year has been documented (NIH LiverTox, Likelihood Class D). Monitor for hepatic symptoms in prolonged use.
Monitor for nausea severity; anti-emetic therapy may be required in approximately 13% of patients.
absolute_contraindications: Uncontrolled hypertension (FDA label contraindication)
Known cardiovascular disease (FDA label contraindication)

Personal or family history of melanoma or dysplastic nevus syndrome — MC1R agonism drives melanogenesis; the FDA Vyleesi label warns of hyperpigmentation and instructs reporting new or changing skin lesions.
relative_contraindications: Patients at elevated cardiovascular risk (e.g., controlled hypertension, diabetes with cardiovascular involvement) — transient BP elevation of ~6 mmHg systolic requires careful benefit-risk assessment
Darker skin phototypes (Fitzpatrick IV–VI) — substantially higher risk of focal hyperpigmentation, potentially irreversible
Concurrent use of oral naltrexone for opioid or alcohol use disorder — bremelanotide significantly reduces naltrexone systemic exposure, compromising addiction treatment efficacy
Patients on oral antibiotics where threshold serum concentrations are clinically critical — gastric motility slowing may impair absorption
Postmenopausal women — not studied; not approved for this population per FDA label
Men — not FDA-approved; investigational only; hemodynamic safety profile in men not fully characterized in large trials
Pregnancy and lactation — safety not established
common_side_effects: Nausea (40% in Phase 3 trials vs 1.3% placebo) — onset typically within 1 hour, duration approximately 2 hours
Flushing/facial flushing (20.3% vs 0.3% placebo)
Injection site reactions: bruising, pain, redness (13.2%)
Headache (11.3% vs 1.9% placebo)
Vomiting (4.8% vs 0.2% placebo)
Transient blood pressure increase: peak +6 mmHg systolic, +3 mmHg diastolic at 2–4 hours post-dose, resolving by 12 hours; accompanied by heart rate decrease up to 5 bpm
Fatigue, hot flush, paresthesia, dizziness, nasal congestion, cough (each 2–3% incidence)
serious_adverse_events: Focal hyperpigmentation: reported in ~1% of patients at label-approved dosing frequency (up to 8 doses/month); incidence ~38% with daily dosing; affects face, gingiva, breasts; may not resolve after discontinuation — a Warnings and Precautions item on the FDA label
Severe nausea requiring anti-emetic therapy (13% of patients) and causing treatment discontinuation in 8% of patients
Acute hepatitis: one documented case after prolonged repeated use (NIH LiverTox Likelihood Class D — possible rare cause of clinically apparent liver injury); hepatic mechanism not established
Overall serious adverse events: 1.1% (VYLEESI) vs 0.5% (placebo) in Phase 3 trials
Treatment discontinuation due to adverse events: 18% (bremelanotide) vs 2% (placebo) in Phase 3 trials

dose_references.frequency: Single intranasal dose before sexual activity in clinic setting

citation.title: An effect on the subjective sexual response in premenopausal women with sexual arousal disorder by bremelanotide (PT-141) — PubMed PMID 16839319

dose_references.notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS: this is a small (n=18) early-phase crossover pilot, not a pivotal RCT. The intranasal route was discontinued from development after Phase 2 male trials (up to 15 mg intranasally in 726 men) showed dose-related blood pressure increases; development pivoted to subcutaneous only. The intranasal formulation is NOT approved or commercially available. This 20 mg intranasal value does not apply to the approved subcutaneous product and must not be used as a dosing reference.

dose_references.context: Off-label investigational use in men with erectile dysfunction — Phase 2/2b trials (not FDA-approved for this indication)

citation.title: Novel Emerging Therapies for Erectile Dysfunction — World Journal of Men's Health

dose_references.notes: No dose is cited because no FDA-approved dose exists for men. Phase 2b trials reported approximately 34% of treated men vs 9% placebo achieved erections sufficient for intercourse, and a Phase 2b diabetes-ED trial showed significant IIEF score improvements. Bremelanotide DOES NOT have FDA approval for men. Any use in men is investigational. No dose is established or endorsed.

synergies.rationale: Bremelanotide acts centrally via MC4R to enhance sexual desire and motivation; PDE5 inhibitors act peripherally by increasing genital blood flow via cGMP-mediated smooth muscle relaxation. These are pharmacologically distinct, non-overlapping pathways. Early intranasal Phase 2 data examined the combination in PDE5i-non-responders, and Palatin Technologies initiated a Phase 2b trial (2023) co-formulating subcutaneous bremelanotide with a PDE5 inhibitor for PDE5i-nonresponsive erectile dysfunction. Presented for mechanistic/educational context only.
synergies.caution_notes: Both agents may independently affect blood pressure: PDE5 inhibitors cause vasodilation and reduce systemic blood pressure, while bremelanotide causes transient blood pressure elevation. The combined hemodynamic interaction has not been fully characterized in large RCTs. This combination is investigational for men and is not established or approved for women. Discuss any combination with a prescribing clinician. Educational context only — not a recommended protocol.

synergies.rationale: Oxytocin acts via oxytocin receptors in limbic and hypothalamic circuits implicated in social bonding and affiliative behavior, while bremelanotide acts via MC3R/MC4R on desire and arousal circuits. The two systems operate through distinct receptor populations in overlapping brain regions (hypothalamus, amygdala), giving a theoretical complementary CNS rationale. This pairing is purely mechanistic and is not supported by any controlled trial.
synergies.caution_notes: No published RCT characterizes the safety or efficacy of this combination; pharmacodynamic interactions are entirely theoretical. Intranasal oxytocin is not FDA-approved for sexual dysfunction. Educational context only — not a recommended protocol.

name: Melanotan II (MT-II)
short_description: Melanotan II is a synthetic, non-selective cyclic heptapeptide analog of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (alpha-MSH) developed at the University of Arizona. It agonizes melanocortin receptors MC1R, MC3R, MC4R, and MC5R, producing skin tanning, appetite suppression, and sexual arousal effects. It has never received FDA approval or approval from any major regulatory body, carries documented serious adverse events, and is classified as a research compound only. It is the structural parent of bremelanotide (PT-141), which subsequently received FDA approval for a distinct indication (HSDD in premenopausal women) in 2019. This entry is educational and does not constitute a dosing recommendation.
mechanism: Melanotan II non-selectively agonizes melanocortin receptors: MC1R (melanocytes — drives eumelanin synthesis and skin pigmentation), MC3R (hypothalamic energy homeostasis, autonomic regulation, limbic feeding behavior), MC4R (hypothalamic appetite suppression, sympathetic cardiovascular tone, penile erection via central NO pathways), and MC5R (exocrine gland function). It does not bind MC2R (the ACTH receptor), so it does not directly stimulate the HPA axis via that receptor. MC4R activation centrally increases sympathetic outflow, which accounts for the cardiovascular pressor effects and appetite suppression. The pro-erectile effect is mediated centrally via MC4R activation in the CNS, leading to downstream nitric oxide-mediated cavernous smooth muscle relaxation, distinct from the peripheral PDE5 mechanism of sildenafil.
monitoring_notes: Blood pressure and heart rate: MC4R activation increases sympathetic tone; document baseline BP/HR and monitor for acute hypertensive episodes. Pressor effects reported peaking 90–120 minutes post-dose.

Dermatologic surveillance: All new or changing moles (nevi) should be evaluated by a dermatologist before and during any exposure. Case reports document eruptive dysplastic nevi and melanoma in situ associated with MT-II use. Any darkening, new nevi, or irregular borders warrant urgent dermatology referral.
Renal function: A case report documents renal infarction (~50% involvement) following months of self-administered MT-II. Baseline creatinine/eGFR and monitoring are warranted if use is disclosed.
Creatine phosphokinase (CPK): Rhabdomyolysis has been documented at supratherapeutic doses. If musculoskeletal symptoms occur, check CPK urgently.

Neurological status: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) has been reported in association with melanotan use. Sudden neurological symptoms — headache, visual changes, altered consciousness — require immediate emergency evaluation.
Priapism screening: Prolonged erections (>4 hours) are a documented adverse event and constitute a urological emergency. Patients should be counseled on this risk before any exposure.
Pregnancy status: Safety in pregnancy and lactation has not been studied. Avoid in pregnant or breastfeeding individuals.
Nausea management: Nausea occurs in a substantial proportion of subjects at studied doses; severe nausea was reported in ~13% at 0.025 mg/kg in one trial.

Product sterility: MT-II is not manufactured under pharmaceutical GMP for human use; gray-market products carry contamination risk. Any disclosed use should prompt questioning about preparation and source.
absolute_contraindications: Personal or family history of melanoma or other melanocytic skin cancers — MC1R stimulation accelerates melanogenesis; case reports link MT-II use to melanoma in situ and eruptive dysplastic nevi
Pregnancy and breastfeeding — no safety data; pharmacological effects on cardiovascular and neuroendocrine axes pose unknown fetal/neonatal risk

Priapism history or predisposing conditions (sickle cell disease, leukemia, multiple myeloma) — MC4R-mediated pro-erectile effects can trigger ischemic priapism, a urological emergency documented in case reports
Prior hypersensitivity or severe adverse reaction to MT-II or any melanocortin analog
relative_contraindications: Pre-existing or poorly controlled hypertension — MC4R activation raises sympathetic tone; exaggerated pressor responses documented in subjects with elevated baseline BP
Cardiovascular disease (coronary artery disease, arrhythmia, heart failure) — acute sympathomimetic effects may be poorly tolerated
Dysplastic nevus syndrome or atypical mole syndrome — already elevated baseline melanoma risk compounded by melanocortin stimulation
Renal impairment — renal infarction case report suggests possible vasoconstrictive or thrombotic renal risk; reduced clearance may alter exposure
History of seizures or neurological conditions — PRES has been reported; mechanism may involve hypertensive vasogenic edema
Concurrent use of antihypertensive medications — pharmacodynamic interaction with pressor effects unpredictable
Concurrent use of PDE5 inhibitors — theoretical additive vasodilatory or unpredictable hemodynamic interaction
History of nausea/vomiting disorders or use of antiemetics — MT-II causes significant nausea at studied doses; risk-benefit particularly unfavorable
common_side_effects: Nausea (dose-dependent; reported in substantial proportion of subjects at 0.025 mg/kg; severe nausea in ~13–21% across trials)
Spontaneous penile erections (documented in human trials; duration 1–5 hours post-injection)
Yawning and stretching complex (characteristic autonomic response observed in human and animal studies)
Facial flushing
Fatigue and somnolence (Grade II at higher doses in Phase I)
Appetite suppression
Darkening of existing moles and skin pigmentation (the primary intended research effect — eumelanin production via MC1R)
serious_adverse_events: Melanoma / melanoma in situ: Case reports document new melanoma in situ and eruptive dysplastic nevi following MT-II use (Ong & Bowling, Australasian J Dermatology, 2012, PMID 22724573). Causal inference is limited by case report design, but mechanistic plausibility via MC1R-driven melanogenesis is strong.
Hypertensive crisis: Acute blood pressure elevation reported, including in individuals with undiagnosed hypertension using gray-market doses.

Priapism (ischemic): Multiple published case reports of low-flow priapism requiring emergency urological intervention including cavernosal aspiration and phenylephrine injection (BMJ Case Rep 2019, PMID 30796078; Sex Med 2021, PMID 33460908).

Rhabdomyolysis: Documented in a case report of a male who self-administered 6 mg SC; CPK peaked at 17,773 IU/L; ICU admission required (Nelson, Bryant, Aks. Clin Toxicol. 2012;50(10):1169-73. DOI 10.3109/15563650.2012.740637).

Renal infarction: Documented in a case report involving repeated SC dosing over ~6 months; right renal infarction involving ~50% of the kidney, new hypertension, elevated creatinine (Peters et al. CEN Case Rep. 2020;9(2):159-161. PMC7148395).
Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES): Documented case report associating melanotan use with PRES on MRI (Kaski et al. Ann Intern Med. 2013;158(9):707-8. PMID 23648958).
Systemic sympathomimetic toxicity at supratherapeutic doses — diffuse myalgia, sweating, anxiety.
Product contamination risk: MT-II sold outside pharmaceutical channels is not subject to GMP controls; contamination with unlabeled substances is a documented concern.

dose_references.context: Phase I pilot study of tanning/pigmentation in healthy male volunteers — the only published formal dose-escalation trial in humans. Educational reference to what was studied, not a usage recommendation.

citation.source: Life Sciences. 1996;58(20):1777-84. Dorr RT, Lines R, Levine N, et al. PMID: 8637402

dose_references.notes: Single-blind, 3 subjects. Starting dose 0.01 mg/kg, escalated in 0.005 mg/kg steps; two subjects reached 0.03 mg/kg, one reached 0.025 mg/kg. Authors suggested 0.025 mg/kg/day for future Phase I work. Adverse effects: mild nausea, Grade II somnolence/fatigue at higher doses, spontaneous penile erections lasting 1–5 hours. This is a 3-subject pilot — extrapolation to a 'therapeutic dose' is not supported. Evidence kept at HUMAN_OBS (not RCT) given n=3.

dose_references.context: Human crossover trial of erectogenic effects in men with organic erectile dysfunction (n=10). Educational reference to studied dose only.
dose_references.frequency: Single dose per study session (each treatment administered twice in crossover design)

citation.title: Effect of an alpha-melanocyte stimulating hormone analog on penile erection and sexual desire in men with organic erectile dysfunction
citation.source: Urology. 2000 Oct 1;56(4):641-6. Wessells H, Gralnek D, Dorr R, Hruby VJ, Hadley ME, Levine N. PMID: 11018622

dose_references.notes: Double-blind placebo-controlled crossover, n=10. Dose 0.025 mg/kg SC. Erections initiated in 12/19 active injections vs 1/21 placebo. Increased sexual desire vs placebo. Adverse effects: nausea (4/19 injections rated severe), yawning, stretching. This is a very small crossover trial — not an RCT powered for efficacy; classified HUMAN_OBS.

dose_references.context: Human crossover trial of penile erection and sexual motivation in men with psychogenic and organic ED (n=20). Educational reference to studied dose only.

citation.title: Melanocortin receptor agonists, penile erection, and sexual motivation: human studies with Melanotan II
citation.source: Int J Impot Res. 2000 Oct;12(Suppl 4):S74-9. Wessells H, Levine N, Hadley ME, Dorr R, Hruby V. PMID: 11035391

dose_references.notes: Double-blind placebo-controlled crossover, n=20. Dose 0.025 mg/kg. Spontaneous erections in 17/20 subjects without stimulation. Sexual desire increased after 68% of MT-II doses vs 19% placebo (p<0.01). Severe nausea in 12.9% of subjects at this dose. Small crossover, classified HUMAN_OBS not RCT.

dose_references.context: Community/self-administration (gray-market tanning context) — no controlled trial supports these doses. Listed only to document that vendor/community dosing has no clinical basis and is associated with serious harm.

citation.title: An unhealthy glow? A review of melanotan use and associated clinical outcomes
citation.source: Clinical, Cosmetic and Investigational Dermatology. 2015. DOI: 10.2147/CCID.S73712

dose_references.notes: No established human therapeutic dose exists. Gray-market use reported in mg (not mg/kg) without medical supervision, often exceeding studied ranges. Rhabdomyolysis case report involved a self-administered 6 mg dose — roughly 6x the studied starting dose. Vendor-promoted dose ranges have no clinical trial basis and are deliberately NOT quantified here.

synergies.rationale: PT-141 is a structurally related melanocortin agonist derived from MT-II research, developed specifically for MC4R-mediated central sexual arousal. Both act on overlapping MC3R/MC4R pathways. This is an educational note on shared lineage and mechanism only — combining them would be pharmacologically redundant (not additive in any therapeutic sense) and is NOT a combination recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: Combining two non-selective melanocortin agonists would compound MC4R-mediated sympathomimetic cardiovascular effects (elevated HR and BP) and nausea. Bremelanotide (Vyleesi) is FDA-approved for HSDD in premenopausal women; MT-II is not approved for any use. There is no clinical rationale for combining these, and no safety data exists for the combination.

synergies.rationale: MT-II produces erections via a central MC4R/NO neural pathway; PDE5 inhibitors work peripherally by preventing cGMP degradation in cavernosal smooth muscle. These are mechanistically distinct pathways, a distinction early researchers noted when studying MT-II. This is educational mechanism information, not a co-administration recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: No controlled human interaction study exists. MT-II acutely raises BP via MC4R sympathetic activation while PDE5 inhibitors lower BP; the net hemodynamic effect of the combination is unpredictable and potentially dangerous. MT-II is not approved for any use. This combination should never be undertaken without direct physician oversight.

name: Kisspeptin
short_description: Kisspeptin is an endogenous hypothalamic neuropeptide encoded by the KISS1 gene. It is the master upstream activator of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis, acting via the KISS1R receptor to stimulate pulsatile GnRH release, and consequently LH, FSH, and sex steroid secretion. Not FDA-approved as a therapeutic; all human use to date is within investigational research settings. Studied in human RCTs for IVF oocyte maturation, hypothalamic amenorrhea, hypogonadotropic states, and psychosexual function. Educational/tracking content only — not a prescribing reference.
mechanism: Kisspeptin binds its cognate G-protein-coupled receptor KISS1R (formerly GPR54), activating the Gαq/11 pathway, which triggers phospholipase C, IP3-mediated intracellular calcium release, protein kinase C activation, and downstream MAPK (ERK1/2, p38) signaling. This cascade depolarizes GnRH neurons in the hypothalamic arcuate and anteroventral periventricular nuclei, triggering pulsatile GnRH secretion into the portal circulation. GnRH in turn stimulates pituitary release of LH and FSH, which drive gonadal sex steroid synthesis and gametogenesis. Kisspeptin neurons in the arcuate nucleus co-express neurokinin B (NKB) and dynorphin (collectively termed KNDy neurons), forming an autoregulatory pulse generator in which kisspeptin and NKB are stimulatory while dynorphin is inhibitory. Kisspeptin also has peripheral KISS1R expression in reproductive tissues, liver, pancreatic beta cells, and adipose tissue with context-dependent metabolic effects. Separately, the KISS1 gene was originally discovered as a metastasis suppressor, and kisspeptin peptides exhibit anti-metastatic and anti-angiogenic properties in preclinical models, though therapeutic translation to oncology remains unestablished.
monitoring_notes: Monitor serum LH, FSH, and sex steroids (testosterone in men; estradiol in women) to assess HPG-axis response and detect over- or under-stimulation.

Monitor for signs of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) in women when kisspeptin is used as an IVF oocyte maturation trigger, particularly in high-risk patients (PCOS, high antral follicle count).

Assess for receptor desensitization (tachyphylaxis): continuous or high-frequency administration causes KISS1R internalization and attenuated LH response. Intermittent dosing showed only partial desensitization over 8 weeks versus near-complete tolerance with twice-daily dosing (Jayasena 2010).
Baseline assessment of hormone-sensitive conditions (prostate cancer, breast cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids) before any HPG-axis-stimulating agent.
Blood pressure and heart rate monitoring is prudent in any research or clinical context, though no significant cardiovascular effects were documented at studied IV and intranasal doses.

Long-term safety data are absent. Effects of repeated administration on ovarian reserve, reproductive aging, and non-reproductive KISS1R-expressing tissues (liver, pancreas, adipose) remain unstudied in humans.
No FDA-approved formulation exists; product identity, purity, and sterility cannot be verified for compounded or research-supply sources.
absolute_contraindications: Active hormone-sensitive malignancy (e.g., hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer, prostate cancer under androgen-deprivation therapy, estrogen-sensitive endometrial or ovarian cancer): kisspeptin stimulates LH/FSH and downstream sex steroids, which could theoretically drive growth of hormone-dependent tumors. Mechanistically derived contraindication; no FDA label exists.
Known hypersensitivity to kisspeptin or any component of the formulation.
Central precocious puberty: kisspeptin drives GnRH secretion and would exacerbate premature pubertal activation.
relative_contraindications: Pregnancy (outside a controlled IVF/fertility-specialist setting): kisspeptin is physiologically elevated by the placenta; exogenous addition in unsupervised contexts carries unknown risk. Supervised IVF maturation protocols are distinct.
Pituitary adenoma or structural pituitary disease: HPG-axis response may be unpredictable; specialist endocrinology assessment required.
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) with high LH pulsatility: kisspeptin further amplifies LH secretion, potentially worsening the LH/FSH ratio and androgen excess.
Risk factors for OHSS during controlled ovarian stimulation: although kisspeptin may be safer than an hCG trigger, OHSS risk remains, particularly with high follicle counts.
Conditions requiring sex-steroid suppression (e.g., gender-affirming hormone therapy, endometriosis managed on GnRH-agonist therapy): kisspeptin would oppose the intended suppression.
common_side_effects: Transient flushing (warmth or facial redness): reported in IV/SC studies.
Mild headache: reported in trial participants.
Nausea: reported at higher doses or with IV infusion.
Lightheadedness or dizziness during or shortly after dosing.
Mild injection-site reactions (redness, discomfort) with SC administration.
Transient LH surge with associated hormonal fluctuation (expected pharmacodynamic effect — can manifest as mood changes, breast tenderness, or libido shifts).
serious_adverse_events: Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS): risk exists when used as an IVF oocyte maturation trigger, especially in high-risk women (PCOS, elevated AFC). One moderate OHSS case occurred in the single-dose group of the Phase 2 RCT (Abbara et al., 2018); none in the double-dose group.

Theoretical exacerbation of hormone-sensitive malignancy via HPG-axis stimulation and sex-steroid elevation. Not observed in trials (cancer populations were excluded) but mechanistically warranted as a serious concern.

Reproductive-axis suppression from receptor desensitization with prolonged continuous administration: sustained exposure can paradoxically suppress GnRH pulsatility, analogous to continuous GnRH-agonist therapy.

Context note: as of 2025 no serious adverse events attributable to kisspeptin have been reported across published clinical studies in 150+ human subjects across multiple centers and isoforms. This reflects limited trial duration and controlled research populations, not established long-term safety.

dose_references.context: IVF oocyte maturation trigger — single subcutaneous bolus of kisspeptin-54. Dose-escalation study (Jayasena/Dhillo et al., JCI 2014). Published doses were 1.6–12.8 nmol/kg SC; 6.4 and 12.8 nmol/kg were clinically effective, producing egg maturation, fertilization, and live births in 53 women.
dose_references.frequency: Single dose, ~36 h before oocyte retrieval (research protocol)

citation.title: Kisspeptin-54 triggers egg maturation in women undergoing in vitro fertilization

dose_references.notes: NUMBERS NULLED BY AUDIT: source doses are published in nmol/kg, not mcg/kg, and the schema has no nmol/kg enum. The prior entry placed 6.4/12.8 under a mcg/kg label, which is incorrect — 6.4 nmol/kg ≈ 43 mcg/kg and 12.8 nmol/kg ≈ 86 mcg/kg for kisspeptin-54 (MW ~5.9–6.7 kDa). To avoid emitting a misleading ~6.7x-understated dose, numeric range is set null; refer to the original publication for exact nmol/kg dosing. Well-tolerated; no serious adverse events. Research/investigational context only — not a prescribing reference.

dose_references.context: Second dose of kisspeptin-54 to improve oocyte yield in IVF patients at high risk of OHSS. Phase 2 randomized controlled trial (Abbara et al., 2018, PMC5850304). Published dose 9.6 nmol/kg SC given at ~36 h and again ~10 h later; 62 women enrolled.

citation.title: A second dose of kisspeptin-54 improves oocyte maturation in women at high risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome: a Phase 2 randomized controlled trial

dose_references.notes: NUMBER NULLED BY AUDIT: source dose is 9.6 nmol/kg (≈64 mcg/kg for kisspeptin-54), not 9.6 mcg/kg. The prior 9.6 value under a mcg/kg label understated the true dose ~6.7x. Refer to original publication for exact nmol/kg dosing. No OHSS cases in the double-dose group; well-tolerated in all 62 women. Research/investigational context only.

dose_references.context: IV infusion of kisspeptin-10 to restore pulsatile LH secretion in patients with neurokinin B signaling deficiencies (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism). Small physiological case series, n=4 with TAC3/TACR3 loss-of-function mutations (Young/Jayasena et al., PMC3902960).
dose_references.frequency: Continuous IV infusion over ~12 h on 2 consecutive days (research protocol)

citation.source: Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism (PMC3902960)

dose_references.notes: Published as ~1.5 mcg/kg/h IV infusion of kisspeptin-10 in n=4 patients. Numeric range nulled because the source value is a per-hour infusion rate (mcg/kg/h), which is not representable in the rate-free mcg/kg field and could be misread as a bolus. Restored LH pulsatility; no adverse events reported. Small uncontrolled series → HUMAN_OBS (downgraded from any RCT framing). Research/investigational context only.

dose_references.context: Comparative IV infusion of kisspeptin-10 vs kisspeptin-54 at three dose levels in healthy men (Abbara et al., PMC4507333). Published doses 0.1, 0.3, and 1.0 nmol/kg/h IV over 3 hours.

dose_references.notes: Doses 0.1–1.0 nmol/kg/h IV (per-hour rate). Both isoforms produced comparable LH responses despite ~37-fold difference in plasma levels, explained by kisspeptin-10 half-life ~4 min vs ~27.6 min for kisspeptin-54. No conversion to mcg entered to avoid fabrication; numeric range remains null. No adverse events in n=10 healthy men. Research setting only.

dose_references.context: IV infusion study of kisspeptin-54 in women with overweight/obesity to assess food intake and metabolic effects (Izzi-Engbeaya et al., 2023, PMC10946989). Published dose 1.0 nmol/kg/h IV over 120 minutes.

citation.title: The effects of kisspeptin on food intake in women with overweight or obesity

dose_references.notes: 1.0 nmol/kg/h IV kisspeptin-54. LH increased confirming biological activity; no significant effect on food intake, hunger, glucose, or insulin. No adverse events. Dose in nmol/kg/h per source; numeric mcg field left null to avoid fabrication. Research setting only.

dose_references.context: fMRI study of kisspeptin effects on sexual and emotional brain processing in healthy men (Comninos/Dhillo group, PMC5272173). Published dose 1 nmol/kg/h IV over 75 minutes.

citation.title: Kisspeptin modulates sexual and emotional brain processing in humans

dose_references.notes: 1 nmol/kg/h IV kisspeptin-54 over 75 min in n=29 healthy heterosexual men. Enhanced limbic activation during sexual imagery and emotional processing. No adverse events. Numeric mcg field null (source is a per-hour nmol/kg/h rate). Author corrected from prior misattribution. Research setting only.

dose_references.context: Intranasal kisspeptin-54 dose-escalation in healthy men/women and women with hypothalamic amenorrhea (Abbara et al., eBioMedicine/Lancet, 2025, PMC12018048). Published doses 3.2, 6.4, 12.8, and 25.6 nmol/kg intranasally; 12.8 nmol/kg identified as most effective.

citation.title: Intranasal kisspeptin administration rapidly stimulates gonadotropin release in humans

dose_references.notes: First demonstration of effective intranasal kisspeptin in humans. Doses given as nmol/kg in source (3.2–25.6 nmol/kg). LH peak at 30–45 min post-dose; response greater in hypothalamic amenorrhea patients vs healthy women. No adverse events. Numeric mcg field null because source uses nmol/kg and schema lacks that unit; do not substitute the nmol value as a mcg value. Research setting only.

dose_references.context: Community/off-label use: kisspeptin-10 administered subcutaneously in non-clinical settings. No peer-reviewed RCT confirms efficacy or safety for this route/context.
dose_references.notes: Controlled IV comparison data indicate SC kisspeptin-10 boluses do not reliably change serum reproductive hormones (short half-life ~4 min). Community use exists but lacks any controlled evidence for efficacy or safety at off-label doses; not an established therapeutic route. No dose range can be responsibly cited. Educational/tracking note only — not an endorsement.

synergies.rationale: In the KNDy neuron system, dynorphin (an endogenous opioid) inhibits GnRH pulsatility. Blocking opioid receptors removes this inhibitory brake. A small human mechanistic study (PMC5010567) reported that naltrexone co-administered with kisspeptin increased LH pulse amplitude relative to kisspeptin alone, consistent with the hypothesis that lifting dynorphin-mediated inhibition amplifies kisspeptin's stimulatory effect on the GnRH pulse generator. This is an educational explanation of KNDy neuroendocrine pharmacology, not a protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_RCT: this is a small mechanistic crossover study (n≈5/group), not a powered clinical trial. Naltrexone is FDA-approved only for alcohol/opioid use disorder and carries hepatic and opioid-withdrawal precautions. Its KNDy-axis use is strictly investigational; combined HPG-axis manipulation can amplify hormonal stimulation unpredictably and requires specialist endocrinology oversight. Not a recommended or established combination.

synergies.rationale: Kisspeptin and NKB are co-expressed in hypothalamic KNDy neurons. NKB acts upstream to modulate GnRH pulse frequency while kisspeptin is the direct GnRH secretagogue. In the same human KNDy investigation (PMC5010567), NKB alone did not robustly stimulate gonadotropins, illustrating the hierarchical roles within the pulse generator. Presented as mechanistic neuroendocrinology for understanding, not a co-administration plan.
synergies.caution_notes: Mechanistic interaction studied in a small research cohort only — not a clinical protocol. Co-administration of multiple HPG-axis agents can amplify hormonal stimulation unpredictably. Any HPG-axis manipulation requires specialist endocrinology oversight.

synergies.rationale: Kisspeptin acts directly upstream of GnRH neurons, so research uses kisspeptin as a functional test of hypothalamic GnRH neuronal reserve: a preserved response to kisspeptin but absent response to exogenous GnRH suggests pituitary dysfunction, whereas absent response to both indicates hypothalamic GnRH neuron failure. This diagnostic hierarchy is educational research pharmacology, not a combined-therapy recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: Sequential or combined use of kisspeptin and GnRH analogs is investigational only. Stacking stimulatory HPG-axis agents risks receptor desensitization, ovarian hyperstimulation in women, or unintended gonadal axis suppression. Educational context only.

name: Gonadorelin
short_description: Synthetic decapeptide identical to endogenous gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) that stimulates pituitary release of LH and FSH. Was FDA-approved for human diagnostic and fertility use (Factrel, Lutrepulse); both products were discontinued and no FDA-approved human gonadorelin product is currently marketed in the US as of 2025. Used off-label via compounding pharmacies in men's hormone optimization to preserve HPG axis function during testosterone therapy — this off-label use is not supported by controlled human trials.
mechanism: Gonadorelin is a synthetic decapeptide structurally identical to endogenous hypothalamic GnRH. It binds transmembrane GnRH receptors on anterior pituitary gonadotrophs, stimulating synthesis and pulsatile release of luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH). When administered in a pulsatile fashion (mimicking the physiological 60-120 minute secretory rhythm of the hypothalamus), it sustains LH and FSH output, which in turn drives testicular Leydig cell testosterone production and Sertoli cell-dependent spermatogenesis. Continuous or non-pulsatile high-dose administration desensitizes GnRH receptors, paradoxically suppressing gonadotropin secretion — the mechanism exploited by long-acting GnRH agonist analogues (e.g., leuprolide). Because gonadorelin is native-sequence GnRH with a plasma half-life of approximately 2-10 minutes (distribution) and 10-40 minutes (terminal), it requires pulsatile delivery or repeated injections to maintain biological activity. Unlike HCG, gonadorelin acts upstream at the pituitary level, preserving FSH secretion alongside LH stimulation.
monitoring_notes: For diagnostic use: measure serum LH at baseline (-15 min, 0 min) and at 15, 30, 45, 60, and 120 minutes post-dose; interpret against laboratory-specific reference ranges
Avoid interfering medications (androgens, estrogens, progestins, glucocorticoids, OCP, digoxin, phenothiazines) for an appropriate washout period before diagnostic testing
In females: conduct diagnostic test during early follicular phase (days 1-7) when possible to obtain interpretable LH response

For pulsatile pump therapy (CHH or hypothalamic amenorrhea): monitor serum LH, FSH, testosterone (males) or estradiol (females), and testicular volume (males) or follicular development (females) at regular intervals
Monitor for signs of ovarian hyperstimulation in women: pelvic pain, bloating, weight gain, decreased urine output
Monitor for injection site reactions with subcutaneous pump; rotate sites to reduce induration and erythema

In men using off-label with exogenous testosterone: no validated monitoring protocol exists; clinician discretion required; consider periodic LH, FSH, testosterone, and semen analysis if fertility is the goal
If pituitary adenoma is suspected prior to use, perform pituitary imaging before administration to assess apoplexy risk
Reconstituted solution should be used within 24 hours (former Factrel label)
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to gonadorelin or any formulation excipient (risk of anaphylaxis/bronchospasm)
Pregnancy — risk of fetal harm and miscarriage (UK SmPC and former Factrel label)
Active pituitary adenoma or pituitary tumor (risk of hemorrhagic infarction / pituitary apoplexy)
Androgen-dependent or hormone-sensitive malignancies (e.g., prostate cancer, breast cancer) — pulsatile use stimulates LH/testosterone production
relative_contraindications: Hyperprolactinemia (blunted or unpredictable gonadotropin response)
Breastfeeding / lactation (insufficient safety data; contraindicated per UK SmPC)
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) — risk of exaggerated ovarian response and ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS)

Concurrent use of agents that alter pituitary gonadotropin secretion: androgens, estrogens, progestins, glucocorticoids, oral contraceptives, digoxin (may blunt or distort LH response, relevant to diagnostic use)
Concurrent phenothiazines or dopamine antagonists (may suppress LH response)
Children under 1 year of age (safety not established per UK SmPC)
Uncontrolled thyroid or adrenal dysfunction (may confound or exaggerate response)
common_side_effects: Injection site pain, swelling, or pruritus
Flushing
Headache
Nausea
Abdominal discomfort
Dizziness
Injection site induration (with repeated subcutaneous pump use)
Skin allergic erythema or scleroma at pump insertion site (reported in pulsatile pump CHH studies)
serious_adverse_events: Anaphylaxis / severe hypersensitivity reaction (rare; more likely with repeated or intravenous dosing)
Bronchospasm / urticaria (rare, reported in Factrel label)

Pituitary apoplexy — sudden hemorrhagic infarction of the pituitary gland; rare but life-threatening; reported with GnRH agonist class; risk elevated in patients with pre-existing pituitary adenoma; presents as thunderclap headache, visual loss, diplopia, altered consciousness
Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) — risk in women with PCOS or those undergoing fertility treatment; severe cases involve fluid shifts, thromboembolism, and organ dysfunction
Ovarian torsion — rare; associated with enlarged ovaries during fertility treatment
Antibody formation with chronic high-dose administration — may reduce efficacy or cause luteolysis
Tachycardia (rare; documented in UK SmPC)

Female patients should be tested during early follicular phase (days 1-7). No current FDA-approved gonadorelin product is marketed in the US; graded HUMAN_OBS to reflect current regulatory status.

dose_references.context: Off-label compounded use in men on testosterone replacement therapy (TRT) to preserve HPG axis / testicular function — community clinical practice, no peer-reviewed RCT

citation.title: Compounded Gonadorelin in Men's Health
citation.source: Empower Pharmacy (compounding vendor — not an authoritative clinical source)

dose_references.notes: No peer-reviewed RCT establishes a validated dose regimen for compounded gonadorelin as a TRT adjunct in men. Various compounding clinics report subcutaneous injection 2-3 times weekly, but these figures are not from controlled human trial data and the only citation is a compounding vendor. Low/high values correctly null. Evidence ANECDOTAL. Mechanistic rationale (maintaining pulsatile LH/FSH signaling during exogenous testosterone suppression) is pharmacologically plausible, but efficacy and optimal dosing remain unestablished in peer-reviewed literature.

synergies.rationale: Exogenous testosterone suppresses endogenous GnRH via hypothalamic negative feedback, causing LH/FSH deficiency and testicular atrophy. Pulsatile gonadorelin theoretically replaces the lost hypothalamic GnRH signal, stimulating pituitary LH and FSH output and thereby maintaining some testicular Leydig cell activity and spermatogenesis. This is the most commonly cited pharmacologic rationale for the gonadorelin + TRT combination, presented here for education only.
synergies.caution_notes: No peer-reviewed RCT has validated this combination. Pulsatile delivery is pharmacologically required — intermittent subcutaneous injections may not replicate true physiological pulsatility given gonadorelin's 2-10 minute plasma half-life. Continuous or non-pulsatile use risks receptor desensitization and paradoxical FSH/LH suppression. Educational context only; not a prescribed protocol. Discuss with a qualified clinician.

synergies.rationale: HCG is an LH mimetic acting directly at testicular Leydig cell LH receptors to maintain intratesticular testosterone and testicular volume during exogenous testosterone use, whereas gonadorelin acts upstream at the pituitary to stimulate both LH and FSH. The two theoretically engage complementary points of the HPG axis. Note: comparative CHH fertility studies evaluated pulsatile GnRH versus HCG-based gonadotropin therapy as alternative treatments — not as a co-administered combination — so those human data do not establish a synergy of the two used together.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded from HUMAN_OBS: the cited human evidence compares gonadorelin and HCG as alternative therapies, it does not support combining them. Simultaneous use in TRT-context men is off-label and unsupported by controlled human trial data. HCG does not stimulate FSH; gonadorelin stimulates both. Combining them may redundantly stimulate LH pathways and could increase estradiol conversion risk. Educational only; clinician oversight required.

synergies.rationale: Clomiphene and enclomiphene are selective estrogen receptor modulators (SERMs) that block hypothalamic estrogen receptors, reducing estrogen-mediated negative feedback on GnRH pulse generation. Gonadorelin acts one step downstream, directly replacing the GnRH signal at the pituitary — so the two mechanisms are conceptually complementary within the HPG axis. In practice SERMs are largely ineffective when exogenous testosterone is present (androgen-receptor-mediated negative feedback dominates), so this pairing is conceptually relevant mainly in testosterone-free hypogonadism management.
synergies.caution_notes: No controlled trial has evaluated gonadorelin + clomiphene/enclomiphene co-administration. Combining upstream HPG axis stimulants may risk ovarian hyperstimulation in women. With androgens present, SERM benefit is largely negated. Educational only; not a recommended protocol.

name: Oxytocin
short_description: A 9-amino-acid hypothalamic neuropeptide with FDA-approved obstetric indications (IV/IM). Investigated off-label via intranasal route for body composition, appetite regulation, sarcopenic obesity, and age-related tissue decline. Plasma oxytocin declines with age; animal data and a small number of human pilot/RCT studies suggest possible roles in muscle maintenance, fat metabolism, and metabolic health, but the only adequately powered body-composition RCT to date (n=61) was negative for weight loss, and chronic human efficacy data remain limited and inconsistent.
mechanism: Oxytocin is produced in the paraventricular and supraoptic nuclei of the hypothalamus and released from the posterior pituitary. It acts on G-protein coupled oxytocin receptors (OXTRs), increasing intracellular calcium in uterine myometrial cells (FDA-approved mechanism). Beyond obstetrics, peripheral and central OXTRs are implicated in: (1) anorexigenic signaling via the nucleus tractus solitarius and arcuate nucleus, reducing both homeostatic and hedonic food intake; (2) activation of MAPK/ERK signaling in muscle satellite cells, promoting proliferation and regeneration in animal models — a pathway that declines with age as circulating oxytocin falls; (3) inhibition of adipogenesis and promotion of osteoblast differentiation in mesenchymal stem cell precursors; (4) lipolytic activity in adipose tissue via sympathetic nervous system activation and direct adipocyte OXTR signaling; (5) modulation of glucose-stimulated insulin secretion in pancreatic beta cells. The hormone also possesses mild antidiuretic activity due to structural similarity with arginine vasopressin (AVP), which is relevant to water intoxication risk at high or prolonged doses. Most non-obstetric metabolic mechanisms are derived from animal/in-vitro data and have not been confirmed as clinically meaningful in adequately powered human trials.
monitoring_notes: For FDA-approved IV/IM obstetric use: continuous monitoring of uterine contraction frequency/duration, maternal blood pressure, fetal heart rate, and fluid intake/output is mandatory per FDA label.

Water intoxication and hyponatremia risk: oxytocin has structural similarity to arginine vasopressin (ADH) and can cause antidiuresis. Signs include headache, nausea, confusion, seizures, and decreasing serum sodium. Prolonged or high-dose administration warrants monitoring of serum sodium and osmolality.
QTc interval: IV oxytocin bolus transiently prolongs QTc. Baseline ECG should be considered in patients with known arrhythmia history or concurrent QT-prolonging drugs before any IV administration.

For intranasal off-label research use: no established monitoring protocol. Research trials screened for baseline electrolyte disorders; patients with impaired renal function, hyponatremia, or conditions affecting water balance were excluded.
Blood pressure monitoring: vasopressor drug interactions can cause severe hypertension. Cyclopropane anesthesia interaction can cause paradoxical hypotension.

Oxytocin receptor downregulation: animal and in-vitro data indicate chronic exogenous oxytocin can decrease OXTR mRNA expression. Clinical significance for off-label chronic intranasal use is unknown; no human studies have characterized receptor desensitization over weeks-to-months timescales.

Baseline and follow-up assessment of lean mass (DEXA), metabolic labs (fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipid panel), and renal function would be reasonable research endpoints given proposed body-composition effects, but are not established clinical monitoring standards for this off-label use, which lacks demonstrated efficacy.
absolute_contraindications: Hypersensitivity to oxytocin or any excipients in the formulation
Contraindications to vaginal delivery (for obstetric IV use): active genital herpes, vasa previa, complete placenta previa, invasive cervical carcinoma, umbilical cord prolapse
Abnormal fetal positioning (transverse lie) with non-imminent delivery (obstetric use)
Significant cephalopelvic disproportion (obstetric use)
Hypertonic or hyperactive uterine patterns (obstetric use)
Fetal distress where delivery is not imminent (obstetric use)
relative_contraindications: Pre-existing hyponatremia or significant electrolyte disorders (elevated water-intoxication risk)
Impaired renal function (reduces oxytocin clearance; increases hyponatremia risk)
Impaired hepatic function (reduces oxytocinase-mediated degradation)
History of water intoxication or syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH)
Concurrent use of vasopressors or vasoconstrictive agents (risk of severe hypertension)
Concurrent use of QT-prolonging medications (risk of arrhythmia with IV bolus)
Active eating disorder behaviors involving excessive water intake (dysregulated fluid balance)
Cardiovascular disease with significant arrhythmia history (QTc concern with IV route)
Pregnancy or possible pregnancy (intranasal off-label use): exogenous oxytocin may trigger uterine contractions; pregnancy is an exclusion criterion in body-composition research trials
Theoretical concern for endogenous oxytocin-system downregulation with prolonged off-label use; clinical significance uncertain
common_side_effects: Nausea and vomiting (particularly with IV administration)
Nasal discomfort, irritation, or congestion (intranasal route: ~19% in one RCT vs. ~7% placebo)
Headache
Lightheadedness or drowsiness
Intensified uterine contractions (with obstetric dosing)
Abdominal cramping (obstetric use)
Injection site erythema (injectable route)
serious_adverse_events: Water intoxication with hyponatremia: potentially fatal; presents as confusion, seizures, coma, cerebral edema; reported with prolonged high-dose IV infusion and documented in a case report of off-label nasal spray overuse (decreased plasma sodium and osmolality, psychotic symptoms after ~4 weeks of multiple-daily-dose self-administration)
Severe hypertension: especially when oxytocin co-administered with vasopressors or vasoconstrictive agents, or within 3-4 hours of caudal anesthesia
Uterine rupture (obstetric overdose / hyperstimulation)
Cardiac arrhythmias including QTc prolongation (IV bolus)
Anaphylaxis and severe allergic reactions
Maternal subarachnoid hemorrhage (obstetric overdose)
Fetal/neonatal harm: bradycardia, arrhythmias, permanent CNS damage, fetal death (obstetric context with hyperstimulation or fetal distress)
Paradoxical hypotension with cyclopropane anesthesia
Afibrinogenemia (reported in obstetric use)

Intranasal Oxytocin Improves Lean Muscle Mass and Lowers LDL Cholesterol in Older Adults with Sarcopenic Obesity: A Pilot RCT

dose_references.notes: VERIFIED against cited human studies. 24 IU intranasally four times daily for 8 weeks was the dose used in a published double-blind RCT (n=61 adults with obesity, NEJM Evidence 2024) - which was NEGATIVE: no significant weight loss vs. placebo (0.20 kg vs 0.26 kg, P=0.934). The same dose was used in a separate proof-of-concept PILOT RCT (n=21 completers) that reported increased lean mass (+2.25 kg, P=0.01) and reduced LDL (-19.3 mg/dL, P=0.023); authors explicitly describe it as preliminary and call for larger trials. Because the only adequately powered RCT was negative and the favorable body-composition signal comes only from a small pilot, this row is graded HUMAN_OBS rather than HUMAN_RCT. This is a research dose from investigational trials, NOT an FDA-approved dose for these indications, and no established therapeutic dose exists for body composition. EDUCATIONAL/TRACKING REFERENCE ONLY - not a prescription.

citation.title: The Effects of Oxytocin on Appetite Regulation, Food Intake and Metabolism in Humans

dose_references.notes: VERIFIED against cited review. Multiple SMALL crossover trials (n=15-25) used 24 IU intranasal as the acute dose. Results show modest reduction in hedonic caloric intake (~100-150 kcal at a single test meal) but inconsistent effects on hunger ratings and metabolic parameters. Given the small samples and acute-only/crossover design, graded HUMAN_OBS rather than HUMAN_RCT. Not a therapeutic dose for any approved indication. citation.title: Oxytocin is an age-specific circulating hormone that is necessary for muscle maintenance and regeneration
Rejuvenation of brain, liver and muscle by simultaneous pharmacological modulation of two signaling determinants that change in opposite directions with age

dose_references.notes: Mouse studies used 1 ug/g body weight SC daily. This is a MOUSE dose; direct human equivalents are NOT established and the human range is intentionally null. The Conboy lab combination study (oxytocin + ALK5 inhibitor) used 1 ug/g/day SC for 7 days in 22-24 month old mice. No validated human dose translation exists for these anti-aging endpoints. EDUCATIONAL/MECHANISTIC REFERENCE ONLY - not a prescription.

synergies.rationale: Mechanistically complementary in animal models: oxytocin activates MAPK/ERK signaling in muscle satellite cells (a pathway that declines with age), while ALK5 inhibitors suppress TGF-beta/pSmad2,3 signaling (a pathway that becomes overactive with age). The Conboy lab reported that simultaneous pharmacological normalization of both pathways in aged mice produced broader tissue rejuvenation (muscle, brain, liver) than either agent alone, at a 10-fold lower ALK5 inhibitor dose. This is animal-only evidence; no human trials of this combination exist. Educational mechanistic framing only.
synergies.caution_notes: Studied exclusively in aged mice. ALK5 inhibitors are NOT approved for human use and remain investigational. TGF-beta pathway suppression carries theoretical risks including impaired immune surveillance and tumor promotion. Do not extrapolate animal results to human protocols. This is not a recommended stack.

synergies.rationale: Both oxytocin and GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce food intake through partially complementary central pathways: GLP-1R agonists act primarily via hindbrain GLP-1 receptors and hypothalamic circuits, while oxytocin acts via paraventricular nucleus projections to the nucleus tractus solitarius and reward circuits. Animal data suggest additive appetite suppression when both systems are activated. GLP-1R agonists are associated with lean-mass loss alongside fat loss; oxytocin's satellite-cell activation has been hypothesized to complement this, though the only adequately powered human oxytocin body-composition RCT was negative. No human combination trials have been published. Educational mechanistic framing only.
synergies.caution_notes: No human combination trial data exist. Each agent independently causes nausea and GI effects; combination could amplify GI adverse effects. GLP-1R agonists are FDA-approved; intranasal oxytocin for body composition is not and lacks demonstrated efficacy. This is theoretical framing only - not a recommendation to combine.

synergies.rationale: Both oxytocin and MOTS-c have been independently studied for age-related muscle and metabolic decline. Oxytocin activates MAPK/ERK in muscle satellite cells (animal data); MOTS-c activates AMPK, improves mitochondrial function, and enhances insulin sensitivity in skeletal muscle. These are distinct, non-overlapping intracellular pathways with hypothetically shared downstream outcomes (muscle homeostasis, metabolic efficiency). No published research has examined this combination in any species. Educational mechanistic framing only.
synergies.caution_notes: No combination studies exist in any species. Mechanistic rationale is speculative and based only on independent single-agent pathway data. Both compounds are research-only for these endpoints. Not a recommended stack.
